<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]`

**Q: Define a Cloud Service Provider. List any three major public cloud providers with their year of public launch.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Cloud Service Provider (CSP)** is a third-party company that delivers on-demand, scalable computing resources (compute, storage, networking, databases, software) over the public Internet using a pay-as-you-go consumption-based billing model.

The three major public CSPs are:

- **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** — launched publicly on **March 14, 2006** with the launch of Simple Storage Service (S3) and Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2).
- **Microsoft Azure** — officially launched on **February 1, 2010** as Windows Azure, rebranded to Microsoft Azure in 2014.
- **Google Cloud Platform (GCP)** — Google App Engine was released in **2008** as a preview; the full GCP platform (Compute Engine, Cloud Storage) was launched in **2011/2012**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Word Count Target:** KTU 3-mark answers should be **80–120 words**. Avoid bloating.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Compare the object storage services of AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud. Mention one unique feature of each.**

**Model Answer:**

All three providers offer 11-nines (99.999999999%) durable object storage with HTTP REST API access:

- **AWS Simple Storage Service (S3):** Pioneered object storage (2006) and offers the deepest storage-class ecosystem, including seven tiers (Standard, Intelligent-Tiering, Standard-IA, One Zone-IA, Glacier Instant Retrieval, Glacier Flexible Retrieval, Glacier Deep Archive).
- **Azure Blob Storage:** Offers unique **WORM (Write-Once-Read-Many)** immutable blob policies with legal hold, making it preferred for regulated industries (BFSI, healthcare).
- **Google Cloud Storage:** Offers **Uniform Bucket-Level Access**, which mandates IAM-only authorization and eliminates legacy ACL confusion — a developer-experience advantage.

Together, these three services (S3, Blob Storage, Cloud Storage) form the **canonical object-storage triad** studied in every cloud-computing curriculum.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. The examiner's valuation key requires *both* a definition AND an analytical comparison.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the architecture and global infrastructure of Amazon Web Services (AWS) in detail. Discuss Regions, Availability Zones, and Edge Locations with their roles in achieving high availability and fault tolerance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the compute service offerings of AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud Platform. Provide a justified recommendation for a startup building a Python-based machine-learning application with limited budget. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Architecture of AWS:**

1. **Global Infrastructure Layer:** AWS operates **32+ geographic regions** globally. Each region is a fully isolated geographic area (e.g., `ap-south-1` Mumbai, `us-east-1` Northern Virginia) containing multiple isolated data centers.
2. **Availability Zones (AZs):** Each region consists of **3 or more AZs** (totaling 100+ AZs globally). An AZ is one or more discrete data centers with redundant power, networking, and cooling, physically separated by a meaningful distance (many kilometers) from other AZs but connected via low-latency fiber (< 1 ms round-trip).
3. **Edge Locations / Points of Presence (PoPs):** AWS has **600+ edge locations** and **13+ regional edge caches** that sit outside regions. They are used primarily by **Amazon CloudFront (CDN)** to cache content closer to end-users, achieving sub-50ms latency globally.
4. **Local Zones and Wavelength:** Introduced for ultra-low-latency edge computing (5G, IoT, gaming).

**Role in High Availability and Fault Tolerance:**

- **Within an AZ:** Achieved through redundant hardware (power, network paths, storage).
- **Across AZs:** Achieved through **synchronous replication** (e.g., RDS Multi-AZ) or **asynchronous replication** (e.g., S3 Cross-Region Replication).
- **Across Regions:** Achieved via **cross-region replication** and **multi-region architectures** for disaster recovery (DR).

The 11-nines durability of S3 is mathematically derived from cross-AZ synchronous replication:

$$
\text{Durability} = 1 - (1 - p)^{n}
$$

Where $p$ is the probability of losing one object copy and $n$ is the number of replicas (typically $n \geq 3$ across AZs).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table (Compute Services):**

| Dimension | AWS EC2 | Azure VM | GCP Compute Engine |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------------------|
| Launch Year | 2006 | 2010 | 2012 |
| Instance Types | 500+ | 300+ | 250+ |
| Custom Silicon | Graviton (ARM) | Cobalt (ARM preview) | Tau T2A (ARM) |
| Live Migration | Limited | Yes | Yes (industry-first) |
| Per-Second Billing | Yes | Yes | Yes (pioneered) |
| Free Tier | 750 hrs/month for 12 months | 750 hrs/month for 12 months | $300 credit for 90 days |
| GPU Support | NVIDIA (A100, H100) | NVIDIA (H100) | NVIDIA + TPU |

**Recommendation for the Python ML Startup:**

The recommended provider is **Google Cloud Platform (GCP)** for the following reasons:

1. **Cost:** GCP's automatic sustained-use discounts reduce compute cost by up to **30%** without any upfront reservation commitment, ideal for a budget-constrained startup.
2. **AI/ML Specialization:** GCP's **Vertex AI** platform and **TPU (Tensor Processing Unit)** access are unmatched for Python-based ML workloads. TPU v4 pods deliver superior price/performance for transformer-based models.
3. **Free Egress Between GCP Regions:** Unlike AWS and Azure, GCP does not charge for data transfer between its own regions, saving significant cost when training models on data stored in different regions.
4. **Python + Jupyter Integration:** GCP's **Colab Enterprise** and **Vertex AI Workbench** provide native Jupyter notebook environments with pre-installed TensorFlow and PyTorch.

The recommended architecture is: **Compute Engine (VM) + Cloud Storage (data) + Vertex AI (model training) + Cloud Run (model serving)**, deployed in the `asia-south1` (Mumbai) region for low latency to Indian users.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the NIST cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) and provide one real-world example service from AWS, Azure, and GCP for each model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the strategic positioning of Microsoft Azure as an enterprise cloud platform. How does Azure differentiate itself from AWS and GCP in the hybrid-cloud and Windows-integration domains? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**NIST Cloud Service Models (SP 800-145):**

The **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology)** defines three service models that form the backbone of cloud-computing classification:

- **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS):** The provider supplies **virtualized hardware resources** — compute, storage, and network — and the consumer manages the OS, middleware, runtime, and applications. *Example:* AWS EC2, Azure VM, GCP Compute Engine.
- **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS):** The provider supplies a **managed runtime + OS + middleware** stack, and the consumer only deploys their application code and data. *Example:* AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service, GCP App Engine.
- **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS):** The consumer uses a **finished, hosted application** accessed via browser or API. *Example:* AWS Chime, Microsoft 365 (Azure-backed), Google Workspace (GCP-backed).

```
+--------------------------------------------+
| SaaS    | Finished Application             |  Consumer manages: nothing
+--------------------------------------------+
| PaaS    | Application + Data               |  Consumer manages: app & data
+--------------------------------------------+
| IaaS    | App + Data + Runtime + OS        |  Consumer manages: app & data & runtime & OS
+--------------------------------------------+
| On-Prem | App + Data + Runtime + OS + Virt |  Consumer manages: everything
+--------------------------------------------+
```

**Example Triad Table:**

| Model | AWS Example | Azure Example | GCP Example |
|-------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| IaaS | EC2 | Virtual Machine | Compute Engine |
| PaaS | Elastic Beanstalk | App Service | App Engine |
| SaaS | Amazon Chime | Microsoft 365 | Google Workspace |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Microsoft Azure's Strategic Positioning:**

Azure differentiates itself from AWS and GCP along three strategic axes:

**1. Enterprise/Hybrid-Cloud Leadership:**

Azure is the only provider with a **native hybrid-cloud story baked into its core product**, called **Azure Arc**. With Azure Arc, an enterprise can manage on-premises Windows/Linux servers, Kubernetes clusters, and even AWS/GCP resources *as if* they were native Azure resources — using a single control plane, single set of policies, and single identity system (Entra ID).

AWS offers **Outposts** (a hardware appliance) and **EKS Anywhere**, while GCP offers **Anthos**. However, Azure Arc's enterprise-grade integration with **Active Directory** (the world's most deployed identity system with 95%+ Fortune 1000 adoption) gives it a significant real-world advantage.

**2. Windows and Microsoft-Stack Integration:**

For organizations running **.NET, SQL Server, Windows Server, Active Directory, SharePoint, Dynamics 365, or Power Platform**, Azure provides zero-friction migration and integration. AWS supports these workloads but treats them as "third-party," while Azure treats them as first-class citizens. This is why **70%+ of Windows Server enterprise workloads** that migrate to public cloud end up on Azure.

**3. Compliance and Regulatory Footprint:**

Azure offers **100+ compliance certifications**, including region-specific certifications required by Indian regulators (RBI, SEBI, MeitY empanelment). For BFSI and government workloads in India, Azure's compliance posture is often the decisive factor.

**Conclusion:**

Azure is positioned as the **enterprise-default** cloud for organizations with Microsoft-stack heritage, regulated industries, and hybrid-cloud needs. AWS remains the **breadth leader**, while GCP remains the **data-and-AI specialist**. KTU students should remember this three-way positioning as the canonical Module-1 takeaway.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Service Categories:** Writing "EC2 is a PaaS" instead of IaaS. *Correct:* EC2 = **IaaS**, Elastic Beanstalk = **PaaS**, Amazon Chime = **SaaS**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Hybrid Story:** Discussing Azure without mentioning **Azure Arc + Entra ID** loses 2 marks on any "Azure differentiation" question.
> 3. **Skipping the Cost Comparison:** Always include at least one numerical comparison (₹/hour or $/month) when answering "compare CSPs" — examiners reward numerical rigor.
> 4. **One-Sided Answers:** Saying "AWS is the best" without justification. Every comparison must have a *balanced* conclusion with use-case-specific recommendations.
> 5. **Ignoring the Indian Context:** KTU examiners appreciate references to the **`ap-south-1` Mumbai region**, RBI compliance, and Indian use cases (e.g., Aadhaar, UPI on cloud).

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam**

### Definitions (Must Memorize)
- **CSP:** Third-party company delivering on-demand cloud services over the Internet.
- **Region:** A separate geographic area containing multiple data centers.
- **Availability Zone (AZ):** One or more isolated data centers within a region.
- **Edge Location:** A small data center used by CDN to cache content close to users.

### Three Big Numbers (Recurring in KTU Questions)
- AWS launched in **2006**, Azure in **2010**, GCP (full platform) in **2011/2012**.
- AWS holds **~31%**, Azure **~24%**, GCP **~11%** of the global IaaS market (2024).
- All three offer object storage with **11 nines (99.999999999%)** durability.

### Canonical Service Mapping (Write This in 60 Seconds in the Exam)
- **VM:** EC2 / Azure VM / Compute Engine
- **Object Storage:** S3 / Blob Storage / Cloud Storage
- **Serverless:** Lambda / Azure Functions / Cloud Functions
- **Managed K8s:** EKS / AKS / GKE
- **NoSQL:** DynamoDB / Cosmos DB / Firestore

### Three Positioning Lines (Memorize Verbatim)
- **AWS:** The **breadth leader** — broadest service catalog, enterprise-default, mature.
- **Azure:** The **enterprise/hybrid leader** — best for Windows, Microsoft 365, Active Directory, BFSI.
- **GCP:** The **data/AI leader** — best for Kubernetes, BigQuery, Vertex AI, TPUs, open-source.

### Strategic Differentiators (One Each — Use in 14-Mark Answers)
- **AWS:** Service breadth (240+ services) + largest customer base.
- **Azure:** Azure Arc + Entra ID + 200+ AZs (most AZs globally).
- **GCP:** TPUs + BigQuery + per-second billing pioneer + uniform bucket access.

### Common KTU Traps
- Confusing **IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS** boundaries.
- Saying "EC2 is serverless" — *No*, Lambda is.
- Missing the **hybrid-cloud** angle when discussing Azure.
- Failing to mention **Indian regions** (`ap-south-1` Mumbai for AWS, `Central India` for Azure, `asia-south1` for GCP).

### Final Mantra
> *"AWS for breadth, Azure for enterprise, GCP for AI. Regions contain AZs, AZs contain data centers. Object storage is 11 nines on all three."*

This mantra, internalized, will fetch **80%+ of the marks** in any Module 1 question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hands-On - Cloud Account Setup and Virtual Machine Deployment - Create Accounts on a Cloud Provider and Deploy Virtual Machine Instances, and Document the Process and Inferences
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST635 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hands-On - Cloud Account Setup and Virtual Machine Deployment - Create Accounts on a Cloud Provider and Deploy Virtual Machine Instances, and Document the Process and Inferences

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Cloud Account Setup and Virtual Machine Deployment** is a hands-on laboratory exercise that involves provisioning a tenant identity on a public cloud platform (such as AWS, Microsoft Azure, or Google Cloud Platform), authenticating it via Identity and Access Management (IAM), allocating compute resources through the provider's Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) console, and instantiating a **Virtual Machine (VM)** instance using a pre-configured OS image. The activity concludes with the generation of an empirical **lab inference report** that captures the architectural choices, billing implications, and performance parameters observed during deployment.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this exercise falls under **CO1: Understand the principles of cloud computing architectures and service models**, with a specific mapping to the practical skill of *translating abstract IaaS concepts into a deployable, measurable resource instance*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This hands-on module is assessed as a **Lab Record + Continuous Evaluation (CE)** component, NOT as a written University theory question. However, viva-voce questions on this lab are frequently asked in the **End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** under CO1 and CO2, typically carrying 3 to 5 marks inside a broader 14-mark question.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of the cloud as a **massive, fully-stocked, virtual shopping mall for computer hardware**:

| Real-World Analogy | Cloud Computing Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Walking into the mall and getting a membership card | Creating a cloud account (registration + verification) |
| The membership card with your photo and access rights | IAM User, Access Keys, MFA |
| Choosing a shop and renting a room/shelf for a month | Selecting a VM instance type (e.g., t2.micro, B1s) |
| The pre-built shop interior you walk into | OS Image (Amazon Machine Image / Azure Marketplace Image) |
| Your rented, furnished shop running your business | Running VM instance |
| The electricity and water meter bills | Cloud billing meter (per-second / per-hour billing) |
| Returning the keys when done | Terminating / Deallocating the instance |

**In Plain English:** A student who has never touched the cloud needs to understand that they are essentially *renting a remote computer* over the internet. The cloud provider (AWS/Azure/GCP) owns millions of physical servers in data centers worldwide. When you "create a VM," you are not buying a computer — you are telling the provider's hypervisor to carve out a slice of a physical server, load your chosen operating system onto that slice, and give you remote desktop/network access to it. You pay only for the time the VM is running.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definitions to Memorize for Viva:**
> - **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service):** The cloud service model where you rent raw compute, storage, and networking primitives. Examples: AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines, GCP Compute Engine.
> - **Hypervisor:** The low-level software (Type-1 or Type-2) that creates and runs VMs. Examples: Xen (AWS), Hyper-V (Azure), KVM (GCP/Linux).
> - **Instance:** A running VM that you have provisioned.
> - **Region & Availability Zone:** A Region is a geographical area (e.g., `us-east-1`, `ap-south-1`); an Availability Zone (AZ) is one or more discrete data centers within a Region.
> - **Free Tier:** A promotional allowance (typically 12 months for AWS, 30 days + always-free for Azure/GCP) that lets students deploy small VMs at zero cost.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

The following **standard cloud KPIs** are measured during this hands-on and are commonly asked in viva:

- **vCPU** — Virtual Central Processing Unit (a fraction or whole of a physical CPU core).
- **RAM (GiB)** — Memory allocated to the instance, in Gibibytes.
- **EBS / Persistent Disk (GB)** — Block storage attached to the instance.
- **Instance Hour** — The unit of billing; **1 instance hour = 1 VM running for 1 hour**.
- **Monthly Uptime SLA** — Typically **99.99%** for paid tiers, lower for free tier.
- **Public IP / Private IP** — Network addressing of the instance.
- **Inbound / Outbound Bandwidth** — Network throughput, often free for ingress (uploads to cloud) and metered for egress (downloads from cloud).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Hands-On Workflow

The end-to-end exercise can be decomposed into **six sequential operational phases**. Each phase has a clear "why" and "how" that students must document in their lab record.

#### Phase 1: Account Registration & Identity Verification
- **Why:** Cloud providers must comply with **KYC (Know Your Customer)** and global anti-fraud regulations. They also must bill someone.
- **How:** Provide a valid email, mobile number (OTP), and a credit/debit card (a **₹2 INR / $1 USD pre-authorization hold** is placed on AWS for verification; Azure gives ₹14,500 / $200 free credit for 30 days without card; GCP gives $300 for 90 days).

#### Phase 2: IAM (Identity and Access Management) Configuration
- **Why:** The root account has unrestricted power and must NEVER be used for daily tasks. Compromising root = total account compromise.
- **How:** Create an **IAM User** with `AdministratorAccess` policy attached, enable **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**, and download the **access key ID + secret access key** CSV.

#### Phase 3: Region & Service Selection
- **Why:** Latency, data residency laws (e.g., DPDP Act 2023 in India), and cost vary by region. Mumbai region (`ap-south-1` on AWS, `Central India` on Azure) is the closest to Kerala and gives the lowest latency.
- **How:** Choose the nearest region. Pin the region in the console to avoid accidentally provisioning in `us-east-1` (which is the most expensive reference).

#### Phase 4: VM Instance Provisioning (The Core Step)
- **Why:** This is the actual IaaS consumption. The hypervisor allocates vCPUs, RAM, and root storage.
- **How:** Select an **AMI (Amazon Machine Image)** or **Marketplace Image** (e.g., Ubuntu 22.04 LTS, Windows Server 2022), pick an **instance type** (`t2.micro` for AWS Free Tier, `B1s` for Azure Free Tier), configure **storage**, and choose a **key pair** for SSH authentication.

#### Phase 5: Network & Security Configuration
- **Why:** By default, the cloud is a closed fortress. You must explicitly open ports.
- **How:** Create or select a **Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)**, configure **Security Group / Network Security Group (NSG)** inbound rules (Port 22 for SSH, Port 3389 for RDP, Port 80 for HTTP), and assign a **Public IP / Elastic IP**.

#### Phase 6: Connection, Validation & Inferences
- **Why:** Documentation of inferences is what converts a "click-click" exercise into an engineering learning outcome.
- **How:** Connect via SSH (`ssh -i key.pem ubuntu@<public-ip>`) or RDP, run system commands (`uname -a`, `df -h`, `top`), record **observed metrics**, and document **inferences** (e.g., "Boot time was 47 seconds for Ubuntu 22.04 on t2.micro").

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit / Boundary Condition | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{vm}$ — Total VM cost | $C_{vm} = (N_{hrs} \times P_{hr}) + (S_{GB} \times P_{stg}) + B_{egress}$ | USD; $N_{hrs} \ge 0$ | Predicts monthly bill before provisioning |
| $P_{hr}$ — Hourly rate | Provider-defined (e.g., `$0.0116/hr` for t2.micro Linux) | USD / instance-hour | Used in cost estimation |
| $S_{GB}$ — Storage | Sum of all attached EBS / Disk volumes | GB | Determines storage cost |
| $B_{egress}$ — Egress bandwidth | $\sum (\text{GB out}) \times P_{gb}$ | USD / GB | Often the **hidden cost** of cloud bills |
| $T_{boot}$ — Boot time | $T_{end} - T_{start}$ measured from console "Launch" to "Status 2/2 checks passed" | Seconds | Empirical inference metric |
| $\text{SLA}_{\text{monthly}}$ — Uptime | $1 - \dfrac{D_{down}}{T_{month}}$ where $D_{down}$ is downtime in minutes | Percentage (e.g., 99.99%) | Service-level guarantee |
| $\text{vCPU}_{\text{alloc}}$ | $\lceil R_{cpu} / P_{cpu} \rceil$ where $R_{cpu}$ is requested cores and $P_{cpu}$ is physical cores per host | Integer cores | Hypervisor scheduling basis |
| $\text{Memory}_{\text{ratio}}$ | $M_{vm} / M_{host}$ — Overcommit ratio (typically 1.5:1 to 4:1 on cloud) | Dimensionless | Memory overcommit headroom |
| $N_{max\_vms\_per\_host}}$ | $\min \left( \lfloor P_{cpu} / v_{cpu} \rfloor, \lfloor M_{host} / M_{vm} \rfloor \right)$ | Integer | Capacity planning (background inference) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule Reminder:** The notation $\vert x \vert$ or $\lfloor x \rfloor$ has been rendered as `\vert x \vert` and `\lfloor x \rfloor` to preserve markdown table integrity. **Do not use raw `|` inside table cells.**

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This hands-on is the **gateway skill** for every modern cloud engineering role:
- **DevOps Engineers** use the same portal to spin up Jenkins build agents.
- **ML Engineers** use it to launch GPU instances (e.g., `p3.2xlarge`) for training models.
- **Site Reliability Engineers (SREs)** use it to replicate production incidents in a sandbox.
- **B.Tech Final-Year Projects** almost always begin with this exact exercise to host a web app on EC2/VM.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Cost Derivation — Worked Example

A student launches a `t2.micro` (AWS Free Tier eligible) Ubuntu VM in `ap-south-1` (Mumbai), keeps it running for **120 hours** in a month, attaches **20 GB** of gp3 EBS storage, and downloads **50 GB** of data from the VM to local laptop.

**Given:**
- $P_{hr} = \$0.0116$ per hour (Linux, t2.micro, on-demand, Mumbai region)
- $P_{stg} = \$0.08$ per GB-month (gp3, Mumbai)
- $P_{gb\_out} = \$0.09$ per GB (first 10 TB / month)
- $N_{hrs} = 120$ hours
- $S_{GB} = 20$ GB
- $B_{egress} = 50$ GB

**Step 1 — Compute instance cost $C_{instance}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{instance} &= N_{hrs} \times P_{hr} \\
&= 120 \times 0.0116 \\
&= \$1.392 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Multiply the number of hours the VM was on (120) by the per-hour rental rate of the VM (0.0116). This gives the cost purely for CPU + RAM rental.

**Step 2 — Compute storage cost $C_{storage}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{storage} &= S_{GB} \times P_{stg} \\
&= 20 \times 0.08 \\
&= \$1.60 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Multiply attached disk size in GB by the per-GB-month storage rate. Even if the VM is OFF, storage is still billed — a common viva trap.

**Step 3 — Compute egress cost $C_{egress}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{egress} &= B_{egress} \times P_{gb\_out} \\
&= 50 \times 0.09 \\
&= \$4.50 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Multiply total data downloaded from the cloud OUT to the internet (egress) by the per-GB rate. **Ingress (uploads to cloud) is free** on AWS — a critical asymmetry to document as an inference.

**Step 4 — Compute total monthly cost $C_{vm}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{vm} &= C_{instance} + C_{storage} + C_{egress} \\
&= 1.392 + 1.60 + 4.50 \\
&= \$7.492 \text{ USD} \\
&\approx ₹625 \text{ INR at 83.5 ₹/\$}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Sum all three components. This is the exact figure the student must record in the "Inferences" section of the lab record, along with the itemized breakdown.

### 3.2 Boot-Time Inference Derivation

A student times the VM launch using a stopwatch from the moment the "Launch Instance" button is clicked until "Status checks: 2/2 passed" appears.

- **Observed start time $T_{start}$:** 14:32:10 IST
- **Observed end time $T_{end}$:** 14:32:57 IST

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{boot} &= T_{end} - T_{start} \\
&= 14:32:57 - 14:32:10 \\
&= 47 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 47-second boot is typical for cold-launch of an Ubuntu 22.04 AMI on `t2.micro`. The boot pipeline consists of:
1. **Hypervisor allocation** (~10s) — Xen hypervisor reserves vCPU + RAM.
2. **Image copy to local disk** (~15s) — EBS snapshot is lazy-loaded.
3. **Kernel + systemd boot** (~22s) — Ubuntu userspace initialization.

This decomposition is a high-yield viva inference.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Provider Comparison for Lab Deployment

| Parameter | AWS EC2 | Microsoft Azure | Google Cloud Platform |
|---|---|---|---|
| Free Tier VM Type | `t2.micro` (1 vCPU, 1 GiB RAM) | `B1s` (1 vCPU, 1 GiB RAM) | `e2-micro` (shared core, 1 GiB RAM) |
| Free Tier Duration | 12 months from first signup | 12 months + $200 credit for 30 days | 90 days + $300 credit |
| Hypervisor | Xen (now Nitro) | Hyper-V | KVM |
| Default OS Image | Amazon Linux 2 / Ubuntu | Ubuntu / Windows Server | Debian / Ubuntu / Container-Optimized OS |
| Authentication | SSH key pair (.pem) | SSH key OR password | SSH key (.pem or .ppk) |
| Closest India Region | `ap-south-1` (Mumbai) | `Central India` (Pune) | `asia-south1` (Mumbai) |
| Default Block Storage | EBS gp3 | Premium SSD / Standard HDD | Persistent Disk (pd-standard / pd-ssd) |
| Outbound Egress Cost | $0.09 / GB | $0.08 / GB | $0.12 / GB |
| Best for KTU Lab | ✅ Widest free tier | ✅ Easiest RDP for Windows | ✅ Best student credit |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Cloud Account Setup & VM Deployment Flow

The diagram below maps the complete hands-on workflow from the student's browser to the cloud provider's physical data center. Every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["STUDENT LAPTOP BROWSER"] --> B1["CLOUD PROVIDER SIGNUP PAGE"]
    B1 --> C1["EMAIL AND MOBILE OTP VERIFICATION"]
    C1 --> D1{"CREDIT CARD AVAILABLE"}
    D1 -- YES --> E1["AWS FREE TIER 12 MONTHS"]
    D1 -- NO --> E2["AZURE OR GCP WITH STUDENT CREDIT"]
    E1 --> F1["ROOT ACCOUNT CREATED"]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1["ENABLE MFA ON ROOT"]
    G1 --> H1["CREATE IAM USER WITH ADMIN POLICY"]
    H1 --> I1["DOWNLOAD ACCESS KEYS CSV"]
    I1 --> J1["LOGIN AS IAM USER"]
    J1 --> K1["SELECT NEAREST REGION AP SOUTH 1"]
    K1 --> L1["CHOOSE SERVICE EC2 OR VIRTUAL MACHINE"]
    L1 --> M1["SELECT BASE OS IMAGE UBUNTU 22 04 LTS"]
    M1 --> N1["SELECT INSTANCE TYPE T2 MICRO"]
    N1 --> O1["GENERATE OR IMPORT SSH KEY PAIR"]
    O1 --> P1["CONFIGURE SECURITY GROUP INBOUND 22 80 3389"]
    P1 --> Q1["LAUNCH VM INSTANCE"]
    Q1 --> R1{"STATUS CHECK 2 OF 2 PASSED"}
    R1 -- NO --> S1["WAIT AND RETRY"]
    S1 --> R1
    R1 -- YES --> T1["SSH OR RDP INTO VM"]
    T1 --> U1["RUN SYSTEM COMMANDS UNAME TOP DF"]
    U1 --> V1["RECORD INFERENCES IN LAB RECORD"]
    V1 --> W1["DOCUMENT COST AND BOOT TIME"]
    W1 --> X1["SUBMIT LAB RECORD FOR EVALUATION"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Deployed VM

This block diagram illustrates the runtime stack inside the provisioned VM, showing how the physical host resources map to the guest OS the student interacts with.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PH1["PHYSICAL HOST SERVER XEON OR EPYC"] --> HV1["HYPERVISOR XEN OR KVM OR HYPERV"]
    HV1 --> VMB["VM INSTANCE T2 MICRO"]
    VMB --> VCPU["1 VCPU ALLOCATED"]
    VMB --> VRAM["1 GIB RAM ALLOCATED"]
    VMB --> VSTO["20 GB EBS PERSISTENT DISK"]
    VMB --> VNET["VIRTUAL NIC WITH PUBLIC IP"]
    VCPU --> GOS["GUEST OS UBUNTU 22 04 KERNEL"]
    VRAM --> GOS
    VSTO --> GOS
    VNET --> GOS
    GOS --> APP1["USER SESSIONS SSH AND WEB SERVER"]
    APP1 --> STUD["STUDENT ACCESS VIA INTERNET"]
```

### 4.3 IAM Role Hierarchy (Defense-in-Depth)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["ROOT ACCOUNT FULL BILLING AND ADMIN"] --> MFA["ENABLE MFA TOTP OR HARDWARE"]
    MFA --> IAM["IAM USER DAILY OPERATIONS"]
    IAM --> GRP1["ADMIN GROUP POLICY ADMINISTRATOR ACCESS"]
    IAM --> GRP2["DEV GROUP POLICY AMAZON EC2 FULL ACCESS"]
    IAM --> GRP3["READONLY GROUP POLICY VIEW ONLY"]
    GRP1 --> USER1["STUDENT ACCOUNT"]
    GRP2 --> USER1
    GRP3 --> AUDIT["AUDIT OR VIEWER ACCOUNT"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List any three steps involved in creating a cloud account on AWS and explain the role of an IAM user.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Account Registration:** Visit the AWS portal, provide a valid email ID, set a strong password, and verify via mobile OTP. A credit/debit card is mandatory for identity verification — a nominal pre-authorization hold is placed. (1 mark)

2. **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA):** Enable MFA on the root account using a virtual MFA app (Google Authenticator) or hardware token to add a second layer of security beyond the password. (1 mark)

3. **IAM User Creation:** Create an **IAM (Identity and Access Management) User** under the root account, attach the `AdministratorAccess` managed policy, and download the access key CSV.

   **Role of IAM User:** It provides **least-privilege, scoped credentials** so that day-to-day operations are not performed using the root account. If the IAM user is compromised, damage is limited; the root account retains billing and account-level control. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*What is a Virtual Machine instance in the context of cloud computing? Differentiate between an AMI and a Snapshot.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Virtual Machine (VM) instance** is a software-emulated computer system that runs on a physical host server through a hypervisor. The cloud provider allocates dedicated vCPU, RAM, storage, and network resources to the instance, and the user interacts with it over the internet via SSH/RDP. (1.5 marks)

**AMI vs. Snapshot:**

| Aspect | AMI (Amazon Machine Image) | Snapshot |
|---|---|---|
| What it contains | OS + application + launch configuration metadata | Point-in-time copy of a single EBS volume |
| Used for | Launching **new** instances | **Backup / restore** of an existing volume |
| Bootable? | Yes (includes root volume snapshot + launch perms) | Only if it is of a root volume |
| Granularity | Aggregate (template) | Single volume (raw) | (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe, step-by-step, the process of deploying a Linux Virtual Machine on AWS EC2. Cover AMI selection, instance type, key pair generation, security group configuration, and SSH connection. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A student provisions a `t2.micro` instance in `ap-south-1` and keeps it running for **240 hours** in a month. The instance has **30 GB of gp3 EBS storage** attached, and **80 GB of data** is transferred out of the cloud to the student's local network. Compute the total monthly bill using the rate card provided. *(7 marks)*

**Given Rate Card:**
- $P_{hr} = \$0.0116/hr$
- $P_{stg} = \$0.08/GB-month$
- $P_{gb\_out} = \$0.09/GB$

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Deployment of a Linux VM on AWS EC2:** *(7 marks — 1 mark per major step, with sub-bullets)*

1. **Step 1 — Region Selection:** Log in to the AWS Console as IAM user, navigate to the top-right region selector, and pin **Asia Pacific (Mumbai) `ap-south-1`** for lowest latency from Kerala. (1 mark)

2. **Step 2 — AMI Selection:** Open EC2 Dashboard → Click **Launch Instance** → In the AMI catalog, choose **Ubuntu Server 22.04 LTS (HVM), SSD Volume Type** — this is Free Tier eligible and has broad community support. (1 mark)

3. **Step 3 — Instance Type Selection:** Choose **`t2.micro`** (1 vCPU, 1 GiB RAM, EBS-only) — qualifies for the 750-hour/month AWS Free Tier allowance. (1 mark)

4. **Step 4 — Key Pair Configuration:** Select **Create a new key pair**, give it a name (e.g., `kerala-lab-key`), and download the `.pem` file. This file is the **only** way to SSH into the instance. On Linux/Mac, run `chmod 400 key.pem` to lock permissions. (1 mark)

5. **Step 5 — Network & Security Group:** Configure VPC (use default), enable **Auto-assign Public IP**, and define a Security Group with inbound rules:
   - **SSH (Port 22)** from `0.0.0.0/0` (or your IP for better security)
   - **HTTP (Port 80)** if hosting a web server
   - **RDP (Port 3389)** only if using Windows (1 mark)

6. **Step 6 — Storage Configuration:** Set root volume to **20–30 GB gp3** (Free Tier covers up to 30 GB). (1 mark)

7. **Step 7 — Launch & Connect:** Click **Launch Instance**, wait for **Status Checks: 2/2 passed** (≈45–60 seconds), copy the **Public IPv4 address**, and SSH from local terminal: `ssh -i kerala-lab-key.pem ubuntu@<public-ip>`. Verify with `uname -a` and `df -h`. (1 mark)

**(b) Bill Calculation:** *(7 marks — distributed as 2 + 2 + 2 + 1)*

**Step 1 — Instance Cost:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{instance} &= N_{hrs} \times P_{hr} \\
&= 240 \times 0.0116 \\
&= \$2.784 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Storage Cost:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{storage} &= S_{GB} \times P_{stg} \\
&= 30 \times 0.08 \\
&= \$2.40 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Egress Cost:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{egress} &= B_{egress} \times P_{gb\_out} \\
&= 80 \times 0.09 \\
&= \$7.20 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Monthly Bill:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{vm} &= 2.784 + 2.40 + 7.20 \\
&= \$12.384 \text{ USD} \\
&\approx ₹1{,}034 \text{ INR at 83.5 ₹/\$}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inference:** Egress ($7.20) is **58%** of the total bill despite the VM being small — this demonstrates the principle that **"data movement, not compute, is the dominant cloud cost."** *(Bonus 0.5 mark captured under inference documentation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned]**

**(a)** Explain the documentation process that must be followed in the lab record after deploying a cloud VM. What specific "inferences" are expected by the KTU 2024 Scheme evaluator? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud Platform across **any four parameters** relevant to a student's first cloud deployment. Justify which provider you would recommend for a B.Tech student at APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Lab Record Documentation Process & Expected Inferences:** *(7 marks)*

The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates a **structured lab record** that converts a manual exercise into a measurable engineering outcome. The expected sections are:

1. **Aim & Apparatus:** State the objective (deploy a VM on a public cloud) and list the tools used (browser, OS, SSH client like PuTTY/MobaXterm, a stopwatch for timing). (1 mark)

2. **Procedure:** Write a **numbered, screenshot-illustrated** step-by-step procedure. Each step should have: action performed + screen observed + observation. (1 mark)

3. **Configuration Snapshot:** Record the exact **AMI ID, instance type, region, vCPU count, RAM, storage size, public IP, and key pair name** of the deployed VM. (1 mark)

4. **Empirical Observations:** A table with:
   - Boot time in seconds
   - Free RAM after boot
   - Free disk after boot
   - Public IP / Private IP
   - Round-trip ping latency from Kerala to `ap-south-1` (typically 25–45 ms) (1 mark)

5. **Inferences (Most Critical Section — 3 marks):** The evaluator expects at least **5 documented inferences**, such as:
   - *"The VM booted in 47 seconds, slower than a local laptop because the image was lazy-loaded from EBS."*
   - *"Egress traffic is billed at $0.09/GB but ingress is free, confirming asymmetric bandwidth pricing."*
   - *"Disabling the VM (stopping) preserves storage cost but does NOT stop the billing clock; only termination does."*
   - *"The root account was never used after IAM creation, demonstrating least-privilege principle."*
   - *"SSH worked without a password, proving public-key cryptography's role over plaintext credentials."*
   - *"Latency from Kerala to Mumbai (~30 ms) is much lower than to N. Virginia (~220 ms)."*

**(b) Comparative Analysis & Recommendation:** *(7 marks — 1 mark per parameter + 3 marks justification*

| Parameter | AWS EC2 | Azure VM | GCP Compute Engine |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free Tier Duration** | 12 months (best) | 12 months + $200 / 30 days | 90 days + $300 credit (highest dollar value) |
| **Closest India Region** | ap-south-1 Mumbai | Central India Pune | asia-south1 Mumbai |
| **Egress Cost (per GB)** | $0.09 | $0.08 (cheapest) | $0.12 (most expensive) |
| **Learning Curve for B.Tech Students** | Steepest but most documentation | Medium; best for Windows labs | Easiest; cleanest console |
| **Hypervisor** | Xen / Nitro | Hyper-V | KVM (open-source aligned) |

**Justification for KTU B.Tech Students:** *(3 marks)*

I recommend **AWS EC2** for the following KTU-specific reasons:
- **Free Tier** lasts 12 full months — longest continuous free usage, ideal for semester-long projects. (1 mark)
- **Industry dominance** — most KTU campus placement drives (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, Cognizant) test on AWS. (1 mark)
- **Largest community** — Stack Overflow, official AWS docs, and YouTube tutorials are most abundant for AWS. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to deallocate resources** — KTU evaluators **strictly deduct marks** if the lab record shows a running instance left ON. Always include a final step: *"Terminated the instance and verified zero residual charges in the Billing Dashboard."*
> 2. **Vague "inferences"** — Writing *"I successfully deployed the VM"* earns ZERO marks. Inferences must be **quantitative** (with numbers) or **comparative** (with a clear contrast).
> 3. **Missing key pair backup** — If the `.pem` file is lost, the VM becomes inaccessible and must be terminated. Many students lose marks for not mentioning key pair storage.
> 4. **Confusing Stop vs Terminate** — "Stopping" the VM preserves storage (billed); "Terminating" deletes the root volume. This distinction is a **favourite viva question**.
> 5. **Using the root account for daily work** — A serious security violation; always show IAM-user login screenshots.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cloud Account = Identity**, **VM Instance = Compute Resource**, **Region = Geographic Location**, **Security Group = Virtual Firewall**.
- **Root account** must NEVER be used for daily work; create an **IAM User** with scoped policies immediately.
- **AWS Free Tier** = 750 hours/month of `t2.micro` for 12 months + 30 GB EBS + 1 GB egress. Beyond this = pay-as-you-go.
- **Key Pair** is the **only** credential to SSH into a Linux VM; **lose the .pem = lose the VM**.
- **Security Group** inbound rules must explicitly open Port 22 (SSH), Port 3389 (RDP), Port 80 (HTTP), Port 443 (HTTPS) — everything else is blocked by default.
- **Boot time** of a typical Ubuntu `t2.micro` = **45–60 seconds**; document it empirically.
- **Cost formula:** $C_{vm} = (N_{hrs} \times P_{hr}) + (S_{GB} \times P_{stg}) + B_{egress}$.
- **Egress is paid, Ingress is free** — the single most important pricing asymmetry to remember.
- **Stopping ≠ Terminating**: Stopping pauses compute billing; storage continues. Terminating deletes the root volume.
- **Inferences must be quantitative and comparative**, not generic success statements.
- **Always terminate the instance after the lab** and attach a screenshot of the empty Billing Dashboard as proof of zero charges.
- **Mumbai (`ap-south-1` / `Central India` / `asia-south1`)** is the lowest-latency region for KTU Kerala students.
- **Document everything**: AMI ID, instance type, region, public IP, key pair name, observed boot time, observed cost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud-Enabling Technology - Networks and Internet Architecture, Cloud Data Center Technology, Modern Virtualization, Multitenant Technology, Service Technology and Service APIs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST635 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cloud-Enabling Technology - Networks and Internet Architecture, Cloud Data Center Technology, Modern Virtualization, Multitenant Technology, Service Technology and Service APIs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cloud-Enabling Technology (CET) — Formal Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Cloud Computing syllabus**, **Cloud-Enabling Technology (CET)** refers to the foundational set of hardware, software, networking, and architectural paradigms that collectively make the on-demand, scalable, and elastic delivery of computing services (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) feasible. Without these underlying building blocks, the "cloud" as we know it cannot exist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Concept):** Cloud-Enabling Technology is the umbrella term covering *Networks, Data Centers, Virtualization, Multi-tenancy, and Service-Oriented APIs* — the five pillars that transform distributed hardware into a consumable utility.

---

## 1.2 The Five Pillars — At a Glance

| # | Pillar | One-Line Meaning | Primary Function |
|---|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Networks & Internet Architecture** | The "roads and highways" of the cloud | Move data between users and data centers |
| 2 | **Cloud Data Center Technology** | The "warehouses" of compute and storage | House thousands of physical servers |
| 3 | **Modern Virtualization** | The "magic trick" of splitting one into many | Abstract hardware into multiple logical instances |
| 4 | **Multitenant Technology** | The "shared apartment" model | Serve many customers from one software instance |
| 5 | **Service Technology & APIs** | The "menu and waiter" interface | Expose cloud capabilities as callable services |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies (Beginner-Friendly)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Networks as a Highway System**
> Think of the Internet as a multi-lane highway. **TCP/IP** are the *traffic rules*, **routers** are the *interchanges*, and **bandwidth** is the *number of lanes*. The more lanes you have, the more data (cars) can flow at once. In cloud computing, this highway connects your laptop to a data center located 1000 km away.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Data Center as a Massive Warehouse**
> A Cloud Data Center is like a giant Amazon warehouse. Inside, thousands of robotic "racks" store and process your data. **Power, cooling, and fiber-optic cabling** are the electricity, AC, and conveyor belts that keep the warehouse running 24/7.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — Virtualization as a Slice of Cake**
> Imagine one large cake (the physical server). **Virtualization** is the act of cutting it into many slices. Each slice (Virtual Machine) believes it has the entire cake to itself, but they all share the same cake underneath. A **hypervisor** is the knife that performs the cutting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 4 — Multitenancy as a Co-Working Space**
> A co-working space (like WeWork) hosts many companies under one roof. Each company has its own locked cabin (tenant isolation), but they share the Wi-Fi, printers, and reception (shared infrastructure). **Multitenancy** in cloud computing does the same — one application instance serves many customers, each with private data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 5 — APIs as a Restaurant Menu**
> An **API (Application Programming Interface)** is like a restaurant menu. It tells you what you can order (the services available) and how to ask for it (the request format). The **kitchen** is the cloud backend that prepares your order, and the **waiter** is the API that delivers it back to you.

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Bandwidth:** Measured in **Gbps** (Gigabits per second). Standard cloud uplinks: **1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, 40 Gbps, 100 Gbps**.
- **Latency:** Measured in **milliseconds (ms)**. Cloud SLAs typically guarantee **< 100 ms** round-trip.
- **Server Density:** Modern hyperscale data centers achieve **> 50,000 servers per facility**.
- **PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness):** Industry benchmark is **PUE ≈ 1.1 – 1.2** (Google achieves ~1.10).
- **VM Density:** A single physical host can host **20–50 VMs** depending on workload.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Networks and Internet Architecture

### 2.1.1 The OSI 7-Layer Model (The Foundation)

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model is a conceptual framework describing how data moves across a network. Every cloud transaction uses these layers.

| Layer # | Layer Name | Function | Cloud Example |
|---------|-----------|----------|---------------|
| 7 | **Application** | User-facing interface | HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, DNS |
| 6 | **Presentation** | Encryption, encoding | TLS/SSL, JSON, XML |
| 5 | **Session** | Maintains connections | RPC, NetBIOS |
| 4 | **Transport** | Reliable delivery, ports | **TCP**, **UDP** |
| 3 | **Network** | Logical addressing, routing | **IP**, ICMP |
| 2 | **Data Link** | MAC addressing, framing | Ethernet, Wi-Fi |
| 1 | **Physical** | Bits over cables/wireless | Fiber, Copper, Radio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Point:** The **TCP/IP 4-layer model** (Application, Transport, Internet, Link) is the actual Internet protocol suite. Always remember: **TCP = reliable & ordered; UDP = fast & unreliable**.

### 2.1.2 Why the Internet is the Backbone of Cloud

- **Packet Switching:** Data is broken into **packets** (typically **1500 bytes** — the MTU), routed independently.
- **Best-Effort Delivery:** The Internet does not guarantee delivery — that's why **TCP** exists on top of IP.
- **IP Addressing:** **IPv4** uses 32-bit addresses (~4.3 billion); **IPv6** uses 128-bit (~340 undecillion).

### 2.1.3 The Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

The **Bandwidth-Delay Product** determines the volume of data that can be "in flight" on a network at any given time.

$$BDP = Bandwidth \times RTT$$

**Where:**
- $Bandwidth$ = Link capacity in bits per second
- $RTT$ = Round-Trip Time in seconds

**Example:** A 1 Gbps link with 50 ms RTT:
$$BDP = 10^9 \times 0.05 = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ bits} = 6.25 \text{ MB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Engineers use BDP to size the **TCP receive window**, ensuring maximum throughput on long-fat networks (LFNs) like transcontinental cloud links.

---

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Cloud Data Center Technology

### 2.2.1 Modern Data Center Architecture (3-Tier)

A cloud data center is organized in three physical tiers:

1. **Core Layer:** High-speed backbone (e.g., 100 Gbps). Connects to the Internet.
2. **Aggregation Layer:** Routes traffic between core and access. Implements firewalls, load balancers.
3. **Access (ToR) Layer:** **Top-of-Rack (ToR)** switches connect directly to servers.

### 2.2.2 Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) — The Critical Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PUE is the #1 metric KTU asks about for data centers.**

$$PUE = \frac{Total\ Facility\ Power}{IT\ Equipment\ Power}$$

**Boundary conditions:**
- **Ideal PUE = 1.0** (impossible — 100% efficient)
- **Industry Average ≈ 1.58**
- **Hyperscale (Google, AWS) ≈ 1.10 – 1.20**

**Inverse — Data Center Infrastructure Efficiency (DCiE):**
$$DCiE = \frac{1}{PUE} \times 100\%$$

### 2.2.3 Storage Technologies in Data Centers

| Type | Technology | Latency | Use Case |
|------|-----------|---------|----------|
| **Hot** | NVMe SSD | **< 0.1 ms** | Databases, caching |
| **Warm** | SATA SSD | ~1 ms | Boot volumes |
| **Cold** | HDD / Tape | **> 10 ms** | Backups, archives |

---

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Modern Virtualization

### 2.3.1 The Two Hypervisor Types

| Type | Also Known As | Examples | Cloud Usage |
|------|--------------|----------|-------------|
| **Type 1 (Bare-Metal)** | Native | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen | **AWS, Azure** production |
| **Type 2 (Hosted)** | Embedded | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation | Dev/testing only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Type-1 hypervisors run **directly on hardware** (no host OS). Type-2 runs **on top of a host OS**. Cloud providers ALWAYS use Type-1.

### 2.3.2 Full Virtualization vs. Para-Virtualization vs. Container Virtualization

- **Full Virtualization:** Guest OS is unaware it's virtualized. Requires **binary translation** or **hardware assist (Intel VT-x, AMD-V)**.
- **Para-Virtualization:** Guest OS is *modified* to know it's virtual. Uses **hypercalls**. Faster but requires OS changes.
- **OS-Level Virtualization (Containers):** Shares the host kernel. Examples: **Docker, Kubernetes**. Lightest weight.

### 2.3.3 VM Density Formula

$$VM_{density} = \left\lfloor \frac{RAM_{host}}{RAM_{VM} + VM_{overhead}} \right\rfloor$$

**Example:** A host with 256 GB RAM, each VM needs 8 GB + 2 GB overhead:
$$VM_{density} = \left\lfloor \frac{256}{8 + 2} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 25.6 \right\rfloor = 25\ VMs$$

---

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Multitenant Technology

### 2.4.1 The Three Tenancy Models

| Model | Instance per Tenant | Database per Tenant | Customization | Cost |
|-------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------|------|
| **Single-Tenant** | Yes (dedicated) | Yes (dedicated) | **High** | **High** |
| **Multi-Instance** | Yes (dedicated) | Shared schema possible | Medium | Medium |
| **Multi-Tenant** | **No (shared)** | **Shared** | Low | **Low** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-tenancy = ONE application instance, MANY customers.** This is the bedrock of SaaS economics (e.g., Salesforce, Gmail).

### 2.4.2 Isolation Levels in Multi-Tenancy

- **Data Isolation:** Tenant data separated via row-level security (e.g., `WHERE tenant_id = ?`).
- **Performance Isolation:** Resource quotas (CPU, RAM, IOPS) per tenant.
- **Security Isolation:** Separate encryption keys, VPCs, or even physical hosts for premium tenants.

---

## 2.5 Pillar 5 — Service Technology & APIs

### 2.5.1 The Three Service Communication Styles

| Style | Protocol | Format | Speed | Use |
|-------|----------|--------|-------|-----|
| **SOAP** | HTTP/SMTP | **XML** | Slow | Enterprise (legacy) |
| **REST** | HTTP | **JSON / XML** | Fast | **Modern web/mobile** |
| **gRPC** | HTTP/2 | **Protobuf (binary)** | **Fastest** | Microservices |

### 2.5.2 REST API Constraints (Roy Fielding, 2000)

> [!NOTE]
> REST is **Representational State Transfer** — an architectural style with **6 constraints**:
> 1. Client-Server separation
> 2. **Stateless** (no session on server)
> 3. Cacheable responses
> 4. Uniform Interface
> 5. Layered System
> 6. (Optional) Code-on-Demand

### 2.5.3 HTTP Methods Used in Cloud APIs

| Method | CRUD Action | Idempotent? |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **GET** | Read | ✅ Yes |
| **POST** | Create | ❌ No |
| **PUT** | Update/Replace | ✅ Yes |
| **PATCH** | Partial Update | ❌ No |
| **DELETE** | Delete | ✅ Yes |

### 2.5.4 API Performance Formulas

**Throughput:**
$$Throughput\ (RPS) = \frac{Requests\ Completed}{Time\ (seconds)}$$

**Average Latency (Little's Law adapted):**
$$Avg\ Latency = \frac{Concurrent\ Users}{Throughput}$$

**Example:** 100 concurrent users, system handles 50 RPS:
$$Avg\ Latency = \frac{100}{50} = 2\ seconds$$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Key Insight |
|---------|---------|-------|-------------|
| Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = BW \times RTT$ | bits | Sizing TCP window |
| Power Usage Effectiveness | $PUE = P_{total} / P_{IT}$ | unitless | Lower is better |
| DCiE | $DCiE = (1 / PUE) \times 100$ | % | Inverse of PUE |
| VM Density | $\lfloor RAM_{host} / (RAM_{VM} + overhead) \rfloor$ | count | Integer floor |
| API Throughput | $RPS = N_{req} / \Delta t$ | req/s | KPI for SLA |
| API Latency | $L = U / RPS$ | seconds | Little's Law |
| Storage Capacity | $Capacity = Disks \times Size \times (1 - RAID\ overhead)$ | TB | Account for parity |
| CPU Utilization | $U = (Busy\ Time / Total\ Time) \times 100$ | % | Target: 60-80% |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proving PUE for a Hyperscale Data Center

**Problem Statement:** A cloud data center consumes a total facility power of **12 MW**. The IT equipment (servers, storage, network) consumes **10 MW**. Calculate the PUE and DCiE. Comment on efficiency.

### Step 1 — Identify the Formula
By definition, PUE is the ratio of total facility power to IT equipment power:
$$PUE = \frac{P_{total\ facility}}{P_{IT\ equipment}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Values
$$PUE = \frac{12\ MW}{10\ MW}$$

### Step 3 — Compute
$$PUE = 1.20$$

### Step 4 — Compute DCiE
$$DCiE = \frac{1}{PUE} \times 100\%$$

$$DCiE = \frac{1}{1.20} \times 100\%$$

$$DCiE = 83.33\%$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A PUE of **1.20** means for every **100 W** powering the IT equipment, an extra **20 W** is used for cooling, lighting, and power distribution losses. This is **excellent** — comparable to Google’s hyperscale efficiency. The global average is ~1.58, meaning this data center is **~32% more efficient** than average.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — VM Packing & Sizing the Hypervisor

**Problem:** A cloud provider wants to host the maximum number of web server VMs on a single physical host.

**Given:**
- Host has **128 GB RAM** and **32 vCPUs**
- Each VM requires **4 GB RAM + 1 vCPU + 1 GB hypervisor overhead**
- VMs must fit within **BOTH** RAM and CPU constraints

### Step 1 — RAM Constraint
$$N_{RAM} = \left\lfloor \frac{RAM_{host}}{RAM_{VM} + overhead} \right\rfloor$$

$$N_{RAM} = \left\lfloor \frac{128}{4 + 1} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 25.6 \right\rfloor = 25\ VMs$$

### Step 2 — vCPU Constraint
$$N_{vCPU} = \left\lfloor \frac{vCPUs_{host}}{vCPUs_{VM}} \right\rfloor$$

$$N_{vCPU} = \left\lfloor \frac{32}{1} \right\rfloor = 32\ VMs$$

### Step 3 — Bottleneck Determination
The **binding constraint** is the smaller value:
$$N_{final} = \min(N_{RAM}, N_{vCPU}) = \min(25, 32) = 25\ VMs$$

### Step 4 — Headroom Calculation
$$RAM_{used} = 25 \times 5 = 125\ GB \Rightarrow Headroom = 128 - 125 = 3\ GB$$
$$vCPU_{used} = 25 \times 1 = 25 \Rightarrow Headroom = 32 - 25 = 7\ vCPUs$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** RAM is the **bottleneck**. To increase density, the provider should either (a) upgrade to 256 GB hosts, or (b) use **memory overcommit** techniques where the hypervisor swaps to SSD (VMware's "memory ballooning"). In real clouds like AWS, **CPU overcommit ratios of 4:1** are common, but RAM is rarely overcommitted beyond 1.2:1.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bandwidth-Delay Product for Cloud WAN Optimization

**Problem:** An AWS S3 bucket in us-east-1 (Virginia) is being accessed from a server in Mumbai, India. The measured RTT is **220 ms**. The link bandwidth is **10 Gbps**. Calculate the BDP and recommend the minimum TCP window size.

### Step 1 — Convert Units
RTT in seconds:
$$RTT = 220\ ms = 0.220\ s$$

### Step 2 — Apply the BDP Formula
$$BDP = Bandwidth \times RTT$$
$$BDP = 10 \times 10^9 \times 0.220$$
$$BDP = 2.2 \times 10^9\ bits$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Bytes
$$BDP_{bytes} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^9}{8} = 2.75 \times 10^8\ bytes \approx 262.5\ MB$$

### Step 4 — Window Size Recommendation
The TCP receive window must be **at least equal to the BDP** to saturate the link:
$$Window_{min} \geq 2.75 \times 10^8\ bytes \approx 262.5\ MB$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The default Linux TCP window is ~64 KB. With BDP = 262.5 MB, the link is severely under-utilized! Cloud engineers enable **TCP Window Scaling** (RFC 7323) to scale windows up to 1 GB. Without it, the **10 Gbps link behaves like a 64 KB / 0.22 s ≈ 2.4 Mbps link** — a 4000× degradation. This is why **TCP BBR** and **CUBIC** congestion control algorithms matter in cloud WANs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Service Communication Styles

| Criterion | **SOAP** | **REST** | **gRPC** |
|-----------|---------|---------|----------|
| **Transport** | HTTP/SMTP | HTTP | HTTP/2 |
| **Payload Format** | XML (verbose) | JSON (lightweight) | Protobuf (binary) |
| **Contract** | WSDL (strict) | OpenAPI/Swagger (optional) | .proto file (strict) |
| **Speed** | Slow (parsing XML) | Medium | **Fastest** (binary) |
| **Browser Support** | Yes | **Yes (native)** | Limited (needs gRPC-Web) |
| **State** | Stateful allowed | Stateless only | Stateless |
| **Best For** | Banking, ERP | **Public APIs, mobile** | Microservices, internal |
| **Cloud Example** | SAP NetWeaver | AWS S3 API, Stripe | Google internal, Kubernetes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 expects students to know:** AWS API Gateway, Azure API Management, and Google Apigee are cloud-managed API gateways that expose REST/gRPC services with throttling, auth, and monitoring.

---

## 3.5 Multi-Tenancy Database Schema Strategies

| Strategy | Description | Pros | Cons |
|----------|-------------|------|------|
| **Separate Database** | One DB per tenant | Max isolation, easy backup | **Expensive** at scale |
| **Separate Schema** | One schema per tenant, shared DB | Good isolation, moderate cost | Migration complexity |
| **Shared Schema** | Single schema with `tenant_id` column | **Cheapest**, easy scaling | Noisy-neighbor risk |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard:** Salesforce uses the **shared schema** model with massive sharding — the reason it can serve 150,000+ companies from one application instance.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Cloud-Enabling Technology and list its five major components. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

Cloud-Enabling Technology (CET) refers to the foundational set of technologies that make cloud computing possible by abstracting, pooling, and exposing physical resources as on-demand services.

**The five major components are:**
1. **Networks and Internet Architecture** — TCP/IP, routing, packet switching
2. **Cloud Data Center Technology** — servers, storage, power, cooling
3. **Modern Virtualization** — hypervisors, VMs, containers
4. **Multitenant Technology** — shared application instance serving many customers
5. **Service Technology and Service APIs** — SOAP, REST, gRPC interfaces

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for the 5 components — list all)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Type-1 and Type-2 Hypervisors. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **Type-1 (Bare-Metal)** | **Type-2 (Hosted)** |
|---------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Installation | Directly on hardware | On top of host OS |
| Performance | **High** (no host OS overhead) | Lower (double OS layer) |
| Examples | VMware ESXi, Xen, Hyper-V | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation |
| Cloud Usage | **Production clouds (AWS, Azure)** | Dev / testing only |
| Security | More secure | Less secure |

*(3 marks: table format with 4 distinct points)*

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**a)** Explain the architecture of a modern cloud data center in detail. Discuss the three-tier network design, power management, and PUE metric with an example. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Describe modern virtualization technology. Compare full virtualization, para-virtualization, and OS-level virtualization. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**1. Modern Cloud Data Center Architecture (3 marks)**

A modern cloud data center is a highly-engineered facility housing thousands of servers, storage systems, and networking equipment. It is built around three design principles:
- **Scalability:** Modular racks added as demand grows
- **Reliability:** Redundant power (N+1 or 2N), cooling, and network paths
- **Efficiency:** Optimized PUE via hot/cold aisle containment

**2. Three-Tier Network Design (2 marks)**

As shown in Section 4.2, the network has:
- **Core Layer:** High-speed 100 Gbps backbone connecting to the Internet
- **Aggregation Layer:** Routes traffic, runs firewalls and load balancers
- **Access (ToR) Layer:** Top-of-Rack 10/25 Gbps switches connecting to servers

**3. Power Management & PUE (2 marks)**

Power Usage Effectiveness:
$$PUE = \frac{P_{total\ facility}}{P_{IT\ equipment}}$$

**Example:** Total power = 12 MW, IT power = 10 MW → PUE = 1.20, DCiE = 83.33%. This is excellent efficiency, achievable only by hyperscale providers (Google, Facebook) using free-air cooling and AI-driven load balancing.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**1. Definition of Modern Virtualization (1 mark)**
Virtualization is the abstraction of physical hardware into multiple logical instances, each running its own OS and applications.

**2. Full Virtualization (2 marks)**
- Guest OS is **unmodified** and unaware of being virtualized
- Relies on **binary translation** or **hardware assist (Intel VT-x, AMD-V)**
- Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V
- Pros: No OS modification needed. Cons: Translation overhead.

**3. Para-Virtualization (2 marks)**
- Guest OS is **modified** to include hypercalls
- Hypercalls are explicit requests to the hypervisor (faster than trapping)
- Examples: Xen, early AWS EC2
- Pros: High performance. Cons: Requires OS source access (not possible for Windows).

**4. OS-Level Virtualization / Containers (2 marks)**
- Shares the **host kernel**; no separate guest OS
- Each container has its own userspace (filesystem, network, process tree)
- Examples: **Docker, Kubernetes, LXC**
- Pros: **Lightest weight**, near-native startup (~milliseconds), high density
- Cons: Less isolation than VMs (shared kernel)

**Comparison Table Summary** (within the answer):
- Full Virtualization → Mod overhead, max isolation
- Para-Virtualization → Modified OS, faster
- Containers → Shared kernel, fastest, least isolation

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**a)** What is multi-tenancy? Explain the three tenancy models with suitable diagrams and discuss the challenges of multi-tenant databases. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain Service-Oriented Architecture and compare SOAP, REST, and gRPC APIs. Discuss HTTP methods used in REST. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**1. Definition (1 mark)**
Multi-tenancy is a software architecture pattern where a **single application instance** serves **multiple customers (tenants)**, with each tenant's data isolated and invisible to others.

**2. Three Tenancy Models (4 marks)**

**(i) Single-Tenant:** Each customer has a **dedicated application instance and database**. Maximum customization, highest cost. Example: traditional on-premise ERP.

**(ii) Multi-Instance:** Multiple application instances on **shared hardware**, each with its own database. Balance of isolation and cost. Example: mid-tier SaaS like HubSpot early architecture.

**(iii) Multi-Tenant:** **One application instance, one shared database** with logical separation via `tenant_id`. Lowest cost, highest scale. Example: **Salesforce, Gmail, Office 365**.

**3. Challenges of Multi-Tenant Databases (2 marks)**
- **Data Isolation:** Must enforce `WHERE tenant_id = ?` in every query
- **Noisy Neighbors:** One tenant's heavy load can slow others
- **Schema Evolution:** Upgrading the schema affects ALL tenants simultaneously
- **Backup/Restore:** Cannot backup one tenant without affecting others
- **Compliance:** GDPR/HIPAA require per-tenant data residency

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**1. Service-Oriented Architecture (1 mark)**
SOA is a design pattern where business functionality is exposed as **loosely-coupled, interoperable services** communicating over a network via standardized APIs.

**2. SOAP (2 marks)**
- **Simple Object Access Protocol**
- Uses **XML** messages over HTTP/SMTP
- Defines operations in **WSDL** contract
- Heavyweight but highly standardized (WS-Security, WS-ReliableMessaging)
- Used in enterprise systems (SAP, banking)

**3. REST (2 marks)**
- **Representational State Transfer** by Roy Fielding (2000)
- Uses **JSON or XML** over HTTP
- Stateless, cacheable, uniform interface
- Most popular for **public cloud APIs** (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud)
- Resource-based URLs (e.g., `GET /api/v1/orders/123`)

**4. gRPC (1 mark)**
- Modern, **HTTP/2-based** framework by Google
- Uses **Protocol Buffers** (binary, 3-10× smaller than JSON)
- Supports streaming, bidirectional calls
- Best for **internal microservice communication**

**5. HTTP Methods Table (1 mark)**

| Method | Action | Idempotent |
|--------|--------|-----------|
| GET | Read | Yes |
| POST | Create | No |
| PUT | Replace | Yes |
| DELETE | Delete | Yes |
| PATCH | Partial Update | No |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in This Topic:**
> 
> 1. **PUE Formula Reversal:** Students often write $PUE = P_{IT} / P_{total}$ — this is WRONG. Always: $PUE = P_{total} / P_{IT}$. Losing 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Type-1 vs Type-2 Confusion:** Many students say "Type-1 needs host OS" — false. Type-1 is BARE-METAL (no host OS). Losing 1-2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Multi-Tenancy vs Multi-Instance:** Examiner penalizes if you say "multi-tenancy means multiple instances" — the *defining feature* is ONE shared instance. Losing 2 marks.
> 
> 4. **REST = HTTP ≠ SOAP:** Students often confuse REST with SOAP. Emphasize: REST is an **architectural style**, SOAP is a **protocol**.
> 
> 5. **Skip Diagrams in Part B:** KTU examiners **expect diagrams** in 14-mark answers. A data center architecture question without a block diagram typically loses 2-3 marks.
> 
> 6. **No Units in Numerical Answers:** Writing PUE = 1.2 instead of PUE = 1.20 (unitless) — always state "PUE is dimensionless" once.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cloud-Enabling Technology (Module 2):**
> 
> ✅ **Networks:** TCP/IP 4-layer (App, Transport, Internet, Link) — TCP is reliable, UDP is fast. IPv4 = 32-bit, IPv6 = 128-bit.
> 
> ✅ **BDP Formula:** $BDP = BW \times RTT$ — used to size TCP window for cloud WANs.
> 
> ✅ **Data Center:** 3-tier (Core → Aggregation → Access/ToR). Industry metric = PUE. Lower PUE = better. Google ≈ 1.10.
> 
> ✅ **PUE Formula:** $PUE = P_{total} / P_{IT}$. Ideal = 1.0, Average = 1.58, Hyperscale = 1.10-1.20.
> 
> ✅ **Virtualization:** Type-1 (bare-metal, production) vs Type-2 (hosted, dev). Full = no OS mod, Para = OS mod, Container = shared kernel.
> 
> ✅ **VM Density:** Bottleneck = min(RAM, vCPU). Memory overcommit rarely exceeds 1.2:1.
> 
> ✅ **Multi-Tenancy:** One instance, many tenants. Use `tenant_id` in shared schema. Salesforce/Gmail model.
> 
> ✅ **Tenancy Models:** Single (dedicated, costly) → Multi-Instance (balanced) → Multi-Tenant (shared, cheap).
> 
> ✅ **APIs:** SOAP (XML, enterprise) vs REST (JSON, public cloud) vs gRPC (Protobuf, internal microservices).
> 
> ✅ **REST Constraints:** Stateless, cacheable, uniform interface, layered system. 6 constraints by Roy Fielding.
> 
> ✅ **HTTP Methods:** GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE — idempotency matters for retries.
> 
> ✅ **Storage Hierarchy:** Hot (NVMe < 0.1ms) → Warm (SATA SSD ~1ms) → Cold (HDD > 10ms).
> 
> ✅ **Cloud Examples:** AWS EC2 = Xen/KVM, AWS S3 = REST API, Salesforce = Multi-Tenant SaaS, Kubernetes = Container orchestration.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Understanding Containerization - Influencers, Fundamental Virtualization and Containerization, Understanding Containers, Understanding Container Images, Multi-Container Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST635 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Understanding Containerization - Influencers, Fundamental Virtualization and Containerization, Understanding Containers, Understanding Container Images, Multi-Container Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Containerization? (Formal KTU Definition)

**Containerization** is a lightweight form of **operating-system (OS) level virtualization** that packages an application along with its entire runtime dependencies — libraries, binaries, configuration files, and system tools — into a single, portable, executable unit called a **Container**. All containers on a host share the underlying host OS kernel, but each operates inside an isolated user-space instance using **Linux kernel features such as Namespaces (for isolation) and Control Groups / cgroups (for resource limiting).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A container is a runtime instance of a container image. It encapsulates application code, runtime, system libraries, settings, and dependencies into a single deployable artifact, guaranteeing consistent execution across any infrastructure (laptop, data center, or public cloud) without the heavy footprint of a traditional Virtual Machine.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shipping Container" Model

Imagine the global shipping industry **before** standardized shipping containers (pre-1956):
- Every ship, truck, and train handled goods (coal, cars, grain) differently.
- Loading/unloading took days because each item was a unique shape.
- Workers physically handled every box, barrel, and crate.

**Malcom McLean's solution:** Standardize a single **steel box (the 20/40 ft container)**.
- Contents are irrelevant to the carrier.
- A container can be moved from a ship → truck → train **without opening or repacking**.
- Universal handling machinery (cranes, twist-locks) works on *any* container.

**In Cloud Computing:** The application is the "cargo." The **container** is the standardized box. The **Docker engine / Container Runtime (runc, containerd)** is the crane. The **host OS kernel** is the ship. This standardization gave us **"Build once, run anywhere"** portability.

### 1.3 The Key Influencers of Modern Containerization

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board frequently asks:** *"List the major influencers/technologies that propelled containerization."* Memorize the four pillars below.

| # | Influencer | Contribution | Year |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Docker (dotCloud → Docker Inc.)** | Made containers user-friendly; introduced Dockerfile, Docker Hub | **2013** |
| 2 | **Google (Borg & Omega → Kubernetes)** | Internal cluster manager that became the de-facto orchestrator | 2003-2014, K8s 2015 |
| 3 | **Linux Kernel (Namespaces + cgroups)** | Provided the technical primitives for isolation & resource control | 2002–2008 |
| 4 | **CNCF (Cloud Native Computing Foundation)** | Vendor-neutral governance under the Linux Foundation | **2015** |
| 5 | **OCI (Open Container Initiative)** | Standardized image format & runtime specification | **2015** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solomon Hykes** (founder of Docker) famously stated at PyCon 2013: *"Containers are like lightweight VMs that start in milliseconds and pack 5–10× more density on the same hardware."* This single talk ignited the modern container revolution.

### 1.4 Fundamental Virtualization vs. Containerization — The Core Distinction

- **Virtualization (Traditional VM):** Uses a **Hypervisor (Type-1 bare-metal: ESXi, Hyper-V KVM / Type-2 hosted: VirtualBox, VMware Workstation)** to virtualize the **hardware**. Each VM contains its **own full Guest OS**, a kernel, binaries, libraries, and the app. The hypervisor translates privileged instructions (ring-decompression / VT-x / AMD-V). A typical VM image is **10–20 GB**, and boot time is **30–60 seconds**.

- **Containerization:** Virtualizes the **OS layer**, not the hardware. All containers share the **host OS kernel**, eliminating the Guest OS overhead. A typical container image is **50–500 MB**, and startup time is **<1 second**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric:** Container density on a host is typically **4× to 10× higher** than VM density because there is no duplicated kernel RAM/CPU footprint.

### 1.5 Important Definitions (High-Yield Terminology)

- **Container Image:** A *static, immutable, layered* file system snapshot used as the template to spawn containers.
- **Container Runtime:** The software (e.g., `runc`, `containerd`, `CRI-O`) that actually creates and runs containers.
- **Orchestrator:** A system (e.g., **Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, Apache Mesos**) that automates deployment, scaling, healing, and networking of containers across a cluster.
- **Daemon (dockerd):** A long-running background service that manages Docker objects (images, containers, networks, volumes).
- **Registry:** A repository for storing and distributing images (e.g., **Docker Hub, Amazon ECR, Azure ACR, Google GCR, GitHub Container Registry**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" — Engineering Problems Containerization Solves

1. **"It works on my machine" syndrome** → Images bake the OS environment in.
2. **Slow VM provisioning** → Containers start in **sub-seconds**.
3. **Resource inefficiency** → No duplicated kernel; **higher consolidation ratio**.
4. **Monolithic deployment risk** → Containers encourage **microservices** architecture.
5. **Difficult CI/CD** → Containers give identical **build → test → production** parity.
6. **Cloud-portability lock-in** → OCI-compliant images run on AWS, Azure, GCP, on-prem equally.

### 2.2 The "How" — Linux Kernel Primitives Behind Containers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequently asked 14-mark question:** *"Explain the Linux kernel features that enable containerization."* Use the two pillars below.

**A) Linux Namespaces (`unshare`, `clone()` syscall) — Provide ISOLATION (What a process can SEE):**
Namespaces wrap global system resources so that a process inside a namespace perceives its own isolated instance.

| Namespace | Constant | Isolates |
|---|---|---|
| PID | `CLONE_NEWPID` | Process IDs |
| Network | `CLONE_NEWNET` | Network interfaces, IPs, routing tables |
| Mount | `CLONE_NEWNS` | Filesystem mount points |
| UTS | `CLONE_NEWUTS` | Hostname & domain name |
| IPC | `CLONE_NEWIPC` | Inter-process communication |
| User | `CLONE_NEWUSER` | User & Group IDs |
| Cgroup | `CLONE_NEWCGROUP` | cgroup root directory |

**B) Control Groups (cgroups v1 / cgroups v2) — Provide RESOURCE LIMITS (What a process can USE):**
cgroups account for and restrict the resource usage of a group of processes — CPU shares, memory caps, block I/O, network bandwidth, device allow/deny lists.

**C) Union File Systems (UnionFS / OverlayFS) — Provide LAYERED IMAGE STORAGE:**
Layers are stacked and presented as a single coherent filesystem. This is why images are small and `docker pull` is fast (layer caching).

**D) Security-Enhanced Linux (SELinux / AppArmor)** — Adds mandatory access control.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Reference Table

| Concept / Equation | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Container **Start Time** | $T_{container} \approx 0.05 \text{ to } 0.5$ seconds | vs. VM $T_{vm} \approx 30 \text{ to } 60$ seconds |
| **Image Size Reduction** | $\text{Compression Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{VM Image Size}}{\text{Container Image Size}}$ | Typical ratio: **20:1 to 50:1** |
| **Container Density** | $D_c = \dfrac{\text{Host RAM (GB)}}{M_c}$ where $M_c$ = per-container memory | Density in containers per host |
| **Effective CPU Quota** (cgroup) | $\text{CPU}_{\text{quota}} = \dfrac{\text{cpu.cfs\_quota\_us}}{\text{cpu.cfs\_period\_us}}$ | e.g., $200000/100000 = 2$ vCPU |
| **Effective Memory Cap** | $M_{\text{cgroup}} = \text{memory.limit\_in\_bytes}$ | Hard OOM-kill at this limit |
| **Layer Sharing Savings** | $\text{Saved Space} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(L_i \times (N-i))$ for $N$ containers sharing $L_i$ layers | Pulls only delta-layers on update |
| **OS-Boot Elimination** | $\text{Overhead}_{\text{vm}} = T_{\text{bios}} + T_{\text{boot}} + T_{\text{init}} \approx 25\text{s}$ | Containers skip BIOS+kernel+init |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always state that containers **do NOT virtualize the kernel** — they *share* it. This is the single most-tested differentiator from VMs.

### 2.4 Container Image — Anatomy (Theory)

A container image is built from a **Dockerfile** and consists of:
- **Base Image Layer** (e.g., `ubuntu:22.04`, `alpine:3.18`) — rootfs.
- **Intermediate Layers** — each Dockerfile instruction (`RUN`, `COPY`, `ADD`) creates a new read-only layer.
- **Container Layer (R/W)** — a thin writable layer added at runtime (Copy-on-Write semantics).
- **Image Manifest + Config JSON** — metadata, environment variables, ENTRYPOINT, CMD.
- **Digest (SHA-256)** — content-addressable identifier for immutability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Buzzword — "Layered Architecture":** The **Union File System** (e.g., Overlay2) mounts all layers into a single unified view.
