Deleting a file in the container layer only "whites it out" in the upper layer — the lower image layer remains intact and cacheable across all sibling containers. This is why 100 containers of the same image only consume the disk space of **1 image + 100 thin R/W layers**.

### 2.5 Multi-Container Architectural Types (Pattern Catalogue)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 2 specifically lists **"Multi-Container Types"**. Treat this as a pattern-recognition question worth 7–10 marks.

| Pattern | Description | Real Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Sidecar Container** | A helper container co-located in the same pod to extend/enhance the main app. | Log shipping (Fluentd), service mesh proxy (Envoy/Istio). |
| **Ambassador Container** | Proxies network traffic between the app and the outside world. | Legacy → cloud DB connection translation. |
| **Adapter Container** | Standardizes output (logs, metrics) of the main app for monitoring tools. | Normalizing heterogeneous app telemetry. |
| **Init Container** | Runs **once-to-completion** before the main container starts (setup, DB migration, secrets fetch). | Kubernetes `initContainer` field. |
| **Pod (Kubernetes)** | The **smallest deployable unit** — one or more containers sharing network namespace & volumes. | Atomic scheduling unit. |
| **Sidecar Mesh** | A service-mesh data-plane proxy per app instance. | Istio, Linkerd, Consul Connect. |
| **Job / Cron Containers** | Run-to-completion batch workloads. | `kubectl create job`. |
| **Docker Compose Stack** | A YAML-declared multi-container application on a **single host**. | Local dev, single-host deployment. |
| **Helm Chart (Multi-Container Release)** | Templated multi-pod release for Kubernetes. | Production-grade packaged apps. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Mathematical Demos

### 3.1 Derivation: Container Resource Quota from cgroup Settings

**Problem (Frequent 7-mark sub-part):**
A developer specifies the following cgroup CFS parameters in a container's JSON spec:
- `cpu.cfs_quota_us = 150000`
- `cpu.cfs_period_us = 100000`

Find the **effective CPU quota** allocated to the container. Also compute the **maximum container density** on a 2-socket, 16-core-per-socket, 64-GB-RAM host, assuming each container is allocated **1 vCPU equivalent (period = 100ms) and 1 GB RAM**.

---

**Step 1: Recall the Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS) bandwidth control formula.**

$$
\text{CPU}_{\text{quota}} \;=\; \frac{\text{cpu.cfs\_quota\_us}}{\text{cpu.cfs\_period\_us}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values.**

$$
\text{CPU}_{\text{quota}} \;=\; \frac{150000}{100000}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the division.**

$$
\text{CPU}_{\text{quota}} \;=\; 1.5
$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result in engineering terms.**

> The container is allowed to consume **1.5 vCPU equivalents** of compute time in every 100 ms scheduling window. If it exceeds this, the CFS throttles it (adds to `cpu.stat nr_throttled`).

$$
\boxed{\text{CPU}_{\text{quota}} = 1.5 \text{ vCPUs}}
$$

---

**Step 5: Maximum container density (theoretical upper bound).**

Total physical cores:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{sockets}} \times C_{\text{per-socket}} = 2 \times 16 = 32 \text{ cores}
$$

Effective containers based on CPU:

$$
D_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{C_{\text{total}} \times \text{vCPU}_{\text{per-core}}}{1.5} = \frac{32 \times 1}{1.5} \approx 21.33 \Rightarrow 21 \text{ containers}
$$

Effective containers based on RAM (each container = 1 GB):

$$
D_{\text{RAM}} = \frac{R_{\text{host}}}{M_{\text{container}}} = \frac{64 \text{ GB}}{1 \text{ GB}} = 64 \text{ containers}
$$

**Step 6: Apply the limiting-resource rule (the bottleneck wins).**

$$
D_{\max} = \min(D_{\text{CPU}}, D_{\text{RAM}}) = \min(21, 64) = 21
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{\max} = 21 \text{ containers}}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** CPU is the bottleneck. Adding more RAM will not increase density. To raise density, either *reduce the per-container vCPU allocation* (e.g., 0.5 vCPU) or *over-commit CPU* (burstable quota).

### 3.2 Derivation: Image Layer De-duplication Savings

**Problem (Frequently asked 7-mark sub-part):**
A base image (`ubuntu:22.04`) is 77 MB. A developer adds three layers:
- L1 (`apt-get install python3`): 120 MB
- L2 (`COPY ./app`): 50 MB
- L3 (`RUN pip install ...`): 200 MB

If **10 containers** are instantiated from this image, calculate:
(a) The **image size** (single image, deduplicated).
(b) The **on-disk size for 10 running containers** (each has a thin 5 MB R/W layer).
(c) The **storage saved vs. 10 full VM copies** (assume each VM image = 10 GB).

---

**Step 1: Image size = sum of read-only layers (no duplication).**

$$
S_{\text{image}} = L_{\text{base}} + L_1 + L_2 + L_3
$$

$$
S_{\text{image}} = 77 + 120 + 50 + 200 = 447 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{image}} = 447 \text{ MB}}
$$

---

**Step 2: On-disk size for 10 containers = 1 shared image + 10 × thin R/W layer.**

$$
S_{10} = S_{\text{image}} + (N \times S_{\text{rw}})
$$

$$
S_{10} = 447 + (10 \times 5) = 447 + 50 = 497 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{10} \text{ on disk} = 497 \text{ MB}}
$$

---

**Step 3: Storage saved vs. 10 VMs.**

$$
S_{\text{vm\_total}} = 10 \times 10 \text{ GB} = 100 \text{ GB} = 102400 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
\text{Saved} = S_{\text{vm\_total}} - S_{10} = 102400 - 497 = 101903 \text{ MB} \approx 99.5 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the compression/efficiency ratio.**

$$
\eta = \frac{S_{\text{vm\_total}}}{S_{10}} = \frac{102400}{497} \approx 206\times
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Storage efficiency} \approx 206\times \text{ improvement}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conclusion Line (board marks):** Containers achieve **~99.5 % storage reduction** by sharing the read-only image layers and adding only a tiny per-instance writable layer — a key driver of their economic efficiency in cloud data centers.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Virtualization vs. Containerization (Comparison Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **This 2-column comparison appears in almost every KTU Module-2 question paper.** Memorize the bolded discriminators.

| Parameter | Virtual Machine (VM) | Container |
|---|---|---|
| **Virtualization Layer** | **Hardware** (Hypervisor) | **OS** (Container Engine) |
| **Boot Time** | 30 – 60 s | **< 1 s (50 – 500 ms)** |
| **Image Size** | 10 – 20 GB | **50 – 500 MB** |
| **OS Overhead** | Full Guest OS per VM | **None — shares host kernel** |
| **Performance** | Near-native (≈ 95–97 %) | **Native (≈ 99 %)** |
| **Isolation** | Strong (separate kernel) | **Process-level (weaker)** |
| **Density per Host** | 10 – 30 VMs | **100 – 1000 containers** |
| **Security Boundary** | Hypervisor-enforced | **Namespaces + cgroups + seccomp** |
| **Portability** | Limited (hypervisor-specific formats) | **High (OCI-standard images)** |
| **Startup Order** | BIOS → Bootloader → Kernel → Init → App | **Runtime → App** |
| **Typical Tool** | VMware vSphere, KVM, Hyper-V | **Docker, Podman, containerd, CRI-O** |
| **Orchestrator** | vCenter, OpenStack | **Kubernetes, Swarm, Nomad** |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Container Image Format Layers

| Layer # | Component | Mutable? | Stored In | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| L0 | **Base Image** (`scratch`, `alpine`, `ubuntu`) | No | Registry | Provides rootfs + libc |
| L1–L(n−1) | **Intermediate Layers** (one per Dockerfile instruction) | No (read-only) | Registry + host cache | Application + dependencies |
| Ln | **Container R/W Layer** (Copy-on-Write) | **Yes** | Container runtime (Overlay2) | Runtime changes |
| — | **Image Manifest** (JSON) | No | Registry | Lists layers + digests |
| — | **Image Config** (JSON) | No | Registry | Env vars, ENTRYPOINT, CMD, history |

> **Deployment Flow:** `docker pull` → fetches **only missing layers** (content-addressed by SHA-256 digest) → daemon assembles them via OverlayFS → `docker run` adds a thin R/W layer on top.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Virtualization vs. Containerization Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Application A]:::app
    A2[Application B]:::app
    A3[Application C]:::app
    A4[App X]:::app
    A5[App Y]:::app
    A6[App Z]:::app

    B1[Guest OS - Ubuntu]:::guestos
    B2[Guest OS - Windows]:::guestos
    B3[Hypervisor - ESXi KVM]:::hyper
    B4[Container Engine - Docker]:::engine
    B5[Host OS Kernel]:::kernel
    B6[Physical Server Hardware]:::hw

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B4
    A5 --> B4
    A6 --> B4

    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B5
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6

    classDef app fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef guestos fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef hyper fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef engine fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef kernel fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#B0BEC5,stroke:#263238,color:#000
```

**How to read this diagram (for exam writing):**
- **Left branch (Virtualization):** Hardware → Hypervisor → Multiple **full Guest OS** → Applications. Each VM has its own kernel.
- **Right branch (Containerization):** Hardware → Host OS Kernel → **Container Engine** → Applications. **No guest OS, no duplicate kernel.**

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Container Image Layered Architecture (OverlayFS)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RWL[Container R W Layer - thin - Copy on Write]:::rw
    L3[Layer 3 - COPY app code - 50 MB]:::lyr
    L2[Layer 2 - RUN pip install - 200 MB]:::lyr
    L1[Layer 1 - RUN apt install - 120 MB]:::lyr
    L0[Layer 0 - Base Image ubuntu 22.04 - 77 MB]:::base
    REG[Container Registry - Docker Hub - OCI compliant]:::reg

    RWL --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L0
    L0 --> REG

    C1[Container Instance 1]:::inst
    C2[Container Instance 2]:::inst
    C3[Container Instance N]:::inst

    RWL -.shared read only layers.-> C1
    RWL -.shared read only layers.-> C2
    RWL -.shared read only layers.-> C3

    classDef rw fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef lyr fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef base fill:#B39DDB,stroke:#311B92,color:#FFF
    classDef reg fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef inst fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** The base image + all Dockerfile layers are stored *immutably* in the registry. When 10 containers start, they all **mount the same read-only layers** and each gets a tiny personal R/W layer. This is the key storage efficiency.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Multi-Container Pattern Catalogue (Pod-level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PodA[Kubernetes Pod]
        MA[Main App Container]:::main
        SC[Sidecar - Log Shipper]:::side
        AMB[Ambassador - DB Proxy]:::amb
        ADP[Adapter - Metrics Normalizer]:::adp
        IC[Init Container - DB Migrator]:::init
    end

    MA --- SC
    MA --- AMB
    MA --- ADP
    IC -.runs once first.-> MA

    classDef main fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000
    classDef side fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef amb fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef adp fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef init fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** A single **Pod** (Kubernetes' atomic scheduling unit) can host multiple containers. The **init container** runs to completion *before* the main container starts. The **sidecar, ambassador, and adapter** containers run alongside the main app, sharing the same network namespace and volumes.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Container Lifecycle (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created : docker create
    Created --> Running : docker start
    Running --> Paused : docker pause
    Paused --> Running : docker unpause
    Running --> Restarting : policy on-failure
    Restarting --> Running : successful restart
    Running --> Exited : process terminates
    Exited --> Running : docker start
    Exited --> Dead : docker rm
    Exited --> [*] : garbage collected
```

**State explanations for the answer sheet:**
- **Created** → filesystem prepared, no process running yet.
- **Running** → process active inside namespaces.
- **Paused** → SIGSTOP sent to all processes (cgroup freezer).
- **Restarting** → Docker policy triggers a restart (e.g., `on-failure`).
- **Exited** → main process terminated; container still exists.
- **Dead** → explicitly removed; cannot be restarted.

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Containerization Toolchain & Influencer Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    USER[Developer]:::dev
    DF[Dockerfile - declarative build spec]:::tool
    BLD[docker build - creates image]:::tool
    IMG[Container Image - OCI format]:::img
    REG[Container Registry - Docker Hub ECR GCR]:::reg
    PULL[docker pull - fetches layers]:::tool
    RUN[Container Runtime - containerd runc]:::engine
    ORCH[Orchestrator - Kubernetes Swarm]:::orch
    MON[Monitoring - Prometheus Grafana]:::mon
    NET[Networking - CNI Flannel Calico]:::net

    USER --> DF
    DF --> BLD
    BLD --> IMG
    IMG --> REG
    REG --> PULL
    PULL --> RUN
    ORCH --> RUN
    RUN --> MON
    RUN --> NET

    classDef dev fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef tool fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef img fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,color:#FFF
    classDef reg fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef engine fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef orch fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef mon fill:#B39DDB,stroke:#311B92,color:#FFF
    classDef net fill:#80DEEA,stroke:#006064,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** It shows the full *influencer chain* — from developer writing a Dockerfile, to the build, registry storage, pull, runtime, and finally the orchestrator + monitoring + networking layers that make containers production-grade.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define containerization. List any three advantages over traditional virtualization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Containerization is an OS-level virtualization technique that packages an application together with its dependencies into a standardized, isolated unit called a container, which runs on a shared host OS kernel.*

*Three advantages:*
1. *Containers start in **<1 second** vs. 30–60 s for VMs — faster scaling.*
2. *Image sizes are **50–500 MB** vs. 10–20 GB VMs — much lower storage footprint.*
3. *Container density on a host is **4×–10× higher** due to absence of duplicate Guest OS / kernel.*

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the three advantages — total 3.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
**What is a container image? Explain the role of Union File Systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*A **container image** is a static, immutable, layered file-system snapshot that contains the application code, runtime, libraries, and configuration. It serves as the **template** from which containers are instantiated.*

*The **Union File System** (e.g., OverlayFS, UnionFS) stacks multiple read-only image layers into a single coherent root filesystem view and adds a thin writable layer on top. It enables **layer caching**, **Copy-on-Write semantics**, and **efficient image distribution** because identical layers are downloaded only once and shared across containers.*

*(1 mark definition, 2 marks for UnionFS role.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model QP, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Linux kernel features** (Namespaces, cgroups, UnionFS) that make containerization possible. How do they differ from hypervisor-based virtualization?

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the **layered architecture of a container image** built from a Dockerfile. Compute the on-disk size for 10 running containers if the base image is 80 MB, three intermediate layers are 100 MB, 50 MB, and 200 MB, and each container adds a 4 MB R/W layer.

---

**MODEL ANSWER — Question A**

**(a) Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Containerization in Linux is built on three foundational kernel features:*

*1. **Namespaces** — Provide process-level **isolation** by wrapping global system resources. There are 7 types: PID (isolates process IDs), NET (network interfaces, IPs, routing), MNT (mount points), UTS (hostname), IPC (inter-process communication), USER (UID/GID), and CGROUP (cgroup root). Each container runs inside its own set of namespaces, so processes inside cannot see or affect those outside.*

*2. **Control Groups (cgroups v1/v2)** — Provide **resource accounting and limiting**. They restrict CPU shares (`cpu.cfs_quota_us`), memory caps (`memory.limit_in_bytes`), block I/O, network bandwidth, and device access. The Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS) bandwidth controller throttles any container that exceeds its CPU quota.*

*3. **Union File Systems (OverlayFS)** — Provide the **layered image storage**. Multiple read-only layers are merged into a single coherent view; the container's writes are captured in a thin upper R/W layer using Copy-on-Write semantics.*

*Difference from hypervisor-based virtualization:*
- *Hypervisors virtualize the **hardware** layer; each VM gets a full Guest OS + its own kernel.*
- *Containers virtualize the **OS layer**; all containers share the host kernel — making them lighter, faster, and denser.*

*(2 marks Namespaces, 2 marks cgroups, 1 mark UnionFS, 2 marks VM vs container comparison.)*

---

**(b) Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Diagram: A container image is built from a Dockerfile. Each instruction (`FROM`, `RUN`, `COPY`, `ADD`) creates a new read-only image layer. Layers are stacked bottom-up and merged by OverlayFS at runtime.*

*Layers (bottom → top):*
- *L0: Base image (e.g., `ubuntu:22.04`) — 80 MB*
- *L1: `RUN apt-get install python3` — 100 MB*
- *L2: `COPY ./app` — 50 MB*
- *L3: `RUN pip install -r requirements.txt` — 200 MB*
- *L4: **Container R/W layer (runtime, 4 MB per container)***

*Each layer is content-addressed by a SHA-256 digest. The image is an OCI manifest pointing to all layer tarballs.*

**Computation (refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for the worked derivation):**

Image size:

$$
S_{\text{image}} = 80 + 100 + 50 + 200 = 430 \text{ MB}
$$

On-disk size for 10 containers:

$$
S_{10} = 430 + (10 \times 4) = 430 + 40 = 470 \text{ MB}
$$

*Conclusion: Only **470 MB** is consumed to run 10 containers, because the 430 MB image is shared. Without sharing, 10 full copies would require **4300 MB** — a **~9× storage saving**.*

*(3 marks diagram + explanation, 4 marks computation.)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** What are the **major influencers of containerization**? Briefly describe the role of **Docker, Kubernetes, and the CNCF** in shaping the modern container ecosystem.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain with diagrams the **multi-container types** (sidecar, ambassador, adapter, init container) commonly used in Kubernetes pods. Why is the **Pod** considered the atomic scheduling unit?

---

**MODEL ANSWER — Question B**

**(a) Model Answer (7 marks):**

*The major influencers of containerization are:*

*1. **Docker (2013)** — Released by Solomon Hykes at PyCon 2013. It made containers easy to use by introducing the Dockerfile, Docker Hub registry, and a simple CLI. It is the technology that brought containers to mainstream developers.*

*2. **Kubernetes (K8s) — 2014/2015** — Originating from Google's internal **Borg** system, Kubernetes is a vendor-neutral **container orchestrator** that automates deployment, scaling, self-healing, service discovery, rolling updates, and secret management. It runs the **Container Orchestration** layer above container runtimes like containerd or CRI-O.*

*3. **CNCF (Cloud Native Computing Foundation, 2015)** — A vendor-neutral sub-foundation of the Linux Foundation that hosts Kubernetes, Prometheus, Envoy, containerd, etcd, and many other cloud-native projects. It ensures **open governance**, **standardization**, and **interoperability**.*

*4. **OCI (Open Container Initiative)** — Standardized the **image format** and **runtime specification**, enabling Docker, Podman, containerd, and CRI-O to interoperate.*

*5. **Linux Kernel (cgroups + namespaces)** — Provided the technical primitives that make isolation possible.*

*(Award ~1.5 marks per major influencer; the three requested are Docker, K8s, CNCF.)*

---

**(b) Model Answer (7 marks):**

*In Kubernetes, the **Pod** is the smallest deployable unit — one or more containers that share the same **network namespace** (same IP, same loopback, same IPC), the same **volumes**, and the same **lifecycle**. Pods are atomic — they are scheduled, started, and stopped as a single unit.*

*The four principal multi-container patterns inside a pod are:*

*1. **Sidecar** — A helper container that *augments* the main app. Example: a `fluentd` container shipping logs from the main app's stdout to a central log store. The main app is unaware of the sidecar's existence.*

*2. **Ambassador** — Acts as a *proxy* for external network communication. Example: a container that translates legacy on-prem database calls into cloud-native DynamoDB calls, abstracting the destination from the main app.*

*3. **Adapter** — *Standardizes* the output (logs, metrics) of heterogeneous main applications. Example: a container that aggregates Prometheus metrics from multiple app instances and exposes them in a uniform format.*

*4. **Init Container** — A specialized container that runs **to completion** *before* the main container starts. Example: a container that waits for a database to be ready, runs schema migrations, or fetches secrets from Vault.*

*Why Pod = atomic unit? Because the patterns above require *co-scheduled*, *co-located*, *shared-namespace* containers. Kubernetes cannot guarantee this at the container level — only at the pod level. Hence the pod is the fundamental unit of scheduling and scaling.*

*(2 marks for pod definition, 4 marks for the four patterns, 1 mark for "atomic scheduling" justification.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Containerization questions:**
>
> 1. **Writing "containers are lightweight VMs"** — KTU examiners **deduct marks** because containers do **not** virtualize hardware; they virtualize the OS. Always say: *"Containers share the host kernel and use namespaces + cgroups for isolation."*
>
> 2. **Confusing "image" with "container"** — An image is a *static, immutable template*; a container is a *running instance* of that image. A single image can spawn N containers.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the R/W layer in size calculations** — Many students compute the image size but forget to add the per-container thin R/W layer when asked about running containers. Always include both.
>
> 4. **Omitting the **cgroups v2** mention** — Modern kernels (≥ 5.x) use unified cgroups v2. Examiners appreciate the v1 → v2 evolution note.
>
> 5. **Writing "Kubernetes is a container runtime"** — K8s is an **orchestrator**, not a runtime. The actual runtime is containerd / CRI-O.
>
> 6. **Missing the "atomic scheduling" justification for Pods** — When asked about multi-container types, *always* end with the line: *"The Pod is the atomic scheduling unit because containers inside share a network namespace and must be co-scheduled."*
>
> 7. **Not mentioning the OCI standard** — Examiners (2024 scheme) expect at least one mention of OCI image-spec / runtime-spec for portability marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Quick-Recall Bullets for the night before the exam:**

- **Container** = runtime instance of an **image** sharing the **host kernel** via **namespaces + cgroups**.
- **Influencers:** **Docker (2013)**, **Kubernetes (2015, from Google Borg)**, **CNCF (2015)**, **OCI (2015)**, **Linux kernel**.
- **Virtualization** virtualizes **hardware** (hypervisor + full Guest OS). **Containerization** virtualizes the **OS** (engine + shared kernel).
- **Boot time:** VM ≈ 30–60 s, **Container ≈ 50–500 ms**.
- **Image size:** VM ≈ 10–20 GB, **Container ≈ 50–500 MB**.
- **Density:** VM ≈ 10–30/host, **Container ≈ 100–1000/host**.
- **Three kernel pillars:** **Namespaces** (isolation), **cgroups** (resource limits), **UnionFS / OverlayFS** (layered storage).
- **Seven namespace types:** **PID, NET, MNT, UTS, IPC, USER, CGROUP**.
- **Image layers are read-only + immutable; container layer is R/W and Copy-on-Write.**
- **Image digest** = SHA-256 hash → content-addressable, tamper-proof.
- **Container R/W layer is ~few MB** (sparse file, expands on demand).
- **CPU quota formula:** $\text{CPU}_{\text{quota}} = \dfrac{\text{cfs\_quota\_us}}{\text{cfs\_period\_us}}$ (default period 100 ms).
- **Max container density = min(CPU-bound, RAM-bound).**
- **Storage saving formula:** $\text{Saved} = N \times S_{\text{image}} - (S_{\text{image}} + N \times S_{\text{rw}})$.
- **Multi-container types:** **Sidecar, Ambassador, Adapter, Init** — all live inside a **Pod** (atomic scheduling unit).
- **Pod shares** network namespace, IPC, volumes, and lifecycle across its containers.
- **Orchestrator vs Runtime:** K8s = orchestrator; containerd/CRI-O/runc = runtime; dockerd = daemon.
- **Standardization bodies:** OCI (image & runtime spec), CNCF (project governance).
- **Lifecycle states:** **Created → Running → Paused / Exited → Dead**.
- **One-line definition to memorize for 3-mark questions:** *"Containerization is an OS-level virtualization method that packages an app + dependencies into a portable, isolated unit sharing the host OS kernel."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hands-On - Hypervisor and Containers Installation - Install Hypervisors and Deploy VMs on Local Machines. Install Any Container Platform and Deploy Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST635 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hands-On - Hypervisor and Containers Installation - Install Hypervisors and Deploy VMs on Local Machines. Install Any Container Platform and Deploy Applications # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Hypervisor — Formal Definition A **Hypervisor** (also called a Virtual Machine Monitor, VMM) is a piece of system software, firmware, or hardware that creates, runs, and manages multiple virtual machines (VMs) on a single physical host machine. It abstracts the underlying physical hardware (CPU, memory, storage, network) and allocates a portion of those resources to each guest operating system, allowing several isolated OS instances to run concurrently on the same hardware. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A hypervisor is a software layer that enables multiple operating systems to share a single hardware host by providing virtualized resources to each guest OS, ensuring isolation, portability, and efficient hardware utilization — a foundational building block of Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) cloud delivery models. ### 1.1.1 Types of Hypervisors (KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis) **Type-1 (Bare-Metal) Hypervisor** - Runs directly on the host's hardware. - No host OS required; the hypervisor itself acts as a thin OS. - Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V (Server Core), Citrix XenServer, KVM (Linux kernel-based). - Used in enterprise data centers and cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP backbone). **Type-2 (Hosted) Hypervisor** - Runs as an application on top of a conventional host operating system. - The host OS mediates access to hardware. - Examples: Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation/Player, Parallels Desktop, QEMU (user-mode). - Used in development, testing, and learning environments (this is what KTU lab sessions typically use). > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** Examiners LOVE asking "Differentiate between Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors." Always mention: (i) hardware dependency, (ii) performance overhead, (iii) use case, and (iv) at least one example for each type. ## 1.2 Virtual Machine (VM) — Formal Definition A **Virtual Machine** is a software-emulated equivalent of a physical computer that runs its own operating system (guest OS) and applications. Each VM is an isolated environment with virtualized CPU cores, RAM, disk, and NIC, fully managed by the hypervisor. ### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Apartment Building" Imagine a single plot of land (your physical hardware). A **Type-1 hypervisor is the foundation + structural engineer** — it builds the building directly on the soil, divides it into apartments (VMs), each with its own plumbing, electricity meter, walls, and door locks. A **Type-2 hypervisor is a landlord** — he owns a house (host OS) and converts rooms into rentable units (VMs), but he eats into some space for hallways and his own office. Each tenant (guest OS) thinks they have the whole plot, but in reality they are sharing it with others — neatly isolated but efficiently packed. ## 1.3 Container — Formal Definition A **Container** is a lightweight, standalone, executable package that bundles an application together with its dependencies (libraries, binaries, configuration files, runtime) into a single unit that runs consistently across any computing environment. Containers share the host OS kernel but run in isolated user-space instances using OS-level virtualization (namespaces, cgroups, union file systems). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition:** A container is an OS-level virtualization primitive that packages an application with all its dependencies into a portable, isolated runtime unit, sharing the host kernel but providing process-level isolation — the core building block of Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) and modern microservices. ### 1.3.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shipping Container" Think of a **VM as a full house** — it has its own foundation, walls, plumbing, electricity, and rooms. To ship one house across the ocean, you'd have to ship the entire house. A **container is like a standardized shipping container** — it can hold any cargo (your app + dependencies), has a standard size that fits on any ship, train, or truck (any host with a container engine), and is sealed so contents don't mix. You don't ship the ship itself — you just plug the container in. ## 1.4 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield) - **vCPU** = virtual CPU; a time-shared portion of a physical CPU core (commonly 1 vCPU ≈ 1 thread). - **Overhead** = resources consumed by the hypervisor/host OS itself; typical Type-2 overhead = **5–15%**; Type-1 overhead = **2–5%**. - **Container Density** = number of containers runnable per host (often 10–100× the VM count on identical hardware). - **Image** = a read-only template used to instantiate containers (e.g., `nginx:latest`). - **OCI (Open Container Initiative)** = the industry standard for container formats and runtimes. - **Docker Engine** = the most widely adopted container platform (daemon + CLI + REST API). - **CRI (Container Runtime Interface)** = Kubernetes' standardized API for pluggable runtimes (containerd, CRI-O). > [!NOTE] > **Recall Rule for Exams:** VMs virtualize the **hardware** (each gets its own kernel); Containers virtualize the **OS** (share the host kernel). This single line differentiates them in 90% of theory questions. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hypervisor Architecture — Operational Logic ### 2.1.1 Type-1 Hypervisor Operation (Bare-Metal) 1. **Boot Loader** loads hypervisor directly into memory. 2. **Control Domain / Dom0** (in Xen) or **Service Console** (in ESXi) starts first to manage the rest. 3. **Hardware resources are partitioned** using schedulers: - CPU → credit scheduler, BVT, or CFS-based vCPU scheduling. - Memory → ballooning, transparent page sharing, swapping. - I/O → para-virtualized drivers (virtio) or full emulation. 4. **Each VM** is launched as a process with isolated virtual devices. 5. **Live Migration** can move running VMs between hosts with near-zero downtime (used in VMware vMotion, KVM live migration). ### 2.1.2 Type-2 Hypervisor Operation (Hosted) 1. Host OS boots normally; user launches the hypervisor as an application. 2. Hypervisor creates a user-mode process for each VM. 3. Hardware access is funneled through host OS drivers (VT-x / AMD-V assist). 4. Performance penalty: every VM instruction may require 2–3 context switches (guest→hypervisor→host→hardware). 5. Suitable for labs, dev environments, and single-user scenarios. ### 2.1.3 Why Hypervisors Exist — The Engineering Rationale - **Server Consolidation** — run workloads from under-utilized servers on one box (typical server utilization rises from **~10%** to **~60–80%**). - **Hardware Independence** — workloads become portable across vendors. - **Fault Isolation** — a crash in one VM does not affect others. - **Snapshot & Cloning** — entire VM state can be saved/restored in seconds. ## 2.2 Container Engine Architecture (Docker Reference) ### 2.2.1 Core Components 1. **Docker Client (`docker` CLI)** — user-facing command interface. 2. **Docker Daemon (`dockerd`)** — long-running background service that manages images, containers, networks, and volumes. 3. **containerd** — high-level container runtime that handles image transfer, container execution, and supervision. 4. **runc** — low-level runtime (compliant with OCI spec) that actually creates containers using kernel features. 5. **Linux Kernel Features Used**: - **Namespaces** → PID, NET, MNT, UTS, IPC, USER (process isolation). - **cgroups** → resource limits (CPU shares, memory caps, blkio throttling). - **UnionFS / OverlayFS** → layered, copy-on-write image storage. ### 2.2.2 Container Lifecycle (Operational Flow) 1. **Pull** — `docker pull nginx:latest` fetches image layers from a registry. 2. **Create** — `docker create` sets up a writable layer over the image. 3. **Start** — `docker start` runs the containerized process via containerd → runc. 4. **Execute** — process runs isolated under cgroup + namespace constraints. 5. **Stop** — SIGTERM sent, then SIGKILL after grace period. 6. **Remove** — `docker rm` deletes the container's writable layer. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` (not `|`) inside all table cells to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Standard Values | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | Total Physical RAM Required for VMs | $\text{RAM}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{RAM}_{VM_i} + \text{RAM}_{\text{hypervisor overhead}}$ | GB; overhead ≈ **1–2 GB** for Type-1, **2–4 GB** for Type-2 | Capacity planning for VM host | | vCPU Allocation | $\text{vCPU}_{\text{total}} \le \text{Physical Cores} \times \text{Hyper-Threading Ratio}$ | Cores; HT ratio = **1, 2, or 4** | Avoid oversubscription crashes | | CPU Oversubscription Ratio | $\text{OSR} = \dfrac{\sum \text{vCPU}_{VMs}}{\text{Physical Core Count}}$ | Dimensionless; recommended $\le \textbf{4{:}1}$ for general workloads | Server consolidation tuning | | Memory Overcommit Ratio | $\text{MOR} = \dfrac{\sum \text{RAM}_{VMs}}{\text{RAM}_{\text{physical}}}$ | Dimensionless; typical = **1.0–1.5** | Trade-off between density and stability | | Container Density | $\text{CD} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Containers}}{\text{Physical Host}}$ | Containers/host; often **50–500** | Compare to VM density (typically **10–20**/host) | | Container Startup Time | $T_{\text{start}} \approx T_{\text{cgroup setup}} + T_{\text{namespace setup}}$ | Seconds; typically **$\le \textbf{1 s}$** | vs. VM boot $\approx \textbf{30–120 s}$ | | Storage Capacity (Thin Provisioning) | $\text{Allocated} = \sum \text{Provisioned Size}$, $\text{Used} = \text{actual data on disk}$ | GB / TB; $\text{Allocated} \ge \text{Used}$ | Avoid over-provisioning physical disks | | Image Layer Reuse Savings | $\text{Saved Space} = (\text{Image Layers Shared}) \times \text{Layer Size}$ | MB / GB | Why containers pull fast on second run | ## 2.4 VM vs Container — Engineering Trade-off Analysis | Parameter | Virtual Machine | Container | |---|---|---| | Virtualization Level | Hardware (full) | OS (kernel shared) | | Boot Time | **30–120 s** | **< 1 s** | | Image Size | **1–50 GB** | **10–500 MB** | | Isolation Strength | Very High (separate kernel) | Process-level (weaker, but hardened in K8s) | | Performance Overhead | **5–15%** | **< 2%** | | Kernel Sharing | No (each VM has own) | Yes (shares host kernel) | | OS Heterogeneity | Any OS (Windows on Linux, etc.) | Same kernel family (e.g., Linux on Linux) | | Hypervisor/Runtime Needed | Hypervisor (ESXi, VirtualBox) | Container engine (Docker, Podman, containerd) | | Typical Use Case | Legacy apps, multi-OS, strong isolation | Microservices, CI/CD, cloud-native apps | | Cloud Model | IaaS | PaaS / CaaS | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The "container vs VM" debate is a false dichotomy. Production systems use BOTH — VMs provide hardware-level isolation and tenancy boundaries on the cloud, while containers provide rapid, reproducible application deployment inside those VMs. This pattern is called **"VM-of-VMs"** or "container-on-VM" and is the de-facto architecture of AWS EKS, Azure AKS, and Google GKE. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Data Centers:** Type-1 hypervisors achieve 10–15× server consolidation ratios. - **DevOps:** Container images guarantee "works on my machine = works in production." - **CI/CD Pipelines:** Spin up a container in 1 s vs. provisioning a VM in minutes. - **Edge Computing:** Containers' small footprint makes them ideal for resource-constrained edge nodes. - **Hybrid Cloud:** OCI-compliant images run identically on AWS, Azure, on-prem — true workload portability. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Procedure A — Install Oracle VirtualBox (Type-2 Hypervisor) on Windows > [!IMPORTANT] > VirtualBox is the recommended hypervisor for KTU lab submissions because it is free, cross-platform, and requires no hardware firmware changes. ### Step 1: System Prerequisites Verification 1. Check OS: Windows 10/11 64-bit (or Linux/macOS). 2. Verify **Hardware Virtualization** is enabled in BIOS/UEFI: - Intel → Intel VT-x (`VMX`) - AMD → AMD-V (`SVM`) 3. Minimum free RAM: **8 GB** (to host host-OS + at least 2 GB for a guest VM). 4. Minimum free disk: **30–50 GB** for VM storage. ### Step 2: Download & Install 1. Navigate to `https://www.virtualbox.org/wiki/Downloads`. 2. Download: **VirtualBox platform package** (e.g., `VirtualBox-7.x.x-xxxxxx-Win.exe`). 3. Run installer → **Next → Next → Install** (accept default features: USB, Network, Python Core). 4. Optionally install **VirtualBox Extension Pack** for USB 2/3, RDP, and NVMe support. ### Step 3: Create Your First VM 1. Open VirtualBox → Click **"New"**. 2. Configure: - **Name:** `KTU-Ubuntu-Lab` - **Type:** Linux - **Version:** Ubuntu (64-bit) 3. **Memory Size:** allocate $\text{RAM}_{VM} = \textbf{2048 MB}$ (adjust per host capacity; never exceed **50%** of host RAM). 4. **Hard Disk:** "Create a virtual hard disk now" → VDI → Dynamically allocated → **20 GB**. 5. Click **Create**. ### Step 4: Mount OS ISO & Install 1. Select VM → **Settings → Storage → Controller: IDE → Empty optical drive**. 2. Click the disk icon → **Choose a disk file** → select downloaded Ubuntu ISO. 3. Click **Start** → VM boots from ISO. 4. Follow standard Ubuntu installation wizard. 5. After installation, **eject the ISO** (Devices → Optical Drives → Remove disk). 6. Reboot VM — you now have a working guest OS. ### Step 5: Install Guest Additions (Critical for Performance) 1. With VM running, click **Devices → Insert Guest Additions CD image**. 2. Inside guest, open terminal: ```bash sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y build-essential dkms linux-headers-$(uname -r) sudo mount /dev/cdrom /mnt sudo /mnt/VBoxLinuxAdditions.run sudo reboot ``` 3. Result: shared clipboard, drag-and-drop, dynamic resolution, and accelerated graphics. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Focus Area:** Always mention *Guest Additions* in practical viva. Examiners often ask: "What happens if Guest Additions are NOT installed?" (Answer: no shared folders, no seamless mouse, low screen resolution, no clipboard sync, slow graphics.) ## 3.2 Procedure B — Install KVM (Type-1) on Ubuntu Linux (Bonus / Advanced) ### Step 1: Verify CPU Virtualization Extensions ```bash egrep -c '(vmx|svm)' /proc/cpuinfo ``` If the output is **≥ 1**, hardware virtualization is supported. ### Step 2: Install Required Packages ```bash sudo apt update sudo apt install -y qemu-kvm libvirt-daemon-system libvirt-clients bridge-utils virt-manager ``` ### Step 3: Verify Installation ```bash sudo systemctl status libvirtd sudo virsh list --all virsh -c qemu:///system ``` ### Step 4: Create a VM via `virt-manager` GUI 1. Launch `virt-manager` → **File → New Virtual Machine**. 2. Choose **Local install media (ISO)** → browse to ISO. 3. Allocate RAM and vCPUs → set disk size (e.g., 20 GB qcow2). 4. Click **Begin Installation** → install guest OS. ## 3.3 Procedure C — Install Docker Engine (Container Platform) on Linux > [!IMPORTANT] > Docker is the most widely used container platform and is the recommended choice for KTU lab submissions on container installation. ### Step 1: Uninstall Old Versions (if any) ```bash sudo apt remove -y docker docker-engine docker.io containerd runc ``` ### Step 2: Set Up the Docker Repository ```bash sudo apt update sudo apt install -y ca-certificates curl gnupg sudo install -m 0755 -d /etc/apt/keyrings curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg sudo chmod a+r /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg echo \ "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg] \ https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu \ $(. /etc/os-release && echo "$VERSION_CODENAME") stable" | \ sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list > /dev/null ``` ### Step 3: Install Docker Engine ```bash sudo apt update sudo apt install -y docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io docker-buildx-plugin docker-compose-plugin ``` ### Step 4: Verify Docker Installation ```bash sudo docker --version sudo docker run hello-world ``` Expected: a multi-line message "Hello from Docker!" confirming the daemon is operational. ### Step 5: Post-Install — Run Docker without `sudo` (Recommended for Lab Use) ```bash sudo usermod -aG docker $USER newgrp docker docker run hello-world # should now work without sudo ``` > [!WARNING] > Adding a user to the `docker` group is equivalent to giving them root access. For production, use **rootless Docker** instead. ## 3.4 Procedure D — Deploy Your First Containerized Application (NGINX Web Server) ### Step 1: Pull the NGINX Image ```bash docker pull nginx:latest ``` This downloads the read-only image layers from Docker Hub (registry). ### Step 2: Run the Container ```bash docker run -d --name ktu-nginx -p 8080:80 nginx:latest ``` Parameter breakdown: - `-d` → detached (runs in background). - `--name ktu-nginx` → assigns a friendly container name. - `-p 8080:80` → **port mapping**: host port **8080** → container port **80** (NGINX default). - `nginx:latest` → the image to instantiate. ### Step 3: Verify the Deployment ```bash docker ps # list running containers curl http://localhost:8080 # should return NGINX welcome HTML ``` Open a browser on the host and visit `http://localhost:8080` → you should see the NGINX welcome page. ### Step 4: Manage the Container Lifecycle ```bash docker stop ktu-nginx # graceful stop (SIGTERM, then SIGKILL) docker start ktu-nginx # restart docker logs ktu-nginx # view stdout/stderr docker exec -it ktu-nginx bash # open interactive shell inside container docker rm -f ktu-nginx # force-remove the container ``` ### Step 5: Persist Custom Content (Volume Mount) ```bash mkdir ~/ktu-nginx-html echo "

KTU Cloud Computing Lab

" > ~/ktu-nginx-html/index.html docker run -d --name ktu-nginx-custom \ -p 8081:80 \ -v ~/ktu-nginx-html:/usr/share/nginx/html:ro \ nginx:latest ``` The `-v` flag mounts the host directory into the container, replacing the default NGINX page. ## 3.5 Procedure E — Alternative Container Platform: Podman (Rootless) For KTU submissions, demonstrating an alternative is a high-value add-on. ```bash sudo apt install -y podman podman --version podman run -d --name ktu-podman-httpd -p 8082:80 docker.io/library/httpd curl http://localhost:8082 ``` Podman is daemonless, rootless, and OCI-compliant — a drop-in alternative to Docker. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Platforms for Lab Use | Platform | Type | Privileged Access | Daemon | Best For | |---|---|---|---|---| | VirtualBox | Type-2 Hypervisor | Yes (host-level) | No daemon | VM labs, multi-OS | | KVM + QEMU | Type-1 Hypervisor | Yes (root) | libvirtd | Linux production hypervisor | | Docker | Container Engine | Optional (rootless mode) | dockerd | General container deployment | | Podman | Container Engine | No (rootless by default) | Daemonless | Secure, rootless containers | | containerd | Container Runtime | — | containerd | K8s node runtime | | LXC/LXD | OS-level | Root | lxdd | System containers (full OS) | ## 3.7 Common Pitfalls & Their Fixes | Symptom | Likely Cause | Fix | |---|---|---| | `docker: permission denied` | User not in `docker` group | `sudo usermod -aG docker $USER`, re-login | | `port is already allocated` | Host port in use by another service | Change host port: `-p 9090:80` | | VirtualBox VM very slow | Nested paging / VT-x disabled | Enable VT-x in BIOS; install Guest Additions | | `cannot connect to Docker daemon` | Daemon not running | `sudo systemctl start docker` | | `image not found` | Wrong image name or no internet | Verify name on Docker Hub; check `docker pull` connectivity | | KVM: `permission denied` on `/dev/kvm` | User not in `libvirt` group | `sudo usermod -aG libvirt $USER` | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Type-1 vs Type-2 Hypervisor Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Type1["TYPE 1 BARE METAL ARCHITECTURE"] direction TB A1["Guest VM 1\nApp + Guest OS"] A2["Guest VM 2\nApp + Guest OS"] A3["Guest VM 3\nApp + Guest OS"] H1["Hypervisor\nDirect on Hardware"] HW1["Physical Server\nCPU RAM Disk NIC"] A1 --> H1 A2 --> H1 A3 --> H1 H1 --> HW1 end subgraph Type2["TYPE 2 HOSTED ARCHITECTURE"] direction TB B1["Guest VM 1\nApp + Guest OS"] B2["Guest VM 2\nApp + Guest OS"] H2["Hypervisor App\nVirtualBox or VMware Workstation"] OS2["Host Operating System\nWindows or Linux"] HW2["Physical Server\nCPU RAM Disk NIC"] B1 --> H2 B2 --> H2 H2 --> OS2 OS2 --> HW2 end ``` ## 4.2 Container Platform Stack (Docker Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DockerArch["DOCKER ENGINE STACK"] direction TB CLI["Docker CLI\ndocker commands"] API["REST API\ndockerd listening endpoint"] DAEMON["Docker Daemon\ndockerd background service"] CONTAINERD["containerd\nhigh level runtime"] RUNC["runc\nlow level OCI runtime"] KERNEL["Linux Kernel\nnamespaces cgroups overlayfs"] CLI --> API API --> DAEMON DAEMON --> CONTAINERD CONTAINERD --> RUNC RUNC --> KERNEL end REG["Docker Hub\nImage Registry"] -->|"pull images"| DAEMON DAEMON -->|"run stop rm"| CONTAINERS["Running Containers"] ``` ## 4.3 End-to-End Lab Deployment Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Step 1\nEnable VT-x in BIOS"] --> S2["Step 2\nInstall VirtualBox"] S2 --> S3["Step 3\nCreate VM\nAllocate RAM vCPU Disk"] S3 --> S4["Step 4\nMount ISO\nInstall Guest OS"] S4 --> S5["Step 5\nInstall Guest Additions"] S5 --> S6["Step 6\nInstall Docker Engine on Host"] S6 --> S7["Step 7\nPull NGINX Image"] S7 --> S8["Step 8\nRun Container with Port Mapping"] S8 --> S9["Step 9\nVerify via curl or Browser"] S9 --> S10["Step 10\nStop Remove Manage Containers"] ``` ## 4.4 VM vs Container Resource Sharing Model ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph VMModel["VIRTUAL MACHINE MODEL"] direction TB VA["App A"] VB["App B"] VAOS["Guest OS A\nown kernel"] VBOS["Guest OS B\nown kernel"] VHYP["Hypervisor"] VHW["Physical Hardware"] VA --> VAOS VB --> VBOS VAOS --> VHYP VBOS --> VHYP VHYP --> VHW end subgraph CONModel["CONTAINER MODEL"] direction TB CA["App A\nshared libs"] CB["App B\nshared libs"] CR["Container Engine\nDocker or Podman"] CHK["Host OS Kernel\nshared"] CHW["Physical Hardware"] CA --> CR CB --> CR CR --> CHK CHK --> CHW end ``` ## 4.5 Port Mapping Mechanism (Why `-p 8080:80` Works) ```mermaid flowchart LR BROWSER["Browser\nhttp localhost 8080"] -->|"host port 8080"| HOST["Host Network Stack"] HOST -->|"DOCKER iptables NAT"| CONTAINER["Container Network\nport 80 NGINX"] CONTAINER -->|"response back"| HOST HOST -->|"html page"| BROWSER ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **Differentiate between Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors. Give one example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Type-1 (Bare-Metal) | Type-2 (Hosted) | |---|---|---| | **Location** | Runs directly on hardware | Runs as an application over host OS | | **Host OS Required** | No | Yes | | **Performance** | High efficiency, low overhead (**2–5%**) | Lower due to host OS mediation (**5–15%**) | | **Use Case** | Enterprise data centers, cloud backends | Development, testing, lab environments | | **Example** | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Citrix XenServer, KVM | Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels | *(Award 1 mark for each well-explained parameter; reserve 1 mark for at least one valid example per type.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Style]** **What is a container? List any two advantages of containers over virtual machines.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **container** is a lightweight, standalone, executable software package that bundles an application with its dependencies (libraries, runtime, configuration) into a single unit, sharing the host OS kernel but running in isolated user-space instances (using namespaces and cgroups). **Two advantages over VMs:** 1. **Faster startup** — containers start in **< 1 s** compared to **30–120 s** for VMs because no separate OS kernel needs to boot. 2. **Higher density and efficiency** — a single host can run **50–500 containers** (vs. **10–20 VMs**) due to shared kernel and smaller image sizes (**10–500 MB** vs. **1–50 GB**). 3. *(Optional 3rd point)* **Better portability** — OCI-compliant images run identically on any host with a container engine. *(Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark each for the two advantages.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU End-Semester Examinations (ESE) follow a strict internal-choice pattern for Module-2 questions. **Attempt either Option A or Option B in full.** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]* **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a **Type-2 hosted hypervisor**. Describe the step-by-step procedure to install **Oracle VirtualBox** and create a Linux VM with at least 2 GB RAM and 20 GB dynamically allocated disk. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of **OS-level virtualization** and describe the **Docker engine architecture** with a diagram. List the Linux kernel features (namespaces, cgroups, union file systems) that enable container isolation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks:** *Step 1 — Architecture (3 marks):* A Type-2 hypervisor runs as an application **on top of the host OS**, which in turn controls the physical hardware. The architecture is: - **Application Layer** → user apps inside each guest VM. - **Guest OS Layer** → each VM runs its own kernel (e.g., Windows guest on Linux host). - **Hypervisor Layer** → VirtualBox translates guest instructions; uses VT-x/AMD-V for hardware assist. - **Host OS Layer** → mediates all hardware access. - **Hardware Layer** → physical CPU, RAM, disk, NIC. *Step 2 — Installation Procedure (4 marks):* 1. Verify **hardware virtualization** is enabled in BIOS (Intel VT-x or AMD-V). *(0.5 mark)* 2. Download VirtualBox installer from `virtualbox.org` and run with default options. *(0.5 mark)* 3. (Optional) Install **VirtualBox Extension Pack** for USB 2/3 and RDP support. *(0.5 mark)* 4. Open VirtualBox → click **New** → enter: - Name: `KTU-Linux-VM` - Type: Linux, Version: Ubuntu (64-bit) - Memory: **2048 MB** $(= \text{RAM}_{VM})$ - Hard disk: **Create VDI, dynamically allocated, 20 GB** $(= \text{Disk}_{VM})$ *(1.5 marks)* 5. Mount Linux ISO in **Settings → Storage → Optical Drive**. *(0.5 mark)* 6. Start VM → install Linux via standard wizard. *(0.5 mark)* 7. Install **Guest Additions** for shared clipboard, dynamic resolution, and accelerated graphics. *(0.5 mark)* *Key formulas to mention:* $$\text{RAM}_{\text{available for VMs}} = \text{RAM}_{\text{host}} - \text{RAM}_{\text{host OS}} - \text{RAM}_{\text{hypervisor overhead}}$$ $$\text{vCPU}_{total} \le \text{Physical Cores} \times \text{Hyper-Threading Ratio}$$ **Part (b) — 7 marks:** *Concept of OS-level virtualization (2 marks):* OS-level virtualization creates multiple **isolated user-space instances** (containers) on a single host OS kernel. Unlike VMs, there is **no separate guest kernel** — all containers share the host kernel but appear as independent OS environments to the applications inside them. *Docker Engine Architecture (3 marks):* The Docker platform has a layered architecture: - **Docker CLI** (`docker` command) — user-facing interface. - **REST API** — communication channel. - **Docker Daemon (`dockerd`)** — background service managing images, containers, networks, volumes. - **containerd** — high-level container runtime that handles image push/pull and supervises containers. - **runc** — low-level OCI-compliant runtime that actually creates containers. *Linux kernel features for isolation (2 marks):* 1. **Namespaces** — wrap global system resources (PID, NET, MNT, UTS, IPC, USER) so each container has its own view. *(1 mark)* 2. **cgroups (Control Groups)** — account for and limit resource usage (CPU shares, memory caps, block I/O). *(0.5 mark)* 3. **Union File Systems (OverlayFS)** — layer image files in a copy-on-write stack for efficient storage. *(0.5 mark)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]* **(a)** Compare and contrast **virtual machines and containers** across at least six parameters. Explain why containers are preferred for **microservices and CI/CD pipelines**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the step-by-step procedure to **install Docker Engine on Ubuntu Linux** and deploy an **NGINX web server** accessible on host port 8080. Include the verification steps. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks:** *Comparison Table (5 marks):* | Parameter | Virtual Machine | Container | |---|---|---| | Virtualization Level | Hardware | OS (kernel shared) | | Boot Time | 30–120 s | < 1 s | | Image Size | 1–50 GB | 10–500 MB | | Isolation | Strong (separate kernel) | Process-level (weaker) | | Performance Overhead | 5–15% | < 2% | | OS Heterogeneity | Any (Windows on Linux, etc.) | Same kernel family | | Hypervisor/Engine | ESXi, VirtualBox, KVM | Docker, Podman, containerd | | Density per host | 10–20 VMs | 50–500 containers | *Why containers preferred for microservices/CI/CD (2 marks):* 1. **Microservices fit naturally** — each service can be a small container image (~100 MB) that starts instantly and scales horizontally. *(1 mark)* 2. **CI/CD pipelines** — containers spin up in **< 1 s**, ensuring fast test runs and reproducible builds; image immutability guarantees "what was tested = what is deployed." *(1 mark)* **Part (b) — 7 marks:** *Step 1 — Install Docker Engine (3 marks):* ```bash sudo apt update sudo apt install -y ca-certificates curl gnupg sudo install -m 0755 -d /etc/apt/keyrings curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg | \ sudo gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg] \ https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu $(. /etc/os-release && echo "$VERSION_CODENAME") stable" | \ sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list > /dev/null sudo apt update sudo apt install -y docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io docker-buildx-plugin docker-compose-plugin ``` *Step 2 — Verify Installation (1 mark):* ```bash sudo docker --version sudo docker run hello-world ``` *Step 3 — Deploy NGINX (2 marks):* ```bash docker pull nginx:latest docker run -d --name ktu-nginx -p 8080:80 nginx:latest ``` *Step 4 — Verify Deployment (1 mark):* ```bash docker ps # confirm container is Up curl -I http://localhost:8080 # expect HTTP/1.1 200 OK # or open browser to http://localhost:8080 ``` **Port-mapping logic to mention:** $$\text{HTTP request} \xrightarrow{\text{host port 8080}} \text{Docker NAT} \xrightarrow{\text{container port 80}} \text{NGINX}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "hypervisor" with "emulator"** — QEMU is an emulator; KVM + QEMU is a hypervisor. Use precise terminology. > 2. **Forgetting to mention Guest Additions in VirtualBox** — Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if omitted. > 3. **Writing `docker run nginx` without `-d` and `--name`** — results in foreground execution; examiners mark the procedure as "incomplete." > 4. **Saying "containers have no isolation"** — WRONG. They have process-level isolation via namespaces/cgroups; it is just weaker than VM-level isolation. > 5. **Mixing up `docker pull` (image) and `docker run` (container)** — these are distinct operations; examiners explicitly test this distinction. > 6. **Not explaining why `-p 8080:80` is needed** — always say "host port → container port mapping via Docker's iptables NAT." > 7. **Oversubscribing vCPUs** — claiming "1 VM = 1 physical core" without explaining HT ratio or oversubscription ratio loses application-level marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:** - ☐ **Hypervisor** = VMM; enables multiple OS on one host; two types: **Type-1 (bare-metal, ESXi, KVM)** and **Type-2 (hosted, VirtualBox, VMware Workstation)**. - ☐ **Virtual Machine** = hardware-level virtualization; each VM has its own kernel; boot time **30–120 s**; image size **1–50 GB**. - ☐ **Container** = OS-level virtualization; shares host kernel; uses **namespaces + cgroups + union FS**; boot time **< 1 s**; image size **10–500 MB**. - ☐ **Docker Architecture** = CLI → REST API → `dockerd` → `containerd` → `runc` → Linux kernel. - ☐ **Hardware Virtualization** must be enabled in BIOS (Intel **VT-x** / AMD **AMD-V**) for both VMs and containers to perform well. - ☐ **VirtualBox Guest Additions** are mandatory for shared folders, clipboard, and dynamic resolution. - ☐ **Port mapping syntax:** `-p :` (e.g., `-p 8080:80`). - ☐ **Resource formulas to memorize:** - $\text{RAM}_{\text{total}} = \sum \text{RAM}_{VM} + \text{Overhead}$ - $\text{OSR} = \dfrac{\sum \text{vCPU}_{VM}}{\text{Physical Cores}} \le 4$ - $\text{MOR} = \dfrac{\sum \text{RAM}_{VM}}{\text{RAM}_{\text{physical}}} \approx 1.0–1.5$ - ☐ **OCI** (Open Container Initiative) governs container image and runtime standards. - ☐ **Common commands to remember:** - `docker pull ` — fetch image - `docker run -d --name -p : ` — start container - `docker ps` / `docker ps -a` — list running / all containers - `docker stop ` / `docker start ` — lifecycle - `docker rm -f ` — force remove - `docker logs ` — view logs - `docker exec -it bash` — open shell - ☐ **Production architecture reality:** VMs provide **tenancy boundaries** in the cloud; containers provide **application deployment agility** inside those VMs — they are **complementary, not competing**. - ☐ **Alternatives to Docker:** Podman (rootless, daemonless), containerd (K8s runtime), LXC/LXD (system containers), CRI-O. - ☐ **Always run `sudo docker run hello-world`** as the first verification step after Docker install. - ☐ **Troubleshooting mnemonic — "PRISM"**: **P**ermissions (group), **R**esources (RAM/disk), **I**mage name, **S**ervice status (daemon), **M**apping (port conflicts).

topics.title: Resource Management - Resource Pooling, Sharing, Provisioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST635 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Resource Management - Resource Pooling, Sharing, Provisioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Resource Management in Cloud Computing (Formal Definition)

**Resource Management** in cloud computing refers to the systematic orchestration, allocation, monitoring, and de-allocation of computing resources (CPU, memory, storage, network bandwidth, and software services) across multiple cloud consumers in a manner that satisfies Quality of Service (QoS) requirements, Service Level Agreements (SLAs), and economic efficiency goals.

According to the **NIST SP 800-145** definition of cloud computing, resource management is the foundational capability that enables the five essential characteristics: *On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, and Measured service*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Resource Management encompasses three core strategies — **Pooling** (consolidation of resources), **Sharing** (multi-tenancy access), and **Provisioning** (on-demand allocation) — that collectively transform distributed physical infrastructure into an elastic, virtualized service fabric.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Cloud Resource Management

| Pillar | Core Idea | KTU Key Term |
|---|---|---|
| **Resource Pooling** | Aggregating heterogeneous physical/virtual resources into a unified logical pool | Consolidation / Aggregation |
| **Resource Sharing** | Multiple consumers accessing the pool concurrently via virtualization & multi-tenancy | Multi-Tenancy |
| **Resource Provisioning** | Dynamic, automated allocation and de-allocation of pooled resources | On-Demand Allocation |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electricity Grid" Model

Imagine cloud resource management like a **municipal electricity grid**:

- **Resource Pooling** ≈ The power plant + substation + transmission lines. All generators (physical servers) feed into one common supply.
- **Resource Sharing** ≈ Every household plugs into the same grid simultaneously. You don't care *which* specific generator powers your bulb; you just get electricity.
- **Resource Provisioning** ≈ The smart meter that ramps up or cuts supply based on whether you switch on a 60W bulb or a 2kW air conditioner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** In the exam, if a question asks "How does cloud achieve economies of scale?", the answer ALWAYS involves all three — *pool the resources → share them across tenants → provision dynamically based on demand*.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **SLA (Service Level Agreement):** Contractual guarantee, typically measured in **availability %** (e.g., **99.9%** = "three nines" = ~8.77 hours downtime/year).
- **Multi-tenancy:** A *single* software instance serving *multiple* tenants (organizations).
- **Elasticity:** Ability to scale resources **out** (horizontal — add more VMs) or **up** (vertical — bigger VM).
- **Over-commitment ratio:** Ratio of provisioned resources to physical resources (commonly **1.5:1 to 2:1** for CPU, higher for storage).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Resource Pooling — Theoretical Breakdown

**Definition:** The process of consolidating heterogeneous physical resources (servers, storage arrays, network switches) belonging to a cloud provider into one or more logical resource pools that can be dynamically carved up for consumers.

**Operational Steps:**
1. **Discovery & Inventory:** Catalog all physical resources (compute hosts, storage volumes, IP addresses).
2. **Virtualization Layer Insertion:** Insert a hypervisor (Type-1: VMware ESXi, Xen, KVM) or container runtime (Docker) to abstract hardware.
3. **Logical Pool Formation:** Group resources by type — *Compute Pool, Storage Pool, Network Pool*.
4. **Pool Exposure:** Register pools with a cloud orchestrator (OpenStack Nova, AWS EC2 controller).
5. **Tenant Mapping:** Map consumer subscriptions to slices of the pool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept:** Pooling happens at the **infrastructure layer**. A "Pool" is the supply side; consumers never interact with it directly — they interact with **virtual resources carved from the pool**.

### 2.2 Resource Sharing — Theoretical Breakdown

Sharing relies on **multi-tenancy** — multiple cloud customers (tenants) share the same underlying pooled resources while maintaining logical isolation.

**Isolation Mechanisms:**
- **Compute isolation:** Virtual machines (VMs) with dedicated vCPUs and RAM.
- **Storage isolation:** Logical Unit Numbers (LUNs), quotas, or namespace separation.
- **Network isolation:** Virtual LANs (VLANs), Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs), Security Groups.
- **Application-level isolation:** Tenant IDs in DB schemas, separate authentication realms.

**Sharing Models:**
- **Dedicated resources:** Single-tenant, e.g., AWS Dedicated Host.
- **Shared resources:** Multi-tenant, default model for public clouds.
- **Hybrid sharing:** Some resources dedicated (e.g., compliance-sensitive data), others shared.

### 2.3 Resource Provisioning — Theoretical Breakdown

Provisioning is the **act of allocating** a slice of the resource pool to a consumer workload.

**Two Modes:**
- **Static Provisioning:** Fixed resources reserved at deployment; wastes capacity during low usage.
- **Dynamic Provisioning:** Auto-scaling triggered by monitoring metrics (CPU > 70% → add VM).

**Provisioning Workflow:**
1. Consumer submits request (e.g., "Launch 4 VMs, 8GB RAM each").
2. Scheduler (e.g., OpenStack Nova Scheduler) evaluates pool health.
3. Placement algorithm (e.g., Filter-Weigher, Best-Fit) selects physical host.
4. Hypervisor carves out VM and binds resources.
5. Consumer receives access credentials.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units / Typical Value | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Availability %** | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$ | Percent (e.g., **99.95%**) | SLA compliance tracking |
| **Downtime per year** | $D = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24$ | Hours/year | Capacity planning |
| **Over-commitment Ratio** | $R_{oc} = \frac{\text{Provisioned vCPUs}}{\text{Physical CPU cores}}$ | Ratio (typical **1.5:1 to 2:1**) | Maximizing pool utilization |
| **Pool Utilization** | $U = \frac{\text{Allocated Resources}}{\text{Total Pool Capacity}} \times 100$ | Percent | Efficiency benchmarking |
| **Elasticity Latency** | $T_{e} = T_{provision} + T_{boot}$ | Seconds / Minutes | SLA response time |
| **Cost per Tenant** | $C_{tenant} = \frac{Total\ Pool\ Cost}{N_{tenants} \times U}$ | Currency / unit | Pricing model design |
| **Scale-out trigger threshold** | $\theta = U_{current} > U_{threshold}$ | Percent (typical **70%–80%**) | Auto-scaling policy |
| **Resource Fairness Index** | $F = 1 - \frac{\sigma(R_{alloc})}{\mu(R_{alloc})}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Multi-tenant fairness |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *Sharing* (multi-tenancy access mechanism) with *Pooling* (physical aggregation). Remember: **Pooling creates the supply; Sharing defines who can tap it; Provisioning decides how much each gets**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AWS EC2:** Maintains a global pool of physical servers; shares them across millions of AWS accounts; provisions EC2 instances on demand.
- **Google BigQuery:** Pools petabytes of storage across data centers; shares via project-isolated multi-tenancy; provisions query slots dynamically.
- **Microsoft Azure:** Uses **Azure Resource Manager (ARM)** to pool, share, and provision resources across subscriptions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Annual Downtime from Availability

**Problem:** A cloud provider guarantees **99.95%** availability. Calculate the maximum permitted annual downtime.

**Step 1:** Write the availability formula.

$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$$

But for SLA calculation, we use the equivalent:

$$A_{decimal} = \frac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime}$$

**Step 2:** Express downtime fraction.

$$1 - A_{decimal} = \frac{Downtime}{Total\ Time}$$

**Step 3:** Total time in a year (in hours).

$$T_{year} = 365 \times 24 = 8760 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values.

$$D = (1 - 0.9995) \times 8760$$

$$D = 0.0005 \times 8760$$

**Step 5:** Compute.

$$D = 4.38 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Conclusion:** The cloud can be down for at most **4.38 hours per year** under a 99.95% SLA.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Pool Utilization Under Over-commitment

**Problem:** A cloud data center has **64 physical CPU cores** in a compute pool. The cloud operator runs an over-commitment ratio of **2:1**. How many vCPUs can be provisioned, and what is the utilization when 80 vCPUs are actively allocated?

**Step 1:** Over-commitment ratio formula.

$$R_{oc} = \frac{V_{prov}}{P_{phys}}$$

where $V_{prov}$ is provisioned vCPUs, $P_{phys}$ is physical cores.

**Step 2:** Solve for provisioned capacity.

$$V_{prov} = R_{oc} \times P_{phys}$$

$$V_{prov} = 2 \times 64 = 128 \text{ vCPUs}$$

**Step 3:** Utilization formula.

$$U = \frac{V_{active}}{V_{prov}} \times 100$$

**Step 4:** Substitute.

$$U = \frac{80}{128} \times 100$$

$$U = 62.5\%$$

**Conclusion:** The pool can provision **128 vCPUs** from **64 physical cores**, and at the current load of 80 active vCPUs, utilization is **62.5%** — safely below the typical auto-scale trigger of 70–80%.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Pooling vs Sharing vs Provisioning

| Dimension | Resource Pooling | Resource Sharing | Resource Provisioning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Layer** | Infrastructure (physical/virtual) | Platform / Multi-tenancy | Service / Application |
| **Who acts** | Cloud Provider | Cloud Platform | Cloud Orchestrator / Consumer (via API) |
| **Trigger** | Infrastructure setup | Tenant onboarding | Workload demand |
| **Mechanism** | Hypervisor, Storage aggregation | VLANs, Auth, Quotas | Scheduler, Auto-scaler |
| **Output** | Resource Pool | Tenant Slice | Active VM / Service Instance |
| **Time-scale** | Days–Months (setup) | Hours–Days (onboarding) | Seconds–Minutes (on-demand) |
| **Failure impact** | Affects all tenants | Affects one tenant group | Affects one workload |
| **KTU keyword** | Aggregation | Multi-tenancy | Elasticity / On-demand |
| **Example** | 1000 servers → 1 compute pool | 1 pool shared by 500 tenants | Tenant A gets 8 vCPUs from pool |

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Auto-Scaling Decision

**Scenario:** A pool reports current utilization $U_{current} = 85\%$. Threshold $\theta = 75\%$. Each new VM adds **4 vCPUs**. Pool has 20 spare vCPUs capacity.

**Step 1:** Compare.

$$U_{current} = 85\% \quad > \quad \theta = 75\%$$

**Step 2:** Decision: **scale-out** triggered.

**Step 3:** Number of VMs to add (using simple linear model).

$$N_{add} = \left\lceil \frac{U_{current} - \theta}{100 / V_{pool}} \right\rceil$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values, assuming $V_{pool} = 100$ vCPUs.

$$N_{add} = \left\lceil \frac{85 - 75}{100/100} \right\rceil = \lceil 10 \rceil = 10 \text{ VMs}$$

**Step 5:** Validate capacity: $10 \times 4 = 40$ vCPUs needed; only **20 vCPUs spare** → provisioning partially fails; only **5 VMs** can be added.

**Step 6:** Pool capacity must be expanded, or threshold relaxed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Resource Management Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Physical Resources Layer] --> B[Virtualization and Hypervisor Layer]
    B --> C[Resource Pools Layer]
    C --> D[Multi Tenancy and Sharing Layer]
    D --> E[Orchestration and Provisioning Layer]
    E --> F[Tenant A Workloads]
    E --> G[Tenant B Workloads]
    E --> H[Tenant C Workloads]
    F --> I[Monitoring and Metering]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[SLA and Billing Engine]
    C -.dynamic allocation.-> E
    I -.feedback signal.-> E
```

### 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram: Pooling → Sharing → Provisioning Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Compute Hosts] --> P2[Compute Pool]
    P3[Storage Arrays] --> P4[Storage Pool]
    P5[Network Switches] --> P6[Network Pool]
    P2 --> S1[Virtualization Abstraction]
    P4 --> S1
    P6 --> S1
    S1 --> S2[Tenant Onboarding and Identity]
    S2 --> S3[Resource Quota Assignment]
    S3 --> PR1[Provisioning Engine and Scheduler]
    S3 --> PR2[Auto Scaler]
    S3 --> PR3[Load Balancer]
    PR1 --> W1[VM Instances for Tenant A]
    PR1 --> W2[VM Instances for Tenant B]
    PR2 --> W1
    PR2 --> W2
    PR3 --> W1
    PR3 --> W2
    W1 --> M1[Usage Metrics Collector]
    W2 --> M1
    M1 --> M2[Billing and SLA Dashboard]
```

### 4.3 Auto-Scaling Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Workload Running]) --> Mon[Monitor Resource Utilization]
    Mon --> Check{Utilization Exceeds Threshold}
    Check -->|No| Stay[Maintain Current Capacity]
    Check -->|Yes| Cap{Sufficient Spare Capacity}
    Cap -->|Yes| ScaleOut[Provision Additional VMs]
    Cap -->|No| ScalePool[Expand Resource Pool]
    ScaleOut --> Log[Log Provisioning Event]
    ScalePool --> Log
    Stay --> Mon
    Log --> Mon
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading these diagrams for KTU exams:** A block diagram question in Module 3 typically asks you to *label* the three pillars and *describe* the data flow. Practice explaining each box in one sentence — that's a guaranteed 2-mark differentiator.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`**  
**Differentiate between Resource Pooling and Resource Provisioning in cloud computing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Resource Pooling | Resource Provisioning |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Aggregates physical/virtual resources into a unified logical pool. | Allocates a slice of the pool to a consumer on demand. |
| **When** | Occurs at infrastructure setup / ongoing consolidation. | Occurs at request time (real-time or scheduled). |
| **Who** | Provider's infrastructure team. | Orchestrator / scheduler / consumer via API. |
| **Example** | 500 physical hosts form a single compute pool. | Tenant A is provisioned 8 vCPUs and 32GB RAM. |

In short, **pooling creates the supply**, while **provisioning delivers the demand** to that supply. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`**  
**What is multi-tenancy in cloud resource sharing? List two isolation mechanisms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Multi-tenancy** is a cloud architecture pattern where a *single* instance of software (and its underlying infrastructure) serves *multiple* tenants (customers/organizations), with each tenant's data and configuration logically isolated.

**Two isolation mechanisms:**
1. **Compute isolation** — via separate VMs or container namespaces with dedicated vCPUs/RAM.
2. **Network isolation** — via VLANs, Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs), or Security Groups.
3. *(Optional third)* Storage isolation via LUNs or per-tenant encryption keys.

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Explain the three core strategies of cloud resource management — **Pooling, Sharing, and Provisioning** — with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Resource Pooling (2.5 marks):**
   - Definition: Consolidation of heterogeneous physical resources (servers, storage, network) into logical pools managed by virtualization layer.
   - Mechanism: Hypervisors (VMware ESXi, KVM) abstract hardware; resources grouped by type — compute pool, storage pool, network pool.
   - Example: 1000 physical hosts consolidated into one compute pool managed by OpenStack Nova.

2. **Resource Sharing (2.5 marks):**
   - Definition: Multiple tenants simultaneously access the same physical pool with logical isolation.
   - Multi-tenancy model: One application instance serves many tenants.
   - Isolation: VLANs (network), quotas (storage), namespaces (compute).

3. **Resource Provisioning (2 marks):**
   - Definition: Dynamic allocation of pool resources to tenant workloads on demand.
   - Modes: Static (manual, fixed) vs Dynamic (auto-scaling).
   - Workflow: Request → Scheduler → Placement → VM creation → Access provisioning.

---

**(b)** A cloud data center has **128 physical CPU cores** running an **over-commitment ratio of 1.5:1**. The pool reports **75% utilization**.  
**(i)** Calculate the total provisionable vCPUs.  
**(ii)** Calculate the actively allocated vCPUs.  
**(iii)** If the auto-scale threshold is **80%**, will scaling trigger? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Provisionable vCPUs (2 marks):**

$$V_{prov} = R_{oc} \times P_{phys} = 1.5 \times 128 = 192 \text{ vCPUs}$$

**(ii) Actively allocated vCPUs (2 marks):**

$$V_{active} = U \times V_{prov} = 0.75 \times 192 = 144 \text{ vCPUs}$$

**(iii) Auto-scale decision (3 marks):**

$$U_{current} = 75\% \quad < \quad \theta = 80\%$$

Since current utilization (**75%**) is **below** the threshold (**80%**), the auto-scaler will **NOT trigger**. The system is comfortably below the scale-out trigger; resources are sufficient for the current load.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Discuss the **challenges of resource sharing** in multi-tenant cloud environments. How does the **NIST cloud deployment model** influence the sharing strategy? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Challenges of Resource Sharing (4 marks):**
1. **Security & Privacy** — Co-tenant data leakage via side-channel attacks, shared memory vulnerabilities (e.g., Meltdown, Spectre).
2. **Performance Interference** — Noisy-neighbor effect: one tenant's heavy workload degrades another's performance.
3. **Fairness** — Ensuring equitable allocation via schedulers and quotas.
4. **Compliance** — Regulatory requirements (GDPR, HIPAA) may restrict sharing.
5. **Resource Contention** — Race conditions on shared CPU caches, disk I/O.

**Influence of NIST Deployment Models (3 marks):**
- **Public Cloud:** Maximum sharing; aggressive multi-tenancy; lowest cost; trust boundary at hypervisor.
- **Private Cloud:** Limited sharing; often single-organization; stronger isolation; higher cost.
- **Hybrid Cloud:** Selective sharing — sensitive workloads in private; burst capacity in public.
- **Community Cloud:** Sharing limited to a closed group of organizations with common compliance needs.

---

**(b)** A cloud consumer requires **99.99% availability** for a mission-critical application.  
**(i)** Calculate the maximum permitted annual downtime.  
**(ii)** If the actual downtime recorded in a year was **52 minutes**, did the provider meet the SLA? Show calculation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
