## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**State and explain Brewer's CAP theorem with a suitable diagram. Why is partition tolerance considered mandatory in cloud environments? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Brewer's CAP theorem, formally proved by Seth Gilbert and Nancy Lynch (2002), states that in any distributed data store, **only two of the three** properties can be guaranteed simultaneously:

- **C — Consistency:** Every read receives the most recent write or an error.
- **A — Availability:** Every request receives a non-error response (no guarantee of recency).
- **P — Partition Tolerance:** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes.

In cloud environments spanning multiple data centers across continents, **network partitions are inevitable** due to fiber cuts, BGP failures, or hardware faults. Therefore, designers must choose between **CP** (e.g., HDFS, MongoDB) and **AP** (e.g., Cassandra, DynamoDB). The "CA" choice is only viable in single-site deployments, which defeats the purpose of cloud distribution.

*(Examiner Note: 1 mark for stating the theorem, 1 mark for explaining the trade-off, 1 mark for justifying P as mandatory.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between strong consistency, eventual consistency, and causal consistency with one real-world example each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Model | Guarantee | Example System |
|-------|-----------|----------------|
| **Strong Consistency** | A read reflects the most recent write — linearizability. | Google Spanner (used in Google Ads billing) |
| **Causal Consistency** | Operations causally related are seen in order; concurrent ops may appear in any order. | Cosmos DB (default session) |
| **Eventual Consistency** | Given sufficient time with no new writes, all replicas converge. | Amazon DynamoDB (shopping cart) |

Strong consistency offers the easiest programming model but suffers the highest latency. Eventual consistency maximizes availability and throughput but requires the application to tolerate stale reads — for example, an e-commerce cart may briefly show a stale item count that converges within milliseconds.

*(Examiner Note: 1 mark per correct pair of definition + example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Distributed Storage Deep Dive**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **major challenges of cloud storage systems** with respect to scalability, consistency, and fault tolerance. Describe how **HDFS** addresses these challenges through its block-based architecture and replication strategy. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Scalability Challenge:** Single-machine file systems hit hard limits on capacity, throughput, and metadata operations. As data grows to petabytes/exabytes, vertical scaling becomes economically infeasible.

**2. Consistency Challenge:** When data is replicated across geographically dispersed nodes, concurrent updates may yield conflicting values. Maintaining ACID-like guarantees over WAN introduces unacceptable latency.

**3. Fault Tolerance Challenge:** Commodity hardware fails frequently (industry studies show ~1–2% annual disk failure rate per drive). With thousands of disks, simultaneous failures are statistical certainties.

**How HDFS Solves These:**

- **Block-based partitioning:** Files are split into fixed-size blocks (default **128 MB**), allowing horizontal distribution across DataNodes. This decouples file size from single-node capacity.
- **Master-slave architecture:** A **NameNode** maintains metadata (namespace, block-to-DataNode mapping); **DataNodes** store the actual blocks. This separates control from data plane.
- **Replication:** Each block is stored on **multiple DataNodes** (default $RF = 3$), placed on different racks to tolerate rack-level failures.
- **Heartbeats & re-replication:** DataNodes send heartbeats every 3 seconds; failure triggers automatic replication by the NameNode to restore the replication factor.
- **Write-once-read-many model:** Simplifies consistency — once a file is created, it is not modified, eliminating complex concurrency control.

*(Examiner Note: 3 marks for challenge identification, 4 marks for HDFS architectural mapping.)*

---

**(b)** A cloud provider stores **$N = 5{,}000{,}000$** blocks of average size **$S = 256$ MB** with a **replication factor $RF = 3$** across three zones, each having independent availability $A = 0.999$.
**(i)** Compute the total physical storage.
**(ii)** Compute the system-level availability assuming the service is available if at least one zone responds.
**(iii)** Compute the maximum allowed annual downtime in minutes for the system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Total physical storage:**

$$C_{repl} = N \times S \times RF$$

$$C_{repl} = 5 \times 10^{6} \times 256 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 3.84 \times 10^{9} \text{ MB}$$

Converting to TB using $1 \text{ TB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ MB}$:

$$C_{repl} = \dfrac{3.84 \times 10^{9}}{1.048576 \times 10^{6}} \approx 3662.11 \text{ TB} \approx 3.66 \text{ PB}$$

**(ii) System-level availability (parallel zones):**
Unavailability per zone: $U = 1 - 0.999 = 10^{-3}$.
Joint unavailability (all three fail):

$$U_{sys} = (10^{-3})^{3} = 10^{-9}$$

$$A_{sys} = 1 - 10^{-9} \approx 0.999999999 \;\; (\text{Nine-9s})$$

**(iii) Maximum annual downtime:**

$$T_{down} = (1 - A_{sys}) \times 525{,}600 = 10^{-9} \times 525{,}600$$

$$T_{down} = 5.256 \times 10^{-4} \text{ minutes} \approx 31.5 \text{ milliseconds per year}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Forgetting to multiply by $RF$ in (i):** Lose **2 marks**.
> - **Using $A_{sys} = A \times 3$ instead of complement rule in (ii):** Lose **2 marks** — parallel reliability uses $(1-U_1)(1-U_2)\cdots$ for *survival*, not multiplication for *failure*.
> - **Using a non-leap year minutes value other than 525,600 in (iii):** Lose **1 mark**.
> - **Showing no units in final answers:** Lose **1 mark** cumulatively.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Track: CAP & Consistency Models**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **CAP theorem** and classify the following systems: **HDFS, Amazon DynamoDB, Apache Cassandra, Google Spanner, MongoDB.** Justify each classification. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000):**
In any distributed data store subjected to network partitions, the designer must choose between **Consistency (C)** and **Availability (A)**. **Partition Tolerance (P)** is non-negotiable in real WAN-scale clouds.

| System | CAP Class | Justification |
|--------|-----------|---------------|
| **HDFS** | **CP** | Strong consistency (single writer per file); rejects writes during NameNode failure to preserve data integrity. |
| **DynamoDB** | **AP** | Uses vector clocks and sloppy quorums; prioritizes always-accepting writes, eventual reconciliation. |
| **Cassandra** | **AP** | Tunable consistency, but default mode is AP with last-write-wins and gossip-based replication. |
| **Spanner** | **CP** | Uses TrueTime API and Paxos for externally consistent global transactions; sacrifices some write latency. |
| **MongoDB** | **CP** | Single-master replica set; writes go to primary, secondaries catch up; read failures possible during failover. |

*(Examiner Note: 3 marks for CAP explanation + diagram, 4 marks for correct classification with justification.)*

---

**(b)** Compare **strong, eventual, and causal consistency models** in detail. For an e-commerce shopping cart deployed on AWS, recommend a model and justify with a minimum of **three engineering reasons**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Strong | Eventual | Causal |
|--------|--------|----------|--------|
| **Read freshness** | Latest write | Possibly stale | Ordered if causally related |
| **Write latency** | High (quorum sync) | Low (async) | Medium |
| **Availability** | Lower | Highest | Medium-High |
| **Implementation cost** | High (Paxos/Raft) | Low (async replicate) | Medium (dependency tracking) |

**Recommendation: Eventual Consistency with Session Guarantees (Read-your-writes).**

**Engineering Justifications:**

1. **User experience priority:** A user adding an item to their cart expects it to appear *immediately*. Synchronous quorum writes across regions would add 100–300 ms latency — a known cause of cart abandonment. Eventual consistency delivers sub-50 ms perceived latency.

2. **Cost economics:** Strong consistency requires multi-region synchronous writes ($RF \geq 3$ across continents), which multiplies write cost by 3–5×. Eventual consistency uses async replication, drastically reducing per-transaction cost.

3. **High availability during regional failure:** If a region's data center fails, a strongly consistent system would reject cart operations. Eventual consistency lets users continue shopping; reconciliation occurs when the region recovers.

4. **Workload characteristics:** Cart data is **session-local** — there is no global invariant requiring immediate cross-user consistency. Eventual convergence within 1–2 seconds is operationally invisible to users.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> - **Drawing a Venn diagram of CAP with overlapping circles instead of a triangle:** Lose **1 mark** — KTU 2024 Scheme expects the *triangle* representation since it conveys the "pick two" trade-off geometrically.
> - **Recommending strong consistency for the cart without justification:** Lose **3 marks** — this is the *wrong* answer; cart workloads are the canonical AP use case.
> - **Failing to mention cost, latency, OR availability trade-off:** Lose at least **2 marks** — the question demands a *justified* recommendation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PECST635 Module 3 — Storage Challenges:**

- ✅ **CAP Theorem:** State all three properties; identify P as mandatory; classify any system as CP or AP.
- ✅ **Consistency Models:** Memorize strong, causal, eventual, read-your-writes — and one real-world example for each.
- ✅ **HDFS Architecture:** NameNode (master) + DataNodes (slaves), default block size **128 MB**, default $RF = 3$.
- ✅ **GFS vs HDFS:** Both are CP, both use chunks/blocks, both support append-only workloads.
- ✅ **Replication Overhead Formula:** $C_{repl} = N_{blocks} \times S_{avg} \times RF$ — *always* multiply by $RF$.
- ✅ **Parallel Availability Formula:** $A_{sys} = 1 - \prod(1 - A_i)$ — *complement* of product of unavailabilities.
- ✅ **SLA Downtime:** Four-9s = 52.56 min/yr; Five-9s = 5.256 min/yr — memorize the **52,560 minutes-per-decade** trick.
- ✅ **Storage Tiers:** Hot (SSD), Warm (HDD), Cold (Tape/Glacier) — matched to access frequency.
- ✅ **Noisy Neighbor:** Multi-tenant resource contention; mitigated by QoS, cgroups, namespace isolation.
- ✅ **At-rest vs In-transit Encryption:** AES-256 for stored data, TLS 1.3 for network data.
- ✅ **CAP Choice for Cloud Storage:** **AP dominates** (DynamoDB, S3) for user-facing data; **CP dominates** (HDFS, Spanner) for analytical & financial workloads.
- ✅ **Draw CAP triangle, not Venn diagram** — KTU 2024 expects the geometric triangle for full marks.
- ✅ **HDFS rack awareness:** Replicas placed on *different* racks to tolerate switch failure.
- ✅ **Eventual convergence time:** Typically **< 1 second** in well-tuned DynamoDB deployments.
- ✅ **Mention "Consensus Algorithms" (Paxos/Raft)** when explaining fault tolerance — examiners reward this term.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Native File System, Deployment Models, Storage Types, Popular Cloud Storages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST635 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Cloud Native File System, Deployment Models, Storage Types, Popular Cloud Storages # Module 3 — Cloud Storage Foundations ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Cloud Native File System (CNFS) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):** > A **Cloud Native File System** is a distributed, horizontally scalable, software-defined storage architecture specifically engineered to run on commodity cloud infrastructure. It decouples storage control plane logic from the underlying physical hardware using micro-services, containerization, and an API-first design philosophy. **Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Postal Locker"** Imagine a huge apartment complex with thousands of residents. Instead of each resident having a private letterbox at the gate (a traditional file system bound to a single server), the building installs a giant automated locker bank. Every locker has an address (a unique URI/object key), anyone can drop or pick up a parcel (data blob) at any time using a barcode (REST API), and the system itself decides where to physically store the box (data placement, replication, sharding). This locker bank is your **Cloud Native File System** — addressable, elastic, location-independent, and accessible from anywhere on Earth via the internet. > [!NOTE] > **Key Characteristics of a Cloud Native File System (NCFS)** > 1. **Stateless Control Plane** — Metadata services can be restarted without data loss. > 2. **Erasure Coding & Replication** — Built-in redundancy across failure domains. > 3. **API-Driven Access** — REST, gRPC, or S3-compatible interfaces (no kernel-level mount required). > 4. **Multi-Tenancy** — Logical isolation between tenants sharing the same physical cluster. > 5. **Elastic Scalability** — Capacity grows from **GB** to **EB** without re-architecture. > 6. **Self-Healing** — Automatic re-replication when nodes fail. --- ### 1.2 Cloud Deployment Models > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** > A *Deployment Model* describes **who owns**, **who manages**, **who accesses**, and **where the physical infrastructure** of a cloud environment is located. The NIST SP 800-145 reference architecture identifies four canonical models; modern industry practice extends this with **Multi-Cloud** and **Community Cloud**. | Deployment Model | Owner | Location | Typical User | KTU Buzzwords | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Public Cloud** | Third-party provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) | Provider's data centers | General public | Pay-per-use, Multi-tenant | | **Private Cloud** | Single organization | On-premise or hosted | Internal employees | Dedicated, Customizable | | **Hybrid Cloud** | Mixed (Org + Provider) | Both | Internal + external burst | Cloud bursting, Portability | | **Community Cloud** | Several organizations | Shared or provider-hosted | Organizations with shared goals | Govt, Banking consortiums | | **Multi-Cloud** | Multiple providers | Distributed across vendors | Any size | Vendor lock-in avoidance | **Intuition — The "Housing" Analogy** - **Public Cloud** → Renting a flat in a busy apartment block. Cheap, fully managed, but you share walls and amenities. - **Private Cloud** → Owning a standalone villa. Full control, higher cost, you manage everything. - **Hybrid Cloud** → Owning a villa but also renting a vacation flat. Sensitive work at home, seasonal workloads on rent. - **Community Cloud** → A co-operative housing society built by multiple families sharing common infrastructure costs. - **Multi-Cloud** → Owning property in two different cities (AWS in Mumbai + GCP in Singapore) for redundancy and best-of-breed services. --- ### 1.3 Cloud Storage Types > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** > Cloud Storage Type refers to the **logical organization**, **access protocol**, and **performance profile** used by the storage layer. The three primary types are **Object**, **Block**, and **File** storage. Emerging fourth categories include **Archive** and **Database-as-a-Service**. **Intuition — The "Warehouse, Container Yard, and Filing Cabinet" Analogy** - **Object Storage** → A massive, automated **warehouse**. Each item is sealed in a box with a tracking label (metadata + key). You cannot easily walk between boxes; you must request retrieval by label. Ideal for unstructured data. - **Block Storage** → A **shipping container yard**. The yard is split into uniform steel containers (blocks). Each container can be mounted onto any truck (VM). High performance, low-latency. - **File Storage** → A traditional **office filing cabinet** with folders and sub-folders, accessible over a network share (NFS/SMB). Familiar hierarchical model. --- ### 1.4 Popular Cloud Storages (Industry Reference List) > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Vendor Snapshot — Must remember for KTU ESE** > - **AWS:** S3 (Object), EBS (Block), EFS (File), Glacier (Archive) > - **Azure:** Blob Storage (Object), Disk Storage (Block), Files (File), Archive Storage > - **Google Cloud:** Cloud Storage (Object), Persistent Disk (Block), Filestore (File), Coldline (Archive) > - **Open Source:** MinIO (S3-compatible object), Ceph (unified block/object/file), GlusterFS # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Cloud Native File System A CNFS is decomposed into four logical planes: 1. **Data Plane** — Responsible for actual read/write of bytes. Stateless daemons run on every storage node (e.g., OSDs in Ceph, chunkservers in GFS/HDFS). 2. **Metadata Plane** — Manages file-to-block mappings, directory hierarchies, permissions, and quotas. Usually implemented as a distributed key-value store (etcd, ZooKeeper). 3. **Control Plane** — Orchestrates placement, rebalancing, and replication policies. 4. **Management Plane** — Exposes dashboards, REST APIs, billing, and policy engines. ### Why "Cloud Native"? The term *Cloud Native* is reserved for systems that explicitly embrace: - **Containerization** (runs inside Docker/Kubernetes pods) - **Declarative configuration** (YAML/CRDs instead of imperative scripts) - **Immutable infrastructure** (nodes are replaced, not patched) - **GitOps-driven operations** (state stored in version control) --- ## 2.2 Comparative Analysis — Deployment Models | Criterion | Public | Private | Hybrid | Community | Multi-Cloud | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **CapEx** | Very Low | Very High | High | Moderate | High | | **OpEx** | High (subscription) | Low–Moderate | Moderate | Shared | High | | **Scalability** | **Unlimited (elastic)** | Limited by hardware | Elastic (burst) | Moderate | Unlimited | | **Security Control** | Lowest | Highest | High (in private leg) | High | Provider-dependent | | **Regulatory Compliance** | Tricky (data residency) | Easiest | Customizable | Shared charter | Most complex | | **Vendor Lock-in Risk** | High | None | Medium | Low | **Lowest** | | **Disaster Recovery** | Provider-managed | Org-managed | Best of both | Shared SLA | Highest resilience | --- ## 2.3 Comparative Analysis — Storage Types | Property | Block Storage | File Storage | Object Storage | |---|---|---|---| | **Data Unit** | Fixed-size block (typically **512 B – 64 KB**) | File (variable, hierarchical) | Object (blob + metadata + key) | | **Access Protocol** | iSCSI, FC, NVMe-oF | NFS v4, SMB 3.0 | REST (HTTP/S), S3 API | | **Typical Latency** | **< 1 ms** (sub-ms with NVMe) | 1–10 ms | 30–200 ms | | **Throughput** | Very High | Moderate | High (parallel reads) | | **Scalability Limit** | Thousands of volumes | Tens of PB | **Exabytes** | | **Best Use Case** | Databases, boot disks | Home directories, CMS, NFS workloads | Backups, media, logs, big data | | **Mutability** | Mutable (RWX) | Mutable | Typically **Immutable** (write-once) | | **Search Capability** | No | Path-based | Metadata + tags | --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Storage Metrics) > [!NOTE] > **Quantitative Metrics Every KTU Student MUST Know** | Metric | Formula | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Storage Capacity** | $C = N \times S_d$ | TB or PB | Total raw capacity | | **Usable Capacity (with replication factor r)** | $C_{usable} = \dfrac{C}{r}$ | TB | Capacity after redundancy | | **Erasure-Coded Efficiency** | $E_{ec} = \dfrac{k}{k+m}$ | dimensionless | Where $k$ = data shards, $m$ = parity shards | | **Storage Cost (Monthly)** | $\text{Cost} = C_{stored} \times P_{perGB}$ | ₹ / $ | TCO calculation | | **IOPS Requirement** | $IOPS_{total} = IOPS_{perVM} \times N_{VM}$ | ops/sec | Block storage sizing | | **Throughput** | $T = IOPS \times \dfrac{B}{1024}$ | MB/s | Network & disk planning | | **Data Transfer Cost** | $C_{transfer} = D_{out} \times P_{egress}$ | ₹ / $ | Cross-AZ / Internet cost | | **Availability (SLA)** | Downtime/year $= (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ | minutes | **99.9 %** → 525.6 min/yr; **99.99 %** → 52.56 min/yr | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Condition for Erasure Coding** > A Reed–Solomon $(k, m)$ code can tolerate any **m** simultaneous node failures out of **k + m** total nodes. So an **RS(10, 4)** scheme yields $E_{ec} = \dfrac{10}{14} \approx 71.4\%$ usable efficiency versus **33.3%** for triple replication ($r=3$). --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Object Storage + CDN** powers Netflix, Instagram, and Hotstar's image/video delivery. - **Block Storage** underpins every relational database (Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL) deployed on cloud VMs. - **Hybrid Cloud + Object Tiering** allows hospitals to keep PHI in private clouds and push anonymized analytics to public clouds — a common **HIPAA-aligned** architecture. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Numerical — Storage Cost & Availability **Problem (KTU Numerical Style):** A startup wants to store **120 TB** of medical images on AWS S3 Standard in the *ap-south-1* region. - Replication factor used internally: **3** - Cost per GB-month (S3 Standard): **₹ 2.3** - SLA provided by AWS for S3 Standard: **99.9 %** - Additionally, the company provisions an **EBS gp3** volume of **500 GB** for their PostgreSQL DB. - gp3 cost: **₹ 8.0 per GB-month**, provisioned IOPS = **3000**, throughput = **125 MB/s**. Compute: 1. Usable capacity after replication overhead. 2. Monthly storage cost for S3. 3. Maximum allowable downtime per year from the SLA. 4. Expected IOPS if the DB workload is served by 4 EC2 instances each demanding 800 IOPS. --- ### Solution — Step 1: Usable Capacity $$ C_{usable} = \frac{C}{r} = \frac{120 \text{ TB}}{3} = 40 \text{ TB} $$ > **Logic:** Raw 120 TB must be stored three times; the *effective* user-addressable storage is one-third of the raw footprint. ### Solution — Step 2: Monthly S3 Cost First, convert TB to GB. $$ 120 \text{ TB} = 120 \times 1024 \text{ GB} = 122{,}880 \text{ GB} $$ $$ \text{Cost}_{S3} = 122{,}880 \text{ GB} \times ₹2.3 = ₹2{,}82{,}624 \text{ / month} $$ > **Logic:** S3 bills per GB-month irrespective of replication factor (replication is internal to AWS). ### Solution — Step 3: SLA Downtime $$ D_{year} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600 $$ $$ D_{year} = (1 - 0.999) \times 525{,}600 = 0.001 \times 525{,}600 = 525.6 \text{ minutes/year} $$ > **Logic:** 99.9 % (three nines) SLA allows 525.6 minutes (~8.76 hours) of downtime per year. This is why mission-critical workloads demand **99.99 % (52.56 min)** or **99.999 % (5.26 min)** tiers. ### Solution — Step 4: Aggregate IOPS Demand $$ IOPS_{total} = IOPS_{perVM} \times N_{VM} = 800 \times 4 = 3{,}200 \text{ IOPS} $$ $$ \text{Provisioned IOPS on gp3} = 3{,}000 $$ > **Conclusion:** The provisioned volume is **short by 200 IOPS**. The engineer must either upgrade to gp3 with **3,200 IOPS** provisioned or switch to **io2 Block Express**, which scales to 256,000 IOPS per volume. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Storage | Workload | Best Storage Type | Justification (Math/Logic) | |---|---|---| | OLTP Database (MySQL, PostgreSQL) | **Block** | Needs sub-ms latency: $Latency \propto \dfrac{1}{IOPS}$; block delivers $>10^4$ IOPS | | Big Data Lake (Hadoop, Spark) | **Object** | Linear scalability: $Throughput \propto N_{nodes}$; EC code $(10,4)$ tolerates 4 racks failure | | Home Directories, CMS | **File** | Hierarchical POSIX semantics: path-based `readdir()` performance is O(log n) | | VM Boot Disk | **Block** | Requires low-latency random RW; uses ext4/xfs directly | | Disaster Recovery Backup | **Object + Archive Tier** | Glacier costs ₹0.06/GB-month (≈40× cheaper) | | Video Streaming | **Object + CDN** | Parallel GET requests + edge caching → $T_{effective} \uparrow$ | --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Erasure Coding Efficiency Given a Ceph cluster with $k = 12$ data shards and $m = 4$ parity shards. $$ E_{ec} = \frac{k}{k+m} = \frac{12}{12+4} = \frac{12}{16} = 0.75 \; (75\%) $$ $$ \text{Storage overhead} = 1 - E_{ec} = 0.25 \; (25\%) $$ > **Comparison vs Replication ($r=3$):** > - EC overhead = **25%** > - Replication overhead = **200%** (i.e., 3× raw) > - Storage savings switching from $r=3$ to EC(12,4) = **175%** $$ \text{Reduction Ratio} = \frac{200\%}{25\%} = 8\times \text{ storage efficiency} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trade-off:** EC saves storage but requires **k + m** nodes to reconstruct any single block, increasing CPU and network rebuild load. This is why hot data still uses replication and cold data uses erasure coding. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cloud Storage Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ClientTier["CLIENT TIER"] vm1["Virtual Machine 1"] vm2["Container Pod A"] vm3["Web Browser"] vm4["Mobile App"] end subgraph APIlayer["API GATEWAY / SDK LAYER"] restapi["REST S3 API"] nfsapi["NFS v4 Endpoint"] iscsiapi["iSCSI / NVMe-oF Target"] end subgraph ControlPlane["CONTROL & METADATA PLANE"] md["Metadata Service - etcd"] policy["Policy Engine - IAM / Bucket Policy"] placement["Data Placement Scheduler"] end subgraph DataPlane["DATA PLANE - STORAGE NODES"] node1["Storage Node 1 - OSD - 4TB NVMe"] node2["Storage Node 2 - OSD - 4TB HDD"] node3["Storage Node 3 - OSD - 8TB HDD"] node4["Storage Node 4 - OSD - 8TB NVMe"] end subgraph ReplicationLayer["REDUNDANCY LAYER"] ec["Erasure Coding Engine - RS 10 4"] rep["Replication Manager - r equals 3"] end vm1 --> restapi vm2 --> restapi vm3 --> restapi vm4 --> restapi vm1 --> iscsiapi vm2 --> nfsapi restapi --> policy nfsapi --> policy iscsiapi --> policy policy --> placement placement --> md md --> ec md --> rep ec --> node1 ec --> node2 ec --> node3 ec --> node4 rep --> node1 rep --> node2 rep --> node3 rep --> node4 ``` --- ## 4.2 Deployment Models Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Need a Cloud Deployment?"] --> q1{"Is the data
regulated
HIPAA / RBI?"} q1 -- Yes --> q2{"Budget > 1 Cr?"} q1 -- No --> q3{"Need elasticity
and pay-per-use?"} q2 -- Yes --> private["Private Cloud
On-Premise OpenStack"] q2 -- No --> hybrid["Hybrid Cloud
Sensitive in Private
Burst to Public"] q3 -- Yes --> q4{"Multiple vendors
for resilience?"} q3 -- No --> onprem["Traditional On-Premise DC"] q4 -- Yes --> multi["Multi-Cloud
AWS plus Azure plus GCP"] q4 -- No --> public["Public Cloud
AWS or Azure or GCP"] start --> community{"Shared mission
with peer orgs?"} community -- Yes --> communitycloud["Community Cloud
e-Governance / Banking Consortium"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Storage Type Comparison Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR app["Application Workload"] --> q1{"Need sub-millisecond
latency?"} q1 -- Yes --> block["Block Storage
EBS - Disk Storage - Persistent Disk
Protocol: iSCSI / NVMe-oF"] q1 -- No --> q2{"Data volume
greater than 10 PB?"} q2 -- Yes --> object["Object Storage
S3 - Blob - GCS
Protocol: REST API"] q2 -- No --> q3{"Need POSIX
hierarchical FS?"} q3 -- Yes --> file["File Storage
EFS - Azure Files - Filestore
Protocol: NFS / SMB"] q3 -- No --> object block --> usecase1["OLTP DB - VM Boot Disk"] object --> usecase2["Data Lake - Backup - Media - Logs"] file --> usecase3["CMS - Home Directories - HPC Scratch"] ``` --- ## 4.4 CNFS Request Lifecycle — Sequence View ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant ClientApp as Client Application participant APIGateway as API Gateway participant AuthZ as AuthN / AuthZ Layer participant MDS as Metadata Service participant Scheduler as Placement Scheduler participant StorageA as Storage Node A participant StorageB as Storage Node B participant StorageC as Storage Node C ClientApp->>APIGateway: PUT /bucket/img.jpg APIGateway->>AuthZ: Validate JWT / IAM token AuthZ-->>APIGateway: 200 OK - Authorized APIGateway->>MDS: Request new object key MDS-->>APIGateway: UUID-1234 APIGateway->>Scheduler: Find placement for UUID-1234 Scheduler-->>APIGateway: Place on Node A B C with EC 10 4 APIGateway->>StorageA: Write shard 1 APIGateway->>StorageB: Write shard 2 APIGateway->>StorageC: Write shard 3 StorageA-->>APIGateway: ACK StorageB-->>APIGateway: ACK StorageC-->>APIGateway: ACK APIGateway-->>ClientApp: HTTP 200 - ETag returned ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** **Differentiate between Block, File, and Object storage in cloud environments. List one ideal use case for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 60–80 words expected):** | Type | Characteristics | Ideal Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Block** | Splits data into fixed-size blocks; accessed via iSCSI/NVMe-oF; lowest latency (<1 ms); mounted as raw volume | Relational database boot disk (e.g., MySQL on AWS EBS) | | **File** | Hierarchical directory tree; NFS/SMB protocol; shared access; supports POSIX semantics | Shared home directories for engineering teams | | **Object** | Flat namespace; data + metadata + unique key; REST API; virtually unlimited scalability; immutable | Unstructured media storage for a video streaming platform | > **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark each for the differentiation column. Partial credit for naming correct protocols even if use case is slightly off. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]** **Explain any three characteristics of a Cloud Native File System.** **Model Answer:** 1. **API-First Design** — All operations (read/write/list) are exposed via REST/gRPC; no kernel-level mount dependency. 2. **Horizontal Scalability** — Adding a new node linearly increases capacity and throughput without downtime. 3. **Built-in Redundancy** — Automatic replication ($r \geq 3$) or Erasure Coding (e.g., RS(10,4)) provides fault tolerance against node/rack failures. 4. **Self-Healing** — When a node fails, the system automatically reconstructs missing shards on healthy nodes in the background. 5. **Multi-Tenancy** — Logical isolation of tenants through namespaces, quotas, and IAM policies. > **Valuation Tip:** Examiner expects **any 3** from the above. Award full marks if each characteristic is defined and exemplified. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### ⭐ **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]** #### **(a)** Compare the four standard cloud deployment models (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community) on the basis of ownership, scalability, cost, and security. *(7 marks)* #### **(b)** A fintech company must store 500 TB of transaction logs that are written once and rarely read. They require 11-nines durability and minimum cost. Recommend the most suitable storage type, deployment model, and redundancy strategy. Justify with calculations. *(7 marks)* --- ### Model Solution for Q-A (a) **Comparison Table (4 marks for table, 3 marks for narrative justification):** | Aspect | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud | Community Cloud | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Ownership** | Third-party provider (AWS, Azure) | Single organization | Mixed (org + provider) | Several organizations | | **Scalability** | Elastic, virtually unlimited | Limited by on-prem capacity | Bursts to public when needed | Shared capacity, moderate | | **Cost Model** | OpEx (pay-per-use) | CapEx heavy | Mixed | Cost-shared OpEx | | **Security** | Provider-managed, lower control | Highest, fully controlled | High for private leg | High, governed by consortium | | **Example** | AWS EC2 | OpenStack on-prem | AWS Outposts + Azure Stack | MeghRaj (Govt of India) | **Narrative Justification (3 marks):** - A startup with unpredictable load prefers **Public Cloud** for elasticity. - A bank handling RBI-regulated data prefers **Private/Hybrid** for compliance. - A consortium of state governments builds a **Community Cloud** (MeghRaj) to share e-governance workloads. --- ### Model Solution for Q-A (b) **Step 1 — Identify Storage Type (2 marks):** The workload is **write-once, read-rarely** → this is the textbook definition of an **Archive Use Case**. The best fit is **Object Storage in Archive Tier**. - AWS: **S3 Glacier Deep Archive** - Azure: **Archive Blob Storage** - GCP: **Archive Storage** **Step 2 — Identify Deployment Model (1 mark):** For a fintech with regulatory constraints, a **Hybrid Cloud** is recommended. Active logs reside in **private cloud** for compliance; cold archive copies move to **public cloud Glacier** to leverage low-cost durability. **Step 3 — Identify Redundancy Strategy (2 marks):** Use **Erasure Coding RS(14, 4)** which provides: $$ \text{Durability} = 1 - \left(\frac{1}{2^{14}}\right) \approx 99.999999999994\% \quad (\text{14 nines}) $$ Even **S3 Standard provides 11 nines (99.999999999%)** natively. Glacier Deep Archive matches the requirement. **Step 4 — Cost Calculation (2 marks):** Convert to GB: $$ 500 \text{ TB} = 500 \times 1024 \text{ GB} = 512{,}000 \text{ GB} $$ Glacier Deep Archive pricing ≈ **₹0.06/GB-month** (illustrative): $$ \text{Monthly Cost} = 512{,}000 \times 0.06 = ₹30{,}720 $$ > **Conclusion:** Storing the same 500 TB on S3 Standard (₹2.3/GB-month) would cost ₹11,77,600 — **~38× more expensive**. --- ### ⭐ **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]** #### **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Cloud Native File System. Describe the role of the metadata service, data placement scheduler, and erasure coding engine. *(7 marks)* #### **(b)** Calculate the following for a Ceph cluster configured with **RS(6, 3)** storing **240 TB** of raw data: - (i) Usable storage - (ii) Storage overhead percentage - (iii) Number of simultaneous node failures the cluster can tolerate - (iv) Cost per usable TB if the hardware cost is ₹50,00,000. *(7 marks)* --- ### Model Solution for Q-B (a) **Architecture Diagram (3 marks):** ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ CLIENT APPLICATIONS (VMs/Pods) │ └────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘ │ REST / gRPC ┌────────────────────▼─────────────────────┐ │ API GATEWAY + AUTHN/AUTHZ │ └────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘ │ ┌────────────────────▼─────────────────────┐ │ METADATA SERVICE (etcd) + PLACEMENT │ │ SCHEDULER + POLICY ENGINE │ └────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘ │ ┌────────────────────▼─────────────────────┐ │ ERASURE CODING ENGINE (Reed-Solomon) │ └────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘ │ ┌──────────┬─────────┴─────────┬──────────┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ Node 1 Node 2 Node 3 Node 4 (OSDs) ``` **Role Descriptions (4 marks):** 1. **Metadata Service (MDS)** — Maintains directory tree, file-to-block mappings, permissions. Stateless and replicated via Raft/Paxos consensus (e.g., Ceph MDS uses Paxos). 2. **Data Placement Scheduler** — Decides which OSD receives a new shard based on CRUSH map, disk utilization, and failure-domain policies. 3. **Erasure Coding Engine** — Splits each object into $k$ data shards and computes $m$ parity shards using Reed–Solomon polynomial arithmetic; reconstructs lost shards on demand. --- ### Model Solution for Q-B (b) **Given:** $k = 6$, $m = 3$, Raw $C = 240$ TB, Hardware cost = ₹50,00,000. **(i) Usable Storage:** $$ C_{usable} = C \times \frac{k}{k+m} = 240 \times \frac{6}{9} = 240 \times 0.6\overline{6} = 160 \text{ TB} $$ **(ii) Storage Overhead %:** $$ \text{Overhead} = \left(1 - \frac{6}{9}\right) \times 100\% = 33.33\% $$ **(iii) Tolerable Failures:** The cluster can tolerate any **m = 3** simultaneous node failures out of **9 total**. **(iv) Cost per Usable TB:** $$ \text{Cost per TB} = \frac{₹50,00,000}{160 \text{ TB}} = ₹31{,}250 \text{ per TB} $$ --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing deployment models with service models** — IaaS/PaaS/SaaS are *service* models, NOT deployment models. Do not mix them up. > 2. **Wrong unit conversion** — Cloud billing uses **1 TB = 1024 GB** (binary) or **1 TB = 1000 GB** (decimal). Always state which one you are using. > 3. **Forgetting availability math** — Many students write "99.9%" without computing the actual downtime. Examiners love numerical translation. > 4. **Missing redundancy justification** — Always specify *why* you chose replication (hot data) vs erasure coding (cold data). > 5. **Block storage ≠ SAN** — Modern cloud block is virtualized (EBS) and decoupled from physical SAN arrays. > 6. **In CNFS questions**, examiners expect you to mention *control plane vs data plane separation* — a frequent 2-mark question. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam** - ☑️ A **Cloud Native File System** is software-defined, API-driven, containerized, and horizontally scalable. - ☑️ Four primary **deployment models** (NIST): Public, Private, Hybrid, Community; **Multi-Cloud** is the modern extension. - ☑️ Three primary **storage types**: Block (low latency), File (POSIX), Object (unlimited scale). - ☑️ **Object storage** is immutable and accessed via REST/S3 API. - ☑️ **Block storage** is mounted as a raw device; best for databases. - ☑️ **File storage** uses NFS/SMB; best for shared hierarchical access. - ☑️ **Replication factor r=3** means storage overhead is 200%. - ☑️ **Erasure Coding (k,m)** efficiency = $k/(k+m)$; tolerates **m** simultaneous failures. - ☑️ **SLA Downtime Formula:** $D_{year} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ minutes. - ☑️ **AWS S3** = Object, **EBS** = Block, **EFS** = File, **Glacier** = Archive. - ☑️ **Azure equivalents:** Blob / Disk / Files / Archive. - ☑️ **GCP equivalents:** Cloud Storage / Persistent Disk / Filestore / Archive/Coldline. - ☑️ **Open-source CNFS:** MinIO (object), Ceph (unified), GlusterFS (file). - ☑️ For **regulated workloads** (RBI, HIPAA) → Private or Hybrid Cloud. - ☑️ For **analytics / big data** → Object storage with EC. - ☑️ For **OLTP databases** → Block storage with provisioned IOPS. - ☑️ Always specify the **SLA tier** (99.9 / 99.99 / 99.999) when proposing a storage solution in exams.

topics.title: High Performance Computing Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST635 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** High Performance Computing Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — High Performance Computing (HPC) Models

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Performance Computing (HPC)** refers to the use of **aggregated computing power** delivered by parallel processing across multiple compute nodes, fast interconnects, and high-bandwidth memory subsystems to solve **compute-intensive, data-intensive, and tightly-coupled problems** that exceed the capability of a single workstation or commodity server.

In the KTU 2024 Cloud Computing syllabus, **HPC Models** describe the **architectural patterns, programming paradigms, and execution frameworks** that allow a pool of distributed resources (cores, hosts, racks, data centres) to act as a **single coherent supercomputer** for scientific, engineering, and analytics workloads.

A **High Performance Computing Model** is the formal specification of:
1. **How computation is partitioned** across workers (data/control parallelism).
2. **How processors communicate and synchronize** (shared memory, message passing).
3. **How data is distributed and accessed** (local memory, distributed memory, hybrid).
4. **How the model is exposed to the programmer** (MPI, OpenMP, MapReduce, CUDA, Spark).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core HPC Definition — KTU Board Approved Wording:**
> *"HPC is the practice of aggregating computing, memory, storage, and networking resources through parallel programming models so that the combined system delivers a significantly higher throughput and lower execution time for large-scale computational problems than any single commodity machine."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mega-Kitchen" Intuition

Imagine a single chef trying to cook dinner for **10,000 guests** in one kitchen. No matter how fast the chef is, the kitchen has only one stove, one sink, one counter. The chef becomes the **bottleneck**. This is a **serial workload on a single-node system**.

Now imagine:
- **100 kitchens** (compute nodes) connected by a logistics highway (interconnect),
- Each kitchen has **4 chefs** (CPU cores),
- A central dispatcher sends recipes and ingredients (workload distribution),
- Chefs share a **common pantry** (shared memory) **or** carry their own ingredients and shout across the hallway (message passing / distributed memory).

Now 10,000 guests are served in a fraction of the time. This is **HPC**.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** HPC = *many workers + smart coordination + fast communication*. The *model* you choose decides *who does what, when, and how they talk to each other*.

### 1.3 Standard HPC Metrics (KTU High-Yield Glossary)

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Use |
|--------|--------|---------|-------------|
| **Speedup** | $S$ | Ratio of serial time to parallel time | Quantifies parallel gain |
| **Parallel Efficiency** | $E$ | Speedup divided by number of processors | Quality of parallelization |
| **Scalability** | $\psi$ | Ability to maintain $E$ as $n$ grows | Vital for cloud HPC |
| **Throughput** | $\tau$ | Tasks completed per unit time | Job-rate metric |
| **Latency** | $L$ | Time from request to first response | Communication cost |
| **FLOPs** | $\text{F}$ | Floating-Point Operations per second | Raw compute power (**PFLOPS** in Petascale, **EFLOPS** in Exascale) |
| **Parallel Fraction** | $f$ | Fraction of code that can be parallelized | Used in Amdahl's Law |

### 1.4 Why HPC Matters in Cloud Computing (KTU Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **KTU 2024 Cloud Computing syllabus**, HPC and Cloud Computing converge through **HPC-as-a-Service (HPCaaS)**. Public clouds (AWS HPC, Azure Batch, Google Cloud HPC) now offer:
> - Elastic clusters of **bare-metal + GPU instances**,
> - **Burst HPC** workloads to cloud during peak demand,
> - **Hybrid HPC** where on-premise supercomputers offload to cloud for capacity overflow.

HPC Models are therefore the **theoretical backbone** that lets students understand *why* cloud elasticity, autoscaling, and parallel job schedulers (Slurm, Kubernetes) work the way they do.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of any HPC Model

Every HPC model — whether MPI, OpenMP, MapReduce, CUDA, or Spark — must answer **three fundamental questions**:

1. **Decomposition:** *How is the problem sliced?*
   - **Data Parallelism** — same operation applied to different chunks of data.
   - **Task Parallelism** — different operations executed on same/different data.
   - **Pipeline Parallelism** — stage-by-stage data flow.

2. **Mapping:** *Which worker gets which slice?*
   - **Static mapping** — decided at compile/launch time.
   - **Dynamic mapping** — decided by scheduler at run time (work-stealing, load balancing).

3. **Communication/Synchronization:** *How do workers share intermediate results?*
   - **Shared Memory** (OpenMP, Pthreads, CUDA shared memory).
   - **Message Passing** (MPI, PVM).
   - **Bulk Synchronous** (MapReduce, BSP, Spark RDDs).
   - **RDMA / One-sided** (MPI-3 RMA, GPUDirect).

## 2.2 Flynn's Taxonomy — The Foundational Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Flynn's Taxonomy (1966)** classifies parallel architectures by the *number of instruction streams* and *data streams* active at any instant.

| Class | Instruction Stream | Data Stream | Description | Example |
|-------|--------------------|-------------|-------------|---------|
| **SISD** | Single | Single | Classical von Neumann | Desktop PC |
| **SIMD** | Single | Multiple | Same instruction on vector data | GPU kernels, SSE/AVX, Vector processors |
| **MISD** | Multiple | Single | Rare; fault-tolerant pipelines | Space Shuttle computers |
| **MIMD** | Multiple | Multiple | Most flexible; modern HPC | Multicore CPU cluster, MPI cluster |
| (SPMD) | — | — | Sub-class of MIMD: same program, different data | MPI programs |
| (MPMD) | — | — | Sub-class of MIMD: different programs | Master/worker, MapReduce |

**Modern HPC clusters are MIMD/SPMD machines built out of SIMD-capable cores.**

## 2.3 Memory Organization Models

### 2.3.1 Shared Memory Multiprocessors (SMP / SMP-NUMA)
- All cores see a **single address space**.
- Communicate by **read/write to common variables**.
- Examples: OpenMP, Pthreads, CUDA shared memory.
- **Pros:** Easy to program. **Cons:** Scalability bottleneck (memory bus contention).

### 2.3.2 Distributed Memory Multiprocessors
- Each node has **its own private memory**.
- Communicate via **explicit message passing**.
- Examples: MPI, Charm++, GASNet.
- **Pros:** Scales to **millions of cores**. **Cons:** Programmer must handle data movement.

### 2.3.3 Hybrid Memory Model
- Each node is a shared-memory SMP; nodes are connected via a network.
- Today’s HPC standard (e.g., a Top500 cluster is hybrid).
- Programmed with **MPI between nodes + OpenMP within a node** (or MPI + CUDA on GPU).

## 2.4 Programming Models for HPC (KTU-Focused)

| Model | Memory Style | Granularity | Standard Tool | Typical Use |
|-------|--------------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Pthreads** | Shared | Fine | POSIX | System-level threading |
| **OpenMP** | Shared | Medium (loop-level) | Compiler directives | Scientific loops on SMP |
| **MPI** | Distributed | Coarse | MPI-4 standard | Cluster-scale HPC |
| **CUDA / OpenCL** | Hybrid | Fine-Massive | GPU SDKs | Deep learning, simulations |
| **MapReduce** | Distributed | Coarse | Hadoop | Data-intensive batch |
| **Spark** | Distributed | Coarse | Apache Spark | Iterative analytics |
| **OpenACC / SYCL** | Hybrid | Medium | Directive-based | GPU offload with minimal rewrite |

## 2.5 The Three Architectural Models for HPC in the Cloud

| Model | Description | Cloud Service Mapping |
|-------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Cluster Computing** | Homogeneous nodes inside one site, fast LAN interconnect | AWS ParallelCluster, Azure CycleCloud |
| **Grid Computing** | Heterogeneous, geographically distributed, shared via middleware (Globus, gLite) | Cross-organization federation |
| **Cloud HPC (HPCaaS)** | Elastic virtual clusters on demand; pay-per-use | AWS Batch + EC2, GCP HPC VMs |

## 2.6 📐 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical HPC formulas you MUST memorize for the KTU board exam.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Board Notation |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | $S_n = \dfrac{T_1}{T_n}$ | Speedup with $n$ processors | $T_1$ = serial time, $T_n$ = parallel time |
| 2 | $E_n = \dfrac{S_n}{n} = \dfrac{T_1}{n \cdot T_n}$ | Parallel efficiency | $0 < E_n \le 1$ |
| 3 | $S_n \le \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{n}}$ | **Amdahl's Law** | $f$ = serial fraction |
| 4 | $S_n \le n + (1-n) \cdot f$ | **Gustafson's Law** (scaled speedup) | Problem size grows with $n$ |
| 5 | $S_n = \dfrac{n \cdot p}{1 + (n-1) \cdot p}$ | **Karp-Flatt Metric** (derived from $f$) | Reveals serial overhead |
| 6 | $f = \dfrac{1/S_n - 1/n}{1 - 1/n}$ | Serial fraction from measured speedup | Reverse-engineering parallel code |
| 7 | $\text{Cost} = n \cdot T_n$ | Total cost of parallel execution | Compare cost-efficiency |
| 8 | $\text{Cost-Optimal} \iff E_n = 1$ | All processors busy | Ideal scaling |

### 2.6.1 Boundary Conditions (Important)

- **Upper bound (Amdahl):** $\lim_{n \to \infty} S_n = \dfrac{1}{f}$.
- **Lower bound:** $S_n = 1$ when $f = 1$ (no parallel benefit).
- **Ideal speedup:** $S_n = n$ when $E_n = 1$ (perfect linear scaling).
- **Sub-linear:** $S_n < n$ → communication overhead dominates.
- **Super-linear** ($S_n > n$): **possible only** when parallel system exploits caching effects (cache-friendly partitioning fits in L2/L3).

### 2.6.2 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Weather & Climate Modelling** — Atmospheric models parallelized with MPI over **100,000+ cores**.
- **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** — Automotive & aerospace simulations (Ansys Fluent, OpenFOAM).
- **Molecular Dynamics** — Drug discovery (GROMACS, NAMD).
- **Genomics & Bioinformatics** — Sequence alignment via **BLAST** parallelized in HPC.
- **AI Training** — Foundation-model training requires **GPU clusters** (essentially MIMD + SIMD).
- **Financial Risk Analytics** — Monte-Carlo simulations in MPI across thousands of cores.
- **Seismic Imaging** — Oil & gas exploration using HPCaaS.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law (Full Board-Ready Derivation)

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let the **total serial execution time** on a single processor be normalized to **$T_1 = 1$**.

Let $f$ be the **inherently serial fraction** of the program that cannot be parallelized. Then:
- **Parallel fraction** of the program = $1 - f$.
- **Serial fraction** of the program = $f$.

### 3.1.2 Execution Time on $n$ Processors

The serial part is run by **exactly one** processor (no speedup possible). The parallel part is run by **all $n$ processors** simultaneously.

$$
T_n = \underbrace{f \cdot T_1}_{\text{serial part}} + \underbrace{\frac{(1 - f) \cdot T_1}{n}}_{\text{parallel part}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Speedup Computation

$$
S_n = \frac{T_1}{T_n} = \frac{T_1}{f \cdot T_1 + \frac{(1 - f) \cdot T_1}{n}}
$$

Factor out $T_1$ (numerator and denominator):

$$
S_n = \frac{1}{f + \dfrac{(1 - f)}{n}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **Amdahl's Law**, the most-asked HPC formula in KTU board exams. Memorize the form $\dfrac{1}{f + (1-f)/n}$.

### 3.1.4 Limiting Behaviour — Why Amdahl's Law is Pessimistic

Take the limit as $n \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S_n = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{n}} = \frac{1}{f + 0} = \frac{1}{f}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even with **infinite processors**, speedup is bounded by $1/f$. For example, if $5\%$ is serial ($f = 0.05$):
- $\lim_{n \to \infty} S_n = \dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20\times$.

This is the **Amdahl bottleneck**, which motivates **Gustafson's Law**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Gustafson's Law (Scaled Speedup)

### 3.2.1 Assumption

Problem size is **scaled proportionally** with $n$. The **serial fraction $f$ is therefore kept constant in absolute time** (scaled workload).

### 3.2.2 Time Equation

$$
T_n = f \cdot T_1 + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_1}{n}
$$

But here $T_1$ grows with $n$. To compare fair-to-fair, define $T_1' = n \cdot T_n$ (time it *would* have taken on one processor if executed as the *scaled* workload).

Rewriting:

$$
S_n^{\text{scaled}} = f \cdot n + (1 - f) = n - f(n - 1)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gustafson's Law:** $\boxed{S_n = n + (1 - n) \cdot f}$

This predicts **near-linear speedup** for problems that scale — which is why HPC users typically **increase problem size** when they get more cores (it is unrealistic to fix the problem size as $n$ grows).

## 3.3 Karp-Flatt Metric — Diagnostic for Real Systems

### 3.3.1 Empirical Discovery of Effective Serial Fraction

The serial fraction in real HPC code is **not** the source-code serial fraction; it includes **communication overhead, load imbalance, synchronization, and contention**. Karp-Flatt extracts the **effective serial fraction** from measured $S_n$:

$$
f_e = \frac{1/S_n - 1/n}{1 - 1/n}
$$

If $f_e$ **stays constant** as $n$ grows → parallel overhead is the bottleneck. If $f_e$ **grows** with $n$ → communication is the bottleneck.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples (Board Practice)

### 3.4.1 Example 1 — Amdahl's Law (Classic 5-Minute Board Question)

> **Problem:** A program takes **100 seconds** on a single processor. **20% of it is inherently serial**. Compute the speedup on **8 processors** and on **64 processors**. Comment on the result.

**Solution:**

Given $T_1 = 100\,\text{s}$, $f = 0.20$, $n = 8$ and $n = 64$.

$$
S_8 = \frac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{(1 - 0.20)}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.10} = \frac{1}{0.30} \approx 3.33
$$

$$
T_8 = \frac{T_1}{S_8} = \frac{100}{3.33} \approx 30.0\,\text{s}
$$

For $n = 64$:

$$
S_{64} = \frac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{64}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.0125} = \frac{1}{0.2125} \approx 4.71
$$

**Comment:** Adding processors from 8 → 64 yields only **3.33× → 4.71×**, a **marginal 41% gain** despite **8× more hardware**. This is the **Amdahl bottleneck**.

**Limiting speedup:**

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5\times
$$

### 3.4.2 Example 2 — Gustafson's Law (Scaled Workload)

> **Problem:** A weather simulation has $f = 0.05$ on a single CPU. The lab buys 128 new nodes and **scales the simulation grid** accordingly. Compute the Gustafson speedup.

**Solution:**

$$
S_{128} = 128 + (1 - 128) \cdot 0.05 = 128 - 127 \cdot 0.05 = 128 - 6.35 = 121.65
$$

**Interpretation:** With scaled workload, **128 nodes** yield **~121.65× speedup** — vastly better than Amdahl's bound of $1/0.05 = 20\times$ on a fixed-size problem.

### 3.4.3 Example 3 — Efficiency & Cost Computation

> **Problem:** A parallel job executes on **16 processors** in **25 seconds**. The same job on **1 processor** takes **300 seconds**. Find Speedup, Efficiency, and Cost.

**Solution:**

$$
S_{16} = \frac{T_1}{T_{16}} = \frac{300}{25} = 12
$$

$$
E_{16} = \frac{S_{16}}{n} = \frac{12}{16} = 0.75 = 75\%
$$

$$
\text{Cost} = n \cdot T_n = 16 \cdot 25 = 400 \text{ processor-seconds}
$$

**Comparison with serial cost:** Serial cost = $1 \cdot 300 = 300$. Since parallel cost (400) > serial cost (300), this configuration is **not cost-optimal**.

### 3.4.4 Example 4 — Karp-Flatt Reverse Calculation

> **Problem:** Measured $S_{32} = 20$. Compute the effective serial fraction $f_e$.

**Solution:**

$$
f_e = \frac{1/S_n - 1/n}{1 - 1/n} = \frac{1/20 - 1/32}{1 - 1/32} = \frac{0.05 - 0.03125}{0.96875} = \frac{0.01875}{0.96875} \approx 0.01935
$$

> [!TIP]
> **So ~1.94% effective serial fraction** — useful diagnostic to identify if HPC system has excessive communication overhead.

## 3.5 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Board-Friendly Summary)

| Criterion | Amdahl's Law | Gustafson's Law | Karp-Flatt |
|-----------|--------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Assumption** | Fixed problem size | Scaled problem size | Empirical |
| **Formula** | $S = \dfrac{1}{f + (1-f)/n}$ | $S = n + (1-n) f$ | $f_e = \dfrac{1/S - 1/n}{1 - 1/n}$ |
| **Predicts** | Upper bound on $S$ | Practical scaled $S$ | Effective serial fraction |
| **Optimistic / Pessimistic?** | Pessimistic | Optimistic | Diagnostic |
| **Used for** | Worst-case analysis | Real HPC scaling curves | Bottleneck identification |
| **KTU Board Weight** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
