# 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 🅰️ Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECST635]`

> **Q: Define High Performance Computing (HPC). List any three programming models used in HPC with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
High Performance Computing is the practice of aggregating computing power by combining multiple processors, fast interconnects, and high-bandwidth memory to solve large-scale computational problems faster than a single machine.

**Three Programming Models (2 marks — 0.5 + 0.5 + 1 split):**

1. **OpenMP** — shared-memory directive-based parallelization for SMP systems. Example: parallelizing a matrix multiplication loop on a multicore CPU.
2. **MPI (Message Passing Interface)** — distributed-memory communication standard for HPC clusters. Example: domain decomposition of a CFD mesh across 1024 nodes.
3. **MapReduce** — batch data-parallel programming model. Example: log analytics on petabytes of clickstream data in Hadoop.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECST635]`

> **Q: With a neat diagram, explain Flynn's classification of computer architectures. Give an example for each class.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark diagram + 1 mark explanation + 1 mark examples):**

Flynn's Taxonomy classifies architectures by the multiplicity of *instruction streams* and *data streams*:

| Class | Instruction | Data | Example (0.25 each) |
|-------|-------------|------|----------------------|
| **SISD** | 1 | 1 | Desktop PC running a serial loop |
| **SIMD** | 1 | Many | GPU kernel applying `ax+b` to 1M array elements |
| **MISD** | Many | 1 | Fault-tolerant space-shuttle pipeline computers |
| **MIMD** | Many | Many | MPI cluster running weather simulation |

**Diagram (1 mark):** Simple 2×2 grid (rows = instruction, cols = data, four cells labelled SISD/SIMD/MISD/MIMD).

---

## 5.2 🅱️ Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** Define **Speedup**, **Efficiency**, and **Cost-Optimality** in HPC. Derive **Amdahl's Law** and explain its significance in parallel computing. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A parallel program executes on **$n = 16$ processors** in **12 seconds**. The same program on a single processor takes **120 seconds**.
> Calculate:
> (i) Speedup, (ii) Efficiency, (iii) Total Cost, and (iv) Comment on cost-optimality. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Speedup $S_n = T_1/T_n$** — how much faster the program runs on $n$ processors versus one. (0.5)
- **Efficiency $E_n = S_n / n$** — fraction of processor time doing useful work, $0 < E_n \le 1$. (0.5)
- **Cost-Optimality** — a parallel execution is cost-optimal when its cost $n \cdot T_n$ is asymptotically equal to $T_1$, i.e. $E_n = 1$. (1.0)

**Amdahl's Law Derivation (4 marks):**

Let $T_1$ = serial execution time, $f$ = serial fraction, $(1-f)$ = parallel fraction.

$$
T_n = f \cdot T_1 + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_1}{n}
$$

$$
S_n = \frac{T_1}{T_n} = \frac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{n}}
$$

**Significance (1 mark):**
Even with infinite processors, speedup is capped at $1/f$, revealing the **serial bottleneck**. Hence, the only way to scale is to reduce the serial fraction — the core motivator for HPC system design and algorithm restructuring.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: $T_1 = 120\,\text{s}$, $T_{16} = 12\,\text{s}$, $n = 16$.

**(i) Speedup (2 marks):**

$$
S_{16} = \frac{T_1}{T_{16}} = \frac{120}{12} = 10
$$

**(ii) Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
E_{16} = \frac{S_{16}}{n} = \frac{10}{16} = 0.625 = 62.5\%
$$

**(iii) Total Cost (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Cost} = n \cdot T_n = 16 \cdot 12 = 192 \text{ processor-seconds}
$$

**(iv) Cost-Optimality Comment (1 mark):**

Serial cost = $1 \cdot 120 = 120$ processor-seconds. Since $192 > 120$, the parallel execution is **not cost-optimal**. It wastes $192 - 120 = 72$ processor-seconds of computation. The system must be redesigned (e.g., reduce communication overhead) until cost-optimality is approached.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** Explain the **Shared Memory** and **Distributed Memory** programming models in HPC with suitable diagrams. Compare MPI and OpenMP highlighting their memory model, communication style, and typical use case. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A parallel application has **5% inherently serial code** ($f = 0.05$). Compute the **maximum speedup** possible using:
> (i) Amdahl's Law with $n = 32$ processors, (ii) Amdahl's Law in the limit $n \to \infty$, and (iii) Gustafson's Law with $n = 32$ processors. **Comment on the difference between the two laws.** **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Shared Memory Model (2 marks):**
- All processors **share a single address space**.
- Communication happens by **reads/writes to common variables**.
- **Synchronization:** locks, semaphores, barriers (OpenMP, Pthreads).
- **Limitation:** contention on shared bus/cache coherence limits scalability.

**Distributed Memory Model (2 marks):**
- Each processor has its **own private memory**.
- Communication by **explicit message passing** (send/receive).
- **Synchronization:** message ordering, collectives (MPI_Allreduce, MPI_Barrier).
- **Advantage:** scales to **millions of cores** (Top500 systems).

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MPI | OpenMP |
|--------|-----|--------|
| Memory model | Distributed | Shared |
| Communication | Explicit messages | Implicit via shared variables |
| Granularity | Coarse (process-level) | Fine (loop-level) |
| Standard | MPI-4 | OpenMP 5.x |
| Typical use | Inter-node cluster parallelism | Intra-node multicore parallelism |
| Scalability | Up to 10⁶+ ranks | Up to ~hundreds of threads |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given $f = 0.05$, $n = 32$.

**(i) Amdahl with $n = 32$ (2 marks):**

$$
S_{32}^{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{32}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.0296875} = \frac{1}{0.0796875} \approx 12.55
$$

**(ii) Amdahl limit (2 marks):**

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20
$$

**(iii) Gustafson with $n = 32$ (2 marks):**

$$
S_{32}^{\text{Gustafson}} = n + (1-n) \cdot f = 32 + (1 - 32)(0.05) = 32 - 1.55 = 30.45
$$

**(iv) Comment (1 mark):**

Amdahl fixes the problem size and predicts only ~12.55× on 32 cores, capped at 20×. Gustafson scales the workload with $n$ and predicts 30.45×, close to linear. In real HPC, problem sizes do scale (larger grids, deeper networks, finer meshes), so Gustafson matches reality better.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the denominator of Amdahl's Law** — many students write $S_n = 1/f$ and lose the parallel-fraction contribution. Always write the full form $\dfrac{1}{f + (1-f)/n}$.
> 2. **Confusing Gustafson vs Amdahl** — Gustafson requires **scaled workload**, not fixed. Marking penalizes students who apply Gustafson without the scaling assumption.
> 3. **Speedup vs Efficiency mix-up** — Speedup has no upper bound proportional to $n$, but efficiency must be between 0 and 1. Many students forget the unit check.
> 4. **Wrong unit on Cost** — Cost is always **processor-seconds** (or core-hours), never wall-clock time. Penalized in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **MPI vs OpenMP confusion** — Students often claim MPI uses shared memory. Examiners deduct full marks if memory model is wrong.
> 6. **No diagram in Flynn's Taxonomy** — KTU 2024 expects a labelled 2×2 grid. Text-only answers get 1 mark less.
> 7. **Missing $f_e$ derivation in Karp-Flatt** — only the formula is asked; examiners expect the rearranged form too.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — High-Density Cheat Sheet**

- ✅ **HPC definition:** Aggregated compute across parallel processors + fast interconnect + efficient parallel programming.
- ✅ **Flynn's 4 classes:** SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD; sub-classes SPMD, MPMD.
- ✅ **Memory models:** Shared (OpenMP, Pthreads), Distributed (MPI), Hybrid (MPI+OpenMP, MPI+CUDA).
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S_n = \dfrac{1}{f + (1-f)/n}$ — fixed-size pessimistic bound.
- ✅ **Gustafson's Law:** $S_n = n + (1-n)f$ — scaled-size optimistic bound.
- ✅ **Karp-Flatt:** $f_e = \dfrac{1/S_n - 1/n}{1 - 1/n}$ — empirical serial fraction.
- ✅ **Speedup:** $S_n = T_1/T_n$.
- ✅ **Efficiency:** $E_n = S_n / n$ — value between 0 and 1.
- ✅ **Cost:** $n \cdot T_n$ — must equal $T_1$ for cost-optimality.
- ✅ **HPC Programming Models:** OpenMP, MPI, CUDA/OpenCL, MapReduce, Spark.
- ✅ **HPCaaS:** Cloud HPC via AWS Batch, Azure CycleCloud, GCP HPC VMs.
- ✅ **Cluster vs Grid vs Cloud HPC:** Cluster = homogeneous local; Grid = heterogeneous federation; Cloud HPC = elastic virtual clusters.
- ✅ **Top500 metric:** LINPACK Rmax measured in **PFLOPS**; modern systems are **Exascale** (> $10^{18}$ FLOPs).
- ✅ **Communication overhead grows with $n$** — main reason Amdahl is pessimistic in practice.
- ✅ **Super-linear speedup** is **rare** and only due to cache effects (more cores = larger aggregate cache).
- ✅ **Cloud HPC trade-offs:** elasticity vs. VM jitter, I/O virtualization penalty, multi-tenancy noise neighbours.
- ✅ **Modern HPC is hybrid** — every Top500 supercomputer is MIMD built from SIMD cores with hybrid memory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hands-On - Use MapReduce to Implement Basic Big Data Applications Such as Word Count
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST635 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hands-On - Use MapReduce to Implement Basic Big Data Applications Such as Word Count # 📘 MODULE 3 — HANDS-ON: MapReduce & The Word Count Application > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Examiner's Note:** This is a *hands-on / implementation* topic. The board examiner expects you to (1) explain the MapReduce paradigm, (2) write or interpret the **Mapper, Reducer, Driver (Main)** classes, and (3) trace the data flow through **Map → Shuffle & Sort → Reduce** for the Word Count problem. Memorize the key-value pair format: `` → `` → ``. --- ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **MapReduce** is a *parallel and distributed programming model* introduced by **Google (Dean & Ghemawat, 2004)** for processing massive datasets on commodity clusters. In the **Hadoop** ecosystem (Apache), it is composed of two principal user-defined functions and an implicit framework-managed phase: $$ \text{MapReduce} = \underbrace{\text{Map}(k_1, v_1) \to \langle k_2, v_2 \rangle}_{\text{user-defined}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Shuffle \& Sort}}_{\text{framework}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Reduce}(k_2, [v_2]) \to \langle k_3, v_3 \rangle}_{\text{user-defined}} $$ The **Word Count** problem is the canonical "Hello, World!" of Big Data. Given a large text corpus $D$ split into $n$ splits $D = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_n\}$, the objective is to compute the frequency $f(w)$ for every distinct word $w \in \mathcal{W}$ appearing in $D$. $$ f(w) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \; \text{count}_i(w) $$ where $\text{count}_i(w)$ is the number of times word $w$ appears in split $d_i$. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Library Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "The National Census"** > > Imagine the Government of India must count the occurrence of every unique word spoken in every speech delivered in 2024. They cannot rely on a single clerk — there are too many speeches. So: > > - **Step 1 (Split):** The corpus is divided into 100 regional bundles, one per state. > - **Step 2 (Map):** 100 clerks (one per state) each read their bundle and write down a slip of paper `(word, 1)` for every word they encounter — that is, they **map** each word to a unit count. > - **Step 3 (Shuffle & Sort):** A logistics team collects all slips and groups them by word into separate bins (e.g., one giant bin for "India", one for "development", etc.). This is done *automatically* by the framework. > - **Step 4 (Reduce):** A single accountant per bin tallies the slips, producing `(word, total_count)`. > > Each clerk is a **Mapper task**, each accountant is a **Reducer task**, and the regional bundles are **HDFS input splits**. --- ## 1.3 Why MapReduce? — The Motivation | Challenge in Big Data | How MapReduce Solves It | |---|---| | **Volume (TB–PB scale)** | Data partitioned across HDFS; processed in parallel on commodity nodes. | | **Fault Tolerance** | Tasks re-executed on failure; intermediate outputs written to local disk. | | **Scalability** | Linear scaling: double the nodes → roughly halve the job time. | | **Programming Simplicity** | User writes only `map()` and `reduce()`; parallelism, distribution, fault-tolerance are framework-managed. | | **Data Locality** | Mapper runs on the node where the data block resides, minimizing network I/O. | --- ## 1.4 Hadoop Cluster Architecture — Quick Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master–Worker Topology (KTU Frequently Tested):** > > - **NameNode (Master)** — manages HDFS metadata (file → block mapping). > - **DataNodes (Workers)** — store actual data blocks (default **128 MB** per block in Hadoop 2.x/3.x). > - **ResourceManager (Master)** — YARN component that schedules jobs cluster-wide. > - **NodeManager (Worker, per node)** — launches and monitors *containers* (Map/Reduce tasks). > - **ApplicationMaster (per job)** — negotiates resources with ResourceManager and tracks task progress. A MapReduce Word Count job is submitted by the **Client** → **Job** is allocated an **ApplicationMaster** → ApplicationMaster requests **containers** on DataNodes hosting the input blocks → **Mappers** run with **data locality** → intermediate output is shuffled → **Reducers** produce final output → results written back to HDFS. # 🔍 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Anatomy of a MapReduce Job — Phase-by-Phase A MapReduce job executes in **four explicit phases** plus an implicit **Driver/Job Submission** phase. ### Phase 0 — InputFormat & Splitting - The **`InputFormat`** (default: `TextInputFormat`) reads the HDFS file and computes **input splits**. - Each split is logically (not physically) a chunk of data; default size = **HDFS block size (128 MB)**. - Each split produces one **Mapper task**. ### Phase 1 — Map Phase (User-Defined) - The `Mapper` receives key-value pairs $\langle k_1, v_1 \rangle$ where: - $k_1$ = byte offset of the line within the file (`LongWritable` in Java) - $v_1$ = the line content as a UTF-8 string (`Text` in Java) - The user-defined `map()` method emits an arbitrary number of intermediate pairs $\langle k_2, v_2 \rangle$. - For Word Count: $\langle \text{line\_offset}, \text{line} \rangle \rightarrow \langle \text{word}, 1 \rangle$ ### Phase 2 — Combiner (Optional Local Reducer) - A **mini-reducer** that runs on the **same node** as the Mapper, immediately after the map output. - Performs **local aggregation** to reduce data shuffled over the network. - For Word Count: a combiner sums the `1`s locally, e.g., $\langle \text{“the”}, [1,1,1] \rangle \rightarrow \langle \text{“the”}, 3 \rangle$ - **Combiner is commutative and associative** (so safe for sum/count). ### Phase 3 — Shuffle & Sort (Framework-Controlled) - All intermediate $\langle k_2, v_2 \rangle$ pairs with the same key $k_2$ are **grouped** and delivered to a single Reducer. - The framework **partitions** keys among reducers (default: `HashPartitioner`). - Within each reducer, data is **sorted by key** before the reduce function executes. - This is the **most network-intensive** phase. ### Phase 4 — Reduce Phase (User-Defined) - The `Reducer` receives $\langle k_2, [v_2, v_2, \dots] \rangle$ — a key and an **iterable list of values**. - The user-defined `reduce()` method emits the final output $\langle k_3, v_3 \rangle$. - For Word Count: $\langle \text{word}, [1,1,\dots,1] \rangle \rightarrow \langle \text{word}, \text{total\_count} \rangle$ ### Phase 5 — OutputFormat & Write-Back - The default `TextOutputFormat` writes final $\langle k_3, v_3 \rangle$ pairs to HDFS, one per line. --- ## 2.2 Key-Value Pair Lifecycle — The Mental Model $$ \underbrace{\langle k_1, v_1 \rangle}_{\text{InputFormat}} \xrightarrow{\text{map()}} \underbrace{\langle k_2, v_2 \rangle}_{\text{Intermediate}} \xrightarrow{\text{shuffle}} \underbrace{\langle k_2, [v_2, v_2, \dots] \rangle}_{\text{Reducer Input}} \xrightarrow{\text{reduce()}} \underbrace{\langle k_3, v_3 \rangle}_{\text{Final Output}} $$ --- ## 2.3 The Mapper — `WordCountMapper.java` (Hadoop Java) ```java // KTU Board-Standard Code — Java Hadoop API import org.apache.hadoop.io.IntWritable; import org.apache.hadoop.io.LongWritable; import org.apache.hadoop.io.Text; import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Mapper; import java.io.IOException; import java.util.StringTokenizer; public class WordCountMapper extends Mapper { private final static IntWritable one = new IntWritable(1); private Text word = new Text(); @Override protected void map(LongWritable key, Text value, Context context) throws IOException, InterruptedException { // Tokenize the input line StringTokenizer itr = new StringTokenizer(value.toString()); while (itr.hasMoreTokens()) { word.set(itr.nextToken()); // Emit for every occurrence context.write(word, one); } } } ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why `LongWritable`, `Text`, `IntWritable`?** Hadoop uses its own Writable types instead of native Java `Long`, `String`, `Integer` to enable efficient serialization across the network and disk. --- ## 2.4 The Reducer — `WordCountReducer.java` (Hadoop Java) ```java // KTU Board-Standard Code — Java Hadoop API import org.apache.hadoop.io.IntWritable; import org.apache.hadoop.io.Text; import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Reducer; import java.io.IOException; public class WordCountReducer extends Reducer { @Override protected void reduce(Text key, Iterable values, Context context) throws IOException, InterruptedException { int sum = 0; // Iterate over all the 1s grouped under this key for (IntWritable val : values) { sum += val.get(); } // Emit final context.write(key, new IntWritable(sum)); } } ``` --- ## 2.5 The Driver — `WordCount.java` (Hadoop Java) ```java // KTU Board-Standard Code — Hadoop Driver / Main Class import org.apache.hadoop.conf.Configuration; import org.apache.hadoop.fs.Path; import org.apache.hadoop.io.IntWritable; import org.apache.hadoop.io.Text; import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Job; import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.lib.input.FileInputFormat; import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.lib.output.FileOutputFormat; public class WordCount { public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception { Configuration conf = new Configuration(); Job job = Job.getInstance(conf, "Word Count"); job.setJarByClass(WordCount.class); job.setMapperClass(WordCountMapper.class); job.setReducerClass(WordCountReducer.class); // Optional but recommended for Word Count job.setCombinerClass(WordCountReducer.class); job.setOutputKeyClass(Text.class); job.setOutputValueClass(IntWritable.class); FileInputFormat.addInputPath(job, new Path(args[0])); FileOutputFormat.setOutputPath(job, new Path(args[1])); System.exit(job.waitForCompletion(true) ? 0 : 1); } } ``` --- ## 2.6 Python Equivalent — Using `mrjob` (Modern, Lightweight) ```python # KTU Hands-On Style — Python mrjob implementation from mrjob.job import MRJob import re WORD_RE = re.compile(r"[\w']+") class MRWordCount(MRJob): def mapper(self, _, line): # Yield for each token in the line for word in WORD_RE.findall(line): yield (word.lower(), 1) def combiner(self, word, counts): # Local aggregation (optional but efficient) yield (word, sum(counts)) def reducer(self, word, counts): # Final tally yield (word, sum(counts)) if __name__ == '__main__': MRWordCount.run() ``` > [!TIP] > **Run command:** `python word_count.py input.txt > output.txt` > For Hadoop: `python word_count.py -r hadoop hdfs:///input/ -o hdfs:///output/` --- ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is HIGH-YIELD for KTU board exams. Memorize it.** | # | Concept / Metric | Formula or Value | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Total Word Frequency | $f(w) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{count}_i(w)$ | Words per word $w$ | | 2 | Number of Mappers | $M = \lceil \text{Total Input Size} / \text{Block Size} \rceil$ | Default block size = **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+) | | 3 | Number of Reducers | $R$ = user-set (default = 1) | Tune via `job.setNumReduceTasks(n)` | | 4 | Data Locality Speedup | $S_{\text{local}} \approx M / 1$ when mappers colocated with blocks | Ideal: $S = M$ | | 5 | Combiner Output Size | $O_{\text{comb}} \le O_{\text{map}}$ | Reduces shuffle volume | | 6 | Hadoop Default Block Size | **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x/3.x); 64 MB (Hadoop 1.x) | Configurable via `dfs.blocksize` | | 7 | Replication Factor (HDFS) | Default = **3** | Configurable per file | | 8 | Map Task Memory | `mapreduce.map.memory.mb` | Default **1024 MB** | | 9 | Reduce Task Memory | `mapreduce.reduce.memory.mb` | Default **1024 MB** | | 10 | Speculative Execution | Backups for straggler tasks | Enabled by default | | 11 | Writable Types | `LongWritable`, `Text`, `IntWritable`, `FloatWritable` | Hadoop serializable types | | 12 | Partitioner (Default) | `HashPartitioner(k) = k.hashCode() \mod R$ | Distributes keys among reducers | | 13 | Counter — Map Input Records | Increments per $\langle k_1, v_1 \rangle$ | Built-in framework counter | | 14 | Counter — Reduce Output Records | Increments per $\langle k_3, v_3 \rangle$ | Built-in framework counter | > **⚠ Table Safety Note:** The modulo operator is written as `\mod` (rendered in LaTeX) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax with the `|` pipe character. --- ## 2.8 Where is This Used in Industry? - **Log analytics** — counting error codes, URL hits, IP addresses in web server logs. - **Search engines** — building **inverted indexes** (precursor to TF-IDF in Lucene/Elasticsearch). - **Social media analytics** — hashtag frequency, sentiment word counts on Twitter/X firehose. - **ETL pipelines** — preprocessing raw clickstreams before loading into a data warehouse. - **Genomics** — counting k-mers in DNA sequences using **Hadoop-BAM** and **ADAM** frameworks. # 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Worked Example — Tracing Word Count on a Toy Dataset Consider the input text (3 lines): $$ D = \{ d_1: \text{“hello world hello”},\; d_2: \text{“hello hadoop”},\; d_3: \text{“world hadoop”} \} $$ ### Step 1 — Input Splits (assuming one split per line) Each line becomes a $\langle k_1, v_1 \rangle$ pair: - $\langle 0, \text{“hello world hello”} \rangle$ - $\langle 21, \text{“hello hadoop”} \rangle$ *(assuming offset 0–20 for line 1)* - $\langle 35, \text{“world hadoop”} \rangle$ ### Step 2 — Map Phase Output (Intermediate Pairs) For each line, the mapper tokenizes and emits one $\langle \text{word}, 1 \rangle$ per token: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Line 1:} \quad & \langle \text{“hello”}, 1 \rangle,\; \langle \text{“world”}, 1 \rangle,\; \langle \text{“hello”}, 1 \rangle \\[4pt] \text{Line 2:} \quad & \langle \text{“hello”}, 1 \rangle,\; \langle \text{“hadoop”}, 1 \rangle \\[4pt] \text{Line 3:} \quad & \langle \text{“world”}, 1 \rangle,\; \langle \text{“hadoop”}, 1 \rangle \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Combiner Phase (Optional Local Aggregation) Assume each line is processed by the same mapper (no combiner cross-mapper aggregation). Within a mapper, local aggregation collapses identical keys: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mapper 1:} \quad & \langle \text{“hello”}, 2 \rangle,\; \langle \text{“world”}, 1 \rangle \\[4pt] \text{Mapper 2:} \quad & \langle \text{“hello”}, 1 \rangle,\; \langle \text{“hadoop”}, 1 \rangle \\[4pt] \text{Mapper 3:} \quad & \langle \text{“world”}, 1 \rangle,\; \langle \text{“hadoop”}, 1 \rangle \end{aligned} $$ > **Shuffle volume reduction:** Without combiner: 7 pairs shipped. With combiner: 6 pairs shipped. In production with millions of occurrences, this is a *huge* saving. ### Step 4 — Shuffle & Sort (Partitioning by Hash) Assume **2 Reducers** ($R = 2$). Default partition rule: $$ \text{partition}(k) = \vert \text{key.hashCode()} \vert \mod R $$ For this example, let's say: - “hello” → partition 0 - “world” → partition 0 - “hadoop” → partition 1 After sorting within each partition: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Reducer 0 receives:} \quad & \langle \text{“hello”}, [2, 1] \rangle,\; \langle \text{“world”}, [1, 1] \rangle \\[4pt] \text{Reducer 1 receives:} \quad & \langle \text{“hadoop”}, [1, 1] \rangle \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Reduce Phase (Final Aggregation) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Reducer 0:} \quad & \langle \text{“hello”}, 3 \rangle,\; \langle \text{“world”}, 2 \rangle \\[4pt] \text{Reducer 1:} \quad & \langle \text{“hadoop”}, 2 \rangle \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Final Output (Written to HDFS) $$ \boxed{ \begin{array}{l} \text{hadoop}\;\; 2 \\ \text{hello} \;\; 3 \\ \text{world} \;\; 2 \end{array} } $$ --- ## 3.2 Mathematical Analysis — Why MapReduce is Linearly Scalable Let: - $T_{\text{seq}}$ = sequential processing time on 1 node - $M$ = number of mapper nodes - $R$ = number of reducer nodes - $T_{\text{map}}$ = time per map task - $T_{\text{shuffle}}$ = network shuffle cost - $T_{\text{reduce}}$ = time per reduce task The total MapReduce job time is modeled as: $$ T_{\text{MR}} = T_{\text{setup}} \;+\; \underbrace{\max_{i=1..M}\left(T_{\text{map}}^{(i)}\right)}_{\text{parallel map}} \;+\; \underbrace{T_{\text{shuffle}}}_{\text{network}} \;+\; \underbrace{\max_{j=1..R}\left(T_{\text{reduce}}^{(j)}\right)}_{\text{parallel reduce}} $$ **Idealized scaling** (perfect load balancing, zero overhead): $$ T_{\text{MR}}^{\text{ideal}}(M) \;\approx\; \frac{T_{\text{seq}}}{M} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{\alpha \cdot N}{B}}_{\text{shuffle bottleneck}} \;+\; \frac{T_{\text{seq}}}{R} $$ where $\alpha$ = proportion of data shuffled, $N$ = total data volume, $B$ = network bandwidth. The **shuffle bottleneck** is why combiners and partitioners are critical. **Speedup:** $$ S(M, R) = \frac{T_{\text{seq}}}{T_{\text{MR}}} $$ For pure map-heavy workloads with $R = 1$ and small $\alpha$, $S \approx M$ (linear speedup). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Failure & Recovery Semantics | Failure Type | Detection | Recovery Mechanism | Retry Limit | |---|---|---|---| | **Mapper Task Failure** | NodeManager heartbeat timeout | ApplicationMaster reschedules on another node | 4 (default) | | **Reducer Task Failure** | NodeManager heartbeat timeout | ApplicationMaster reschedules on another node | 4 (default) | | **ApplicationMaster Failure** | ResourceManager timeout | ResourceManager restarts AM (YARN) | 2 (default) | | **NodeManager Failure** | ResourceManager heartbeat timeout | All running containers on node marked failed | N/A | | **Data Block Corruption** | Checksum mismatch on read | HDFS reads replica from another DataNode | N/A | > [!NOTE] > **Speculative Execution:** If one mapper is running much slower than peers (a "straggler"), YARN launches a backup copy on another node and accepts whichever finishes first. This trades compute for latency. --- ## 3.4 Mapping the Word Count to Database Operations | MapReduce Term | SQL Equivalent | Notes | |---|---|---| | Input split | Table scan with predicate | File = table, line = row | | `map()` | `SELECT key, 1 FROM input` | Projects tuples | | `combine()` / `reduce()` | `SELECT key, COUNT(*) FROM mapped GROUP BY key` | Aggregation | | `OutputFormat` | Result set / new table | Persistent storage | The Word Count problem is equivalent to: $$ \text{SELECT LOWER(word), COUNT(*)} \;\;\text{FROM} \;\; \text{tokens} \;\; \text{GROUP BY} \;\; \text{LOWER(word)}; $$ # 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 MapReduce Word Count — End-to-End Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[HDFS Input File
Multiple Blocks] --> B[InputFormat
TextInputFormat] B --> C1[Split 1
Mapper Task 1] B --> C2[Split 2
Mapper Task 2] B --> C3[Split 3
Mapper Task 3] C1 --> D1[Local Output
word,1 pairs] C2 --> D2[Local Output
word,1 pairs] C3 --> D3[Local Output
word,1 pairs] D1 --> E1[Combiner
Local Aggregation] D2 --> E2[Combiner
Local Aggregation] D3 --> E3[Combiner
Local Aggregation] E1 --> F[Shuffle and Sort
Partition by Key] E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> G1[Reducer 1
Partition 0] F --> G2[Reducer 2
Partition 1] G1 --> H1[HDFS Output Part 0
word count] G2 --> H2[HDFS Output Part 1
word count] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram:** Each Mapper processes **one HDFS block** (≈128 MB). The Combiner is **optional** but always placed between the mapper and the network shuffle. Reducer output is written as **`part-r-00000`, `part-r-00001`** … files in HDFS. --- ## 4.2 Hadoop YARN Cluster Architecture (Word Count Submission Path) ```mermaid flowchart TB Client[Client
hadoop jar wordcount.jar] --> RM[ResourceManager
Master Scheduler] RM --> AM[ApplicationMaster
Per Job] AM --> NM1[NodeManager 1
DataNode 1] AM --> NM2[NodeManager 2
DataNode 2] AM --> NM3[NodeManager 3
DataNode 3] NM1 --> CT1[Container
Mapper Task] NM2 --> CT2[Container
Mapper Task] NM3 --> CT3[Container
Reducer Task] CT1 --> SR[Shuffle and Sort
HTTP Transfer] CT2 --> SR SR --> CT3 ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Data Locality ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DN1[DataNode 1 - Rack 1] B1[Block A] B2[Block B] M1[Mapper 1] end subgraph DN2[DataNode 2 - Rack 1] B3[Block C] M2[Mapper 2] end subgraph DN3[DataNode 3 - Rack 2] B4[Block D] R1[Reducer 1] end M1 -.reads.-> B1 M1 -.reads.-> B2 M2 -.reads.-> B3 M1 -.shuffles.-> R1 M2 -.shuffles.-> R1 DN1 --- NET[Network Rack Switch] DN2 --- NET DN3 --- NET ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for the exam:** The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests *data locality* — Mappers are scheduled by YARN on the **same node** as the input block whenever possible to avoid network I/O. If a slot is unavailable on the local node, YARN falls back to *same-rack* then *off-rack* nodes. --- ## 4.4 Key-Value Pair Lifecycle — Visual Summary ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Input k1 v1
LongWritable, Text] --> M[map function
User Defined] M --> P2[Intermediate k2 v2
Text, IntWritable] P2 --> S[Shuffle and Sort
Framework] S --> P3[Grouped k2 list of v2
Text, Iterable IntWritable] P3 --> R[reduce function
User Defined] R --> P4[Output k3 v3
Text, IntWritable] ``` # 🎓 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Assessment Pattern Followed:** > - **Part A:** Short-answer questions (3 marks each) > - **Part B:** Long-answer with internal choice (14 marks = 2 × 7 marks sub-parts) > - Mark distribution aligned with **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** (Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze). --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]** > **Explain the role of the Mapper and Reducer functions in the MapReduce programming model with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Mapper** is a user-defined function that processes one input key-value pair at a time and emits zero or more intermediate key-value pairs. For Word Count, the Mapper receives $\langle \text{line\_offset}, \text{line\_text} \rangle$, tokenizes the line, and emits $\langle \text{word}, 1 \rangle$ for each word. The **Reducer** is a user-defined function that aggregates all intermediate values associated with the same key. For Word Count, the Reducer receives $\langle \text{word}, [1,1,1,\dots] \rangle$ and emits $\langle \text{word}, \text{sum} \rangle$. The **framework** handles splitting input data, partitioning intermediate keys, shuffling, sorting, and fault tolerance between these two user functions. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper Style]** > **What is a Combiner in MapReduce? Why is it useful for the Word Count application?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Combiner** is an optional, user-defined function that performs **local aggregation** on the output of a Mapper **before** the data is shuffled across the network to the Reducers. It acts as a "mini-reducer" running on the same node as the Mapper. For Word Count, if a Mapper emits 1000 copies of $\langle \text{“the”}, 1 \rangle$, the Combiner collapses these into a single $\langle \text{“the”}, 1000 \rangle$ locally. This dramatically reduces the **volume of data transferred over the network** during the shuffle phase, lowering both bandwidth consumption and job completion time. A Combiner must satisfy the **commutative** ($a+b=b+a$) and **associative** ($(a+b)+c = a+(b+c)$) properties — which addition satisfies — so it is safe for Word Count. **(3 marks)** --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question B-A (Choice A) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 3]** > **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **execution flow of a MapReduce job** in a Hadoop cluster. Describe the role of **NameNode, DataNode, ResourceManager, NodeManager, and ApplicationMaster**. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Implement the **Word Count** problem in Hadoop. Provide the **Mapper**, **Reducer**, and **Driver** classes, and explain the role of `setOutputKeyClass()`, `setOutputValueClass()`, `setMapperClass()`, and `setReducerClass()`. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — Cluster Architecture (7 marks) **1. Job Submission (1 mark)** The client invokes `Job.waitForCompletion()` from the Driver class. The job is submitted to the **ResourceManager (RM)** which allocates a **container** on a node to host the **ApplicationMaster (AM)**. **2. ApplicationMaster Initialization (1 mark)** The AM is responsible for the entire lifecycle of the job — task scheduling, monitoring, and rescheduling on failure. **3. Input Splits Computation (1 mark)** The AM computes input splits via `InputFormat.getSplits()`. For a 512 MB file with 128 MB blocks, $M = \lceil 512/128 \rceil = 4$ splits → 4 mapper tasks. **4. Mapper Task Scheduling (2 marks)** The AM requests containers from the RM. **YARN scheduler** (default: CapacityScheduler) places mapper tasks on **nodes hosting the input blocks** (data locality). Each Mapper reads its split via `RecordReader`, applies the user-defined `map()` method, and writes intermediate output to a **circular buffer in local disk** (not HDFS). **5. Shuffle, Sort & Reduce (1 mark)** After all mappers finish, intermediate output is **partitioned** (default `HashPartitioner`), **sorted**, and transferred to reducer nodes over HTTP. Each Reducer merges sorted input, runs `reduce()`, and writes final output to HDFS via `OutputFormat`. **6. Cleanup (1 mark)** AM deregisters from RM, releases containers, and job statistics are reported to the client. **Roles Summary Table:** | Component | Role | |---|---| | **NameNode** | HDFS metadata (namespace tree, block → DataNode mapping) | | **DataNode** | Stores actual HDFS blocks; serves read/write requests | | **ResourceManager** | Global scheduler; allocates containers cluster-wide | | **NodeManager** | Per-node agent; launches/monitors containers | | **ApplicationMaster** | Per-job master; negotiates resources, tracks task progress | --- #### Model Solution for (b) — Word Count Code (7 marks) **Mapper Class (2.5 marks):** ```java public class WordCountMapper extends Mapper { private final static IntWritable one = new IntWritable(1); private Text word = new Text(); public void map(LongWritable key, Text value, Context context) throws IOException, InterruptedException { StringTokenizer itr = new StringTokenizer(value.toString()); while (itr.hasMoreTokens()) { word.set(itr.nextToken()); context.write(word, one); } } } ``` **Explanation:** `LongWritable` is the byte offset (input key), `Text` is the line (input value). The mapper tokenizes each line and emits a `(word, 1)` pair. The static `one` object is reused to avoid object-creation overhead. **Reducer Class (2.5 marks):** ```java public class WordCountReducer extends Reducer { public void reduce(Text key, Iterable values, Context context) throws IOException, InterruptedException { int sum = 0; for (IntWritable val : values) { sum += val.get(); } context.write(key, new IntWritable(sum)); } } ``` **Explanation:** The reducer iterates over all `1`s associated with each word and emits the total count. **Driver Class & Method Roles (2 marks):** ```java job.setOutputKeyClass(Text.class); // Sets k3 type job.setOutputValueClass(IntWritable.class); // Sets v3 type job.setMapperClass(WordCountMapper.class); // Registers Mapper job.setReducerClass(WordCountReducer.class); // Registers Reducer ``` - **`setOutputKeyClass(Text.class)`** — declares the type of the *final output key* emitted by the Reducer (`Text` = UTF-8 string). - **`setOutputValueClass(IntWritable.class)`** — declares the type of the *final output value* (a 32-bit integer). - **`setMapperClass(WordCountMapper.class)`** — tells the framework which class contains the `map()` method to invoke on each mapper task. - **`setReducerClass(WordCountReducer.class)`** — registers the Reducer class; the framework calls `reduce()` once per unique key after shuffle/sort. --- ### Question B-B (Choice B — Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 3]** > **(a)** Explain the **four phases of MapReduce data flow** with reference to Word Count. Show the intermediate key-value pairs produced for the input: > > `"deer bear river"` \newline > `"bear car bear"` \newline > `"car deer river car"` \newline > **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** What is a **Partitioner**? Explain the default `HashPartitioner`. Why might you write a **custom Partitioner** for Word Count, and how? Provide a code snippet. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — Four Phases (7 marks) **Phase 1 — Input Splitting (1 mark)** Each of the 3 lines is treated as one record with a byte-offset key: - $\langle 0, \text{“deer bear river”} \rangle$ - $\langle 16, \text{“bear car bear”} \rangle$ - $\langle 32, \text{“car deer river car”} \rangle$ **Phase 2 — Map (2 marks)** Each Mapper tokenizes and emits one `(word, 1)` per token: - Line 1 → `(deer,1) (bear,1) (river,1)` - Line 2 → `(bear,1) (car,1) (bear,1)` - Line 3 → `(car,1) (deer,1) (river,1) (car,1)` **Phase 3 — Shuffle & Sort (2 marks)** Grouping identical keys (assume 1 Reducer for simplicity): - `bear → [1, 1]` - `car → [1, 1]` - `deer → [1, 1]` - `river → [1, 1]` **Phase 4 — Reduce (2 marks)** Final output: - `(bear, 2)` - `(car, 3)` - `(deer, 2)` - `(river, 2)` --- #### Model Solution for (b) — Partitioners (7 marks) A **Partitioner** determines which Reducer receives a given intermediate key. The framework calls `getPartition(key, value, numReduceTasks)` to compute a partition index in $[0, R-1]$. **Default HashPartitioner (3 marks):** $$ \text{partition}(k) = \left( k.\text{hashCode}() \;\bmod\; R \right) $$ This works well when keys are uniformly distributed, but can lead to **data skew** if a few keys dominate (e.g., the word "the" in Word Count). **Custom Partitioner — Skew Handling (4 marks):** ```java public class CustomPartitioner extends Partitioner { @Override public int getPartition(Text key, IntWritable value, int numReduceTasks) { // Send very common words (e.g., "the", "a") to separate reducers String word = key.toString(); if (word.equals("the") || word.equals("a")) { return 0; // Dedicated reducer for stop words } return (word.hashCode() & Integer.MAX_VALUE) % numReduceTasks; } } ``` Register in Driver: ```java job.setPartitionerClass(CustomPartitioner.class); ``` **Why customize?** To **balance reducer load** when data is skewed, ensuring no single reducer becomes a straggler. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps (from KTU Valuation Key Analysis):** > > 1. **Confusing intermediate and final key types** — Students write `(k3, v3) = (IntWritable, Text)` instead of `(Text, IntWritable)`. The Reducer emits the **final output**, which is the *word* (key) and *count* (value). **Always:** `(Text, IntWritable)`. > > 2. **Forgetting `setCombinerClass()`** — Using the Reducer class as a Combiner in Word Count is best practice and a frequent mark-grabber. Set `job.setCombinerClass(WordCountReducer.class)`. > > 3. **Mixing up `Mapper` types with `Reducer` types** — The Mapper's *output* type must match the Reducer's *input* type. If Mapper outputs `(Text, IntWritable)`, Reducer must input `(Text, IntWritable)`. This is a 2-mark deduction if violated. > > 4. **Confusing `InputFormat` with `OutputFormat`** — `FileInputFormat.addInputPath()` reads HDFS; `FileOutputFormat.setOutputPath()` writes HDFS. The output directory must **not exist** before job submission. > > 5. **Writing `context.write(key, 1)`** instead of `context.write(key, new IntWritable(1))` — Hadoop requires `Writable` types, not raw Java primitives. > > 6. **Skipping the Driver class explanation** — A code-only answer without explaining each `set*Class()` method loses 2–3 marks. > > 7. **Not stating the Combiner is optional** — Marks are reserved for noting that the Combiner is framework-optional, only safe for associative operations. --- ## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize This Bullet List:** - ☐ **MapReduce = Map + Reduce + Framework-managed Shuffle/Sort + Driver/Job Submission** - ☐ Word Count problem: input → `(word, 1)` pairs → sum per word - ☐ **Mapper types:** `Mapper` = `Mapper` - ☐ **Reducer types:** `Reducer` = `Reducer` - ☐ **Driver** uses `Configuration`, `Job`, `FileInputFormat`, `FileOutputFormat` - ☐ **Combiner** = optional local reducer; reduces shuffle volume; must be commutative + associative - ☐ **Partitioner** = assigns keys to reducers; default `HashPartitioner` uses `hashCode() mod R` - ☐ Default HDFS **block size = 128 MB**; default **replication = 3** - ☐ **Data locality** — YARN schedules mappers on nodes holding the input blocks - ☐ **Writable types** — `LongWritable, IntWritable, Text, FloatWritable, NullWritable` - ☐ **Speculative execution** — YARN backs up straggler tasks to reduce job latency - ☐ **Shuffle & Sort** is the most network-intensive phase; combers minimize its cost - ☐ **Reduce output files** in HDFS are named `part-r-00000`, `part-r-00001`, … - ☐ **Failure recovery** — ApplicationMaster reschedules failed tasks up to 4 times (default) - ☐ **Word Count equivalence in SQL:** `SELECT word, COUNT(*) FROM tokens GROUP BY word` - ☐ YARN = **Yet Another Resource Negotiator** (Hadoop 2.x+); replaced the old JobTracker/TaskTracker

topics.title: Identify Possible Attacks of Any Selected Cloud Applications and Suggest/Implement Solutions/Policies for Mitigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST635 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Identify Possible Attacks of Any Selected Cloud Applications and Suggest/Implement Solutions/Policies for Mitigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Application Attack Surface** refers to the complete sum of vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, weak entry points, and exploitable interfaces across the network, host, application, and data layers of a cloud-deployed application that an adversary can leverage to compromise the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of cloud resources.

According to the **Cloud Security Alliance (CSA)** and **NIST SP 500-292**, cloud application attacks are deliberate attempts to disrupt, disable, manipulate, steal, or destroy data, applications, or infrastructure hosted on a public, private, hybrid, or community cloud.

### 1.2 Selected Cloud Application — Case Study Anchor

For this module, the selected cloud application is a **Cloud-Hosted SaaS-based Customer Relationship Management (CRM) Web Application** (representative of platforms such as Salesforce, Microsoft Dynamics 365, or AWS-deployed custom CRMs). This selection is deliberate because SaaS CRM applications simultaneously expose **APIs, multi-tenant storage, identity services, and front-end web interfaces** — providing a rich attack surface for pedagogical analysis.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a high-tech bank vault (the cloud) shared by many tenants. Each tenant rents a locker (their data) and uses a key-card (credentials) to access it. Now imagine:
> - A **duplicate key** is forged (Credential Theft)
> - A **vent** in the wall is too small but lets sound through (Side-Channel Attack)
> - A **crowd jams the entrance** so legit customers can't enter (DDoS)
> - A **bank employee** sells customer records (Insider Threat)
> - A **fake ATM** is placed outside (Phishing / Wrapping Attack)
>
> Each of these is a *cloud attack*, and the bank must install *cameras, alarms, identity checks, encryption, audits, and policies* — exactly analogous to cloud security controls.

### 1.4 The CIA Triad as the Governing Principle

Every cloud attack ultimately violates one or more pillars of the CIA Triad:

$$ \text{Security}_{\text{Cloud}} = f(\text{Confidentiality}, \text{Integrity}, \text{Availability}) $$

| Pillar | Definition | Example Cloud Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Restricting data access to authorized entities only | Data Breach via S3 Misconfiguration |
| **Integrity** | Ensuring data is not tampered with during storage/transit | Man-in-the-Middle Attack, SQL Injection |
| **Availability** | Ensuring services remain accessible on demand | DDoS Attack, Ransomware |

### 1.5 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 4 of PECST635 explicitly tests the student's ability to **map real-world threats to cloud deployment models (SaaS/PaaS/IaaS)** and propose **defense-in-depth solutions**. KTU board examiners routinely allocate **14-mark Part B questions** to this exact competency.

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Attack Taxonomy for Cloud Applications

Cloud application attacks can be classified into **four logical layers** corresponding to the cloud stack:

1. **Network Layer Attacks** — DDoS, ARP Spoofing, Sniffing
2. **Host / Virtualization Layer Attacks** — VM Escape, Hypervisor Exploits
3. **Application Layer Attacks** — SQL Injection, XSS, CSRF, Insecure APIs
4. **Data Layer Attacks** — Data Breach, Data Loss, Cryptojacking

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "**N-H-A-D**" — *Network, Host, Application, Data* — the four layers from outermost perimeter to innermost asset.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Attack Vector | Violated CIA Pillar | Mitigation Control | Standard / Framework |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Data Breach | Confidentiality | Encryption (AES-256), Tokenization, DLP | NIST SP 800-53, ISO 27001 |
| 2 | Account / Service Hijacking | Confidentiality + Integrity | MFA, RBAC, Session Timeout | OWASP A07:2021 |
| 3 | Insecure APIs | All three | OAuth 2.0, API Gateway, Rate Limiting, WAF | OWASP API Top 10 |
| 4 | DoS / DDoS | Availability | Auto-Scaling, CDN, Cloudflare, Blackholing | NIST SP 800-61 |
| 5 | Shared Technology Misuse | All three | Multi-tenancy isolation, VPC, micro-segmentation | CSA CCM v4 |
| 6 | Insider Threat (Malicious) | Confidentiality + Integrity | Least Privilege, Audit Logs, UEBA | NIST SP 800-53 AC-2 |
| 7 | Side-Channel Attack | Confidentiality | VM placement isolation, cache flushing | CSA EKM |
| 8 | Malware Injection (Wrapping/SaaS) | All three | Input validation, CSP, sandboxing | OWASP Top 10 |
| 9 | Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) | Confidentiality + Integrity | TLS 1.3, Certificate Pinning, HSTS | RFC 8446 |
| 10 | Cloud Misconfiguration | All three | CSPM tools (AWS Config, Azure Defender) | CIS Benchmarks |
| 11 | Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) | All three | Zero Trust Architecture, SIEM, SOC | MITRE ATT&CK |
| 12 | Cryptojacking | Availability + Resources | EDR, CPU monitoring, browser hardening | NIST IR-8290 |

### 2.3 The Defense-in-Depth Equation

The strength of a cloud application's security posture can be expressed as:

$$ S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot C_i $$

Where:
- $S_{total}$ = Total Security Posture Score (out of 100)
- $C_i$ = Effectiveness of the $i$-th control layer (0–100)
- $w_i$ = Weighted importance of that layer ($\sum w_i = 1$)
- $n$ = Number of independent defense layers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice the **sum** (not product) relationship. This means **even if one control fails, the others still contribute positively**. This is the mathematical justification for "Defense-in-Depth" — a single failure does NOT collapse the entire security posture.

### 2.4 Risk Severity Scoring (CVSS-aligned)

Cloud application risks are commonly quantified using a **DREAD** or **CVSS v3.1** model:

$$ R = \frac{(V \times E \times C \times I \times A)}{5} $$

Where:
- $R$ = Risk Score (0–10)
- $V$ = Vulnerability severity
- $E$ = Exposure breadth
- $C$ = Confidentiality impact
- $I$ = Integrity impact
- $A$ = Availability impact

A score $\geq 7.0$ triggers **immediate mitigation policy activation**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AWS, Azure, GCP** all publish shared-responsibility threat catalogs directly tied to this analysis.
- The **OWASP Cloud Top 10** is used by SOC teams globally.
- **ISO/IEC 27017** and **27018** are codified compliance controls derived from this exact taxonomy.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Case Study

### 3.1 Selected Application — Architecture Profile

> [!NOTE]
> **Application:** *CloudCRM Pro* — a multi-tenant SaaS CRM hosted on AWS, serving 50,000 enterprise users, integrated with a public REST API, mobile clients, and a third-party analytics service.

**Architecture Stack:**

| Layer | Technology |
|---|---|
| Frontend | ReactJS + CDN (CloudFront) |
| API Layer | REST API + API Gateway |
| Auth | OAuth 2.0 + JWT |
| Compute | AWS EC2 Auto-Scaling Group |
| Storage | AWS S3 (customer documents) + RDS (customer records) |
| Identity | AWS Cognito (User Pool) |
| Logging | AWS CloudTrail + CloudWatch |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step — Top 5 Critical Attacks & Mitigations

#### ATTACK 1 — Insecure API leading to Data Breach (OWASP API1:2023)

**Step 1: Identify the Attack Surface**

The REST endpoint `GET /api/v1/customers/{id}` requires only a JWT but does **not enforce object-level authorization**.

**Step 2: Adversary Action**

An attacker authenticates as Tenant A's low-privilege user, then iterates `id` parameters to read Tenant B's customer records.

$$\text{Breach Size} = N_{users} \times N_{tenants} \times P_{success}$$

Where $P_{success}$ = probability of access without object-level check ≈ **1.0** (100% if no check exists).

**Step 3: Confidentiality Impact**

All customer PII (Personally Identifiable Information) is exposed → **complete Confidentiality violation**.

**Step 4: Mitigation Policy**

Implement **Object-Level Authorization** at API Gateway:

$$ \text{Decision} = f(\text{User}_{\text{tenant\_id}} \stackrel{?}{=} \text{Resource}_{\text{tenant\_id}}) $$

- **Control 1:** Validate `tenant_id` claim in JWT against the requested resource's owner.
- **Control 2:** Apply principle of **Least Privilege** — never trust client-supplied IDs.
- **Control 3:** Enable **AWS WAF + API Gateway Throttling** at 100 req/min.
- **Control 4:** Encrypt all PII at rest with **AWS KMS (AES-256-GCM)**.

#### ATTACK 2 — Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS)

**Step 1: Attack Pattern**

Botnet generates $\geq \mathbf{10\ Gbps}$ of L7 HTTP flood against the CloudFront distribution.

**Step 2: Availability Impact**

$$ \text{Availability}_{\text{degraded}} = 1 - \frac{\text{Survived RPS}}{\text{Required RPS}} $$

If Required = 50,000 RPS and Survived = 5,000 RPS → Availability drops to **10%**.

**Step 3: Mitigation Policy — Layered Defense**

- **Layer 1 (Edge):** Enable **AWS Shield Standard** (free) + **Shield Advanced** for volumetric attacks.
- **Layer 2 (CDN):** CloudFront absorbs HTTP floods via cached static assets.
- **Layer 3 (Compute):** Configure **Auto-Scaling Group** with cooldown scaling policy.
- **Layer 4 (Application):** Deploy **Rate Limiting** at API Gateway (Token Bucket algorithm):

$$ \text{Tokens}_{\text{remaining}} = \min\left(C, \text{Tokens}_{\text{prev}} + r \cdot \Delta t - n_{req}\right) $$

Where $C$ = bucket capacity, $r$ = refill rate, $n_{req}$ = consumed tokens.

#### ATTACK 3 — Account Hijacking via Credential Stuffing

**Step 1: Attack Vector**

Attacker uses leaked credentials from a third-party breach and replays them against `/login` (credential stuffing).

**Step 2: Mitigation Policies**

- **MFA Enforcement:** All administrative accounts MUST use TOTP or FIDO2.
- **Password Policy (NIST SP 800-63B):**
  - Minimum **12 characters**
  - No mandatory periodic rotation (unless breach suspected)
  - Check against **HIBP breach corpus**
- **Anomaly Detection:** AWS Cognito adaptive authentication flags logins from new geolocations.

$$ \text{Risk Score} = w_1 \cdot \Delta_{geo} + w_2 \cdot \Delta_{device} + w_3 \cdot \Delta_{velocity} $$

If Risk Score $\geq 0.7$, trigger **MFA challenge**.

#### ATTACK 4 — Cloud Misconfiguration (S3 Public Bucket)

**Step 1: Root Cause**

The bucket `cloudcrm-customer-docs` has ACL set to `public-read` due to a developer misconfiguration during CI/CD deployment.

**Step 2: Mitigation Policy Stack**

- **Preventive:** Use **AWS Config Rule** `s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited` — auto-remediates to `private`.
- **Detective:** Enable **AWS Macie** to scan and classify sensitive data.
- **Responsive:** CloudWatch Alarm → SNS → SOC team in <5 minutes.
- **Policy-as-Code:** Terraform/CloudFormation templates enforce `BlockPublicAcls = true`.

#### ATTACK 5 — Insider Threat (Privileged User)

**Step 1: Threat Profile**

A malicious DevOps engineer with `AdministratorAccess` IAM role exfiltrates RDS snapshots.

**Step 2: Mitigation Controls**

- **Just-In-Time (JIT) Privileges:** Use AWS IAM Identity Center with temporary elevation (max 1 hour).
- **Separation of Duties:** No single user can create + delete critical resources.
- **Audit Trail:** CloudTrail logs every API call to an immutable S3 bucket with **Object Lock** (WORM compliance).
- **UEBA:** AWS GuardDuty detects anomalous `GetSnapshot` patterns.
- **Policy:** All administrative actions require **dual approval** via Service Control Policies (SCP).

### 3.3 Risk Matrix Summary (Analytical Output)

| Attack | Likelihood | Impact | Inherent Risk | Post-Mitigation Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Insecure API / BOLA | High | Critical | **Critical** | Low |
| DDoS | High | High | **High** | Low |
| Account Hijacking | Medium | Critical | **High** | Low |
| S3 Misconfiguration | Medium | Critical | **High** | Very Low |
| Insider Threat | Low | Critical | **Medium** | Low |

### 3.4 Policy Document Skeleton (Deliverable for KTU)

A complete cloud security policy MUST contain:

1. **Scope Statement** — defines assets, users, environments
2. **Roles & Responsibilities** — RACI matrix
3. **Acceptable Use Policy** — user conduct
4. **Data Classification Standard** — Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted
5. **Access Control Policy** — RBAC, ABAC, MFA mandates
6. **Incident Response Plan** — NIST 800-61 lifecycle (Preparation → Detection → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Lessons Learned)
7. **Encryption Standards** — TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest
8. **Audit & Compliance** — quarterly review, ISO 27001 alignment
9. **Business Continuity** — RPO $\leq$ 1 hour, RTO $\leq$ 4 hours

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cloud Application Attack Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Reconnaissance - OSINT, Shodan, GitHub Dorking] --> A2[Weaponization - Crafting Payload or Credential List]
    A2 --> A3[Delivery - Phishing Email, Public API Endpoint, Misconfigured Bucket]
    A3 --> A4[Exploitation - Exploiting BOLA, SQLi, Credential Stuffing]
    A4 --> A5[Installation - Web Shell, Backdoor Account, C2 Beacon]
    A5 --> A6[Command and Control - C2 Channel via HTTPS or DNS Tunnel]
    A6 --> A7[Actions on Objectives - Data Exfiltration, Ransom, Service Disruption]
    A7 --> A8[Persistence - New IAM Keys, Lambda Backdoor, Modified AMI]
    A8 --> A1

    B1[Defense Layer 1 - Perimeter WAF and CDN] -.blocks.-> A3
    B2[Defense Layer 2 - IAM and MFA] -.blocks.-> A4
    B3[Defense Layer 3 - Encryption and DLP] -.blocks.-> A7
    B4[Defense Layer 4 - SIEM and SOC Monitoring] -.detects.-> A6
```

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

*What is "Defense-in-Depth"? Justify mathematically why a single control failure does not collapse the entire cloud security posture.*

**Model Answer:**

Defense-in-Depth is the strategy of deploying **multiple independent security controls** across network, host, application, and data layers.

$$ S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot C_i $$

If control $C_j$ fails (i.e., $C_j \rightarrow 0$), the remaining terms still contribute:

$$ S_{total} = w_j \cdot 0 + \sum_{i \neq j} w_i \cdot C_i = \sum_{i \neq j} w_i \cdot C_i > 0 $$

Hence the posture is never zero. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark derivation = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** *For a selected cloud-hosted SaaS CRM application, identify and explain **five (5) critical attack vectors** with reference to the architecture stack (API, identity, storage, network).* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Suggest a comprehensive **mitigation policy framework** (technical + administrative + physical) for each identified attack, mapped to ISO 27001 / NIST controls.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Five Critical Attack Vectors (7 marks — 1.4 marks each):**

1. **Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA)** — REST API lacks object ownership validation → cross-tenant data leakage.
2. **Credential Stuffing on /login** — Replay of leaked passwords from external breaches.
3. **DDoS via HTTP Flood** — L7 volumetric attack on CloudFront.
4. **S3 Bucket Misconfiguration** — Public ACL on `cloudcrm-customer-docs`.
5. **Insider Threat** — Privileged DevOps engineer exfiltrates RDS snapshot.

*(For each: 0.7 mark identification + 0.7 mark explanation with architecture reference.)*

**(b) Mitigation Policy Framework (7 marks — 1.4 marks each):**

| Attack | Technical Control | Administrative Control | NIST/ISO Mapping |
|---|---|---|---|
| BOLA | Object-level check at API Gateway | Secure SDLC code review policy | ISO 27001 A.14.2.5 |
| Credential Stuffing | MFA + rate limiting | NIST SP 800-63B password policy | NIST IA-2 |
| DDoS | Shield Advanced + Auto-Scaling | BCP/DR plan | ISO 27001 A.17.2 |
| S3 Misconfig | AWS Config auto-remediate | IaC policy-as-code mandate | CIS AWS 2.1.5 |
| Insider | JIT IAM + CloudTrail | Separation of duties + dual approval | NIST AC-5 |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** *Explain the **OWASP Cloud Top 10** threats. Discuss in detail **Data Breach, Account Hijacking, and Insecure APIs** with one real-world example each.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Design a **Zero-Trust Architecture** policy framework for the cloud application. Include the principles, technical implementation steps, and policy controls.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) OWASP Cloud Top 10 — Detailed Discussion (7 marks):**

- **Data Breach (2.3 marks):** Definition + Real-world example — *Capital One breach (2019)* where a misconfigured WAF allowed an SSRF attack exposing 100M customer records. Mitigation: Encryption + tokenization + DLP + CSPM tools.

- **Account Hijacking (2.3 marks):** Definition + Real-world example — *Dropbox credential stuffing (2012)* where 68M passwords were reused from LinkedIn breach. Mitigation: MFA + adaptive authentication + session timeout.

- **Insecure APIs (2.4 marks):** Definition + Real-world example — *Facebook Graph API (2018)* exposing 50M user tokens via "View As" feature. Mitigation: OAuth 2.0 + API Gateway throttling + strict input validation + WAF.

**(b) Zero-Trust Architecture (ZTA) Framework (7 marks):**

**Three Core Principles (3 marks):**

1. **Never Trust, Always Verify** — every request authenticated, authorized, encrypted.
2. **Least Privilege Access** — JIT, time-bound, just-enough permissions.
3. **Assume Breach** — micro-segmentation + continuous monitoring.

**Technical Implementation Steps (2 marks):**

1. Implement AWS IAM Identity Center with MFA enforcement.
2. Deploy micro-segmentation via VPC + security groups.
3. Enable CloudTrail + GuardDuty for continuous verification.
4. Apply mTLS for service-to-service communication.
5. Use AWS Verified Access for context-aware authorization.

**Policy Controls (2 marks):**

- Device posture assessment before granting access.
- Continuous risk scoring per session.
- Automated revocation on anomaly detection.
- Quarterly ZTA policy audit.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write generic answers like "use firewalls and encryption" — name the **specific technology** (e.g., AWS WAF, AWS KMS, Shield Advanced).
> 2. **Do NOT** skip mapping to a recognized framework (NIST/ISO/CSA) — examiners award marks specifically for compliance alignment.
> 3. **Do NOT** confuse SaaS attacks with IaaS attacks — specify the **service model** explicitly.
> 4. Always draw or describe the **architecture diagram** — visual answers consistently earn 1–2 extra marks.
> 5. In risk questions, **quantify** using likelihood × impact — qualitative-only answers lose 1 mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

- Cloud application attacks are categorized into **4 layers**: Network, Host, Application, Data (NHAD mnemonic).
- All attacks map to violations of the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).
- The **OWASP Cloud Top 10** + **OWASP API Top 10** are the canonical references — memorize them.
- **Defense-in-Depth** is mathematically additive — failure of one control ≠ total compromise.
- **Zero Trust Architecture** = Never Trust + Always Verify + Least Privilege + Assume Breach.
- Mitigation must span **Technical + Administrative + Physical** controls — never one-dimensional.
- Always map controls to **NIST SP 800-53 / ISO 27001 / CSA CCM** — this is a guaranteed scoring opportunity.
- **Encryption:** AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, KMS-managed keys.
- **Identity:** MFA mandatory, OAuth 2.0 / SAML for federation, JIT for privileged access.
- **Monitoring:** CloudTrail + GuardDuty + SIEM + centralized logging.
- **Risk scoring:** Use CVSS or DREAD; score $\geq 7.0$ triggers immediate mitigation.
- **Incident Response:** Follow NIST 800-61 — 6 phases (Preparation → Detection → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Lessons Learned).
- **Configuration Hygiene:** CSPM tools (AWS Config, Azure Defender) to prevent misconfig-driven breaches.
- **Remember the formula:** $S_{total} = \sum w_i \cdot C_i$ — derived and usable in any theoretical sub-question.
